<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define threshold voltage in a digital logic family. List the four standard threshold voltage parameters used to characterize a logic gate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Threshold voltage refers to the input voltage levels of a logic gate that demarcate the boundaries of valid logic 0 and logic 1 recognition. It is the voltage at which the output of a gate undergoes transition from one stable state to the other.

**Four parameters (1.5 marks):**
1. $V_{IL}$ — maximum input voltage recognized as logic 0.
2. $V_{IH}$ — minimum input voltage recognized as logic 1.
3. $V_{OL}$ — maximum output voltage when the output is logic 0.
4. $V_{OH}$ — minimum output voltage when the output is logic 1.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define noise margin. Derive the expressions for high-state and low-state noise margins. Why is a high noise margin desirable in a digital system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Noise margin is the maximum amount of unwanted voltage (noise) that can be superimposed on a logic level at the input of a receiving gate without causing it to misinterpret the logic state.

**Expressions (1 mark):**

$$
N_{ML} = V_{IL,max} - V_{OL,max} \qquad N_{MH} = V_{OH,min} - V_{IH,min}
$$

**Importance (1 mark):** A large noise margin ensures reliable operation of the digital system in the presence of noise from supply fluctuations, crosstalk, ground bounce, and electromagnetic interference. It widens the *forbidden region* $V_{IH} - V_{IL}$ where the gate behaviour is undefined, providing immunity against false triggering.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two sub-parts, typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice between full Question A and full Question B.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

#### [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Draw the voltage transfer characteristic (VTC) of a CMOS inverter and clearly mark $V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$, $V_{OL}$, $V_{OH}$ and the switching threshold $V_{th}$. Explain the three distinct regions of operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CMOS inverter operating at $V_{DD} = 3.3\ \text{V}$ with $V_{tn} = 0.6\ \text{V}$, $\vert V_{tp}\vert = 0.7\ \text{V}$, and $k_p/k_n = 1.5$, calculate the switching threshold $V_{th}$. Given $V_{IL,max} = 0.9\ \text{V}$, $V_{IH,min} = 2.31\ \text{V}$, $V_{OL,max} = 0.2\ \text{V}$, $V_{OH,min} = 3.1\ \text{V}$, find the noise margins $N_{ML}$ and $N_{MH}$. Comment on the noise immunity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — VTC diagram and three regions (7 marks):**

The CMOS inverter VTC is an S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve that inverts the input.

**Region 1 — Cut-off HIGH region (2 marks):**
For $0 \le V_{in} < V_{IL}$:
- $V_{GS,n} = V_{in} < V_{tn}$ → NMOS is OFF.
- $V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_{in}$ is large → PMOS is ON (in triode).
- Output is pulled up to $V_{OH} \approx V_{DD}$.
- Slope $\frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} \approx 0$.

**Region 2 — Transition / Active region (3 marks):**
For $V_{IL} \le V_{in} \le V_{IH}$:
- Both NMOS and PMOS are ON. NMOS moves from cutoff into saturation then into triode; PMOS does the opposite.
- Near $V_{in} = V_{th}$, both devices are in **saturation** simultaneously — short-circuit current flows.
- The slope $\vert \frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}}\vert > 1$ — a small change in $V_{in}$ produces a large change in $V_{out}$.
- $V_{th}$ is the point where the VTC curve crosses the line $V_{out} = V_{in}$.

**Region 3 — Cut-off LOW region (2 marks):**
For $V_{in} > V_{IH}$:
- $V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_{in} < \vert V_{tp}\vert$ → PMOS is OFF.
- $V_{GS,n} = V_{in} > V_{tn}$ → NMOS is ON (in triode).
- Output is pulled down to $V_{OL} \approx 0$.
- Slope $\frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} \approx 0$.

> The boundary points $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ are defined by the **unity-slope (–1) rule** on the VTC: $V_{IL}$ is the input voltage where $\frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ on the descending edge, and $V_{IH}$ is where the same condition is met on the upper edge.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Switching threshold:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{th} &= \frac{V_{tn} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}} \\
       &= \frac{0.6 + \sqrt{1.5}\,(3.3 - 0.7)}{1 + \sqrt{1.5}} \\
       &= \frac{0.6 + (1.2247)(2.6)}{1 + 1.2247} \\
       &= \frac{0.6 + 3.1843}{2.2247} \\
       &= \frac{3.7843}{2.2247} \\
       &\approx 1.701\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Low-state noise margin:**

$$
N_{ML} = V_{IL,max} - V_{OL,max} = 0.9 - 0.2 = 0.7\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — High-state noise margin:**

$$
N_{MH} = V_{OH,min} - V_{IH,min} = 3.1 - 2.31 = 0.79\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Comment on noise immunity:**

The switching threshold $V_{th} \approx 1.7\ \text{V}$ lies close to the ideal midpoint $V_{DD}/2 = 1.65\ \text{V}$, indicating a *near-symmetric* inverter. Both noise margins ($\sim 0.7$–$0.8\ \text{V}$) are reasonably balanced and acceptable for a 3.3 V logic system, providing robust immunity to ground-bounce and supply-rail noise. The HIGH margin slightly exceeds the LOW margin, meaning the gate is marginally more tolerant of noise on a HIGH signal than on a LOW signal — typical of PMOS-skewed designs.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Logic swing:**

$$
\Delta V = V_{OH,min} - V_{OL,max} = 3.1 - 0.2 = 2.9\ \text{V}
$$

This is the full output voltage swing available for noise immunity.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a CMOS inverter. Identify the operating regions of the NMOS and PMOS transistors at the three key points of the VTC: (i) $V_{in} = 0$, (ii) $V_{in} = V_{th}$, (iii) $V_{in} = V_{DD}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define noise margins. A logic family has the following specifications: $V_{OH,min} = 4.9\ \text{V}$, $V_{OL,max} = 0.1\ \text{V}$, $V_{IH,min} = 3.5\ \text{V}$, $V_{IL,max} = 1.5\ \text{V}$, $V_{DD} = 5\ \text{V}$. Calculate $N_{ML}$, $N_{MH}$, the forbidden-region width, and the switching threshold assuming symmetry. State whether this family has good noise immunity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — CMOS inverter operation and operating regions (7 marks):**

A CMOS inverter consists of a **PMOS pull-up** transistor (source tied to $V_{DD}$, gate to input) in series with an **NMOS pull-down** transistor (source tied to GND, gate to input). The drains are tied together to form the output.

**Operating point (i) — $V_{in} = 0$ (1.5 marks):**
- NMOS: $V_{GS} = 0 < V_{tn}$ → **OFF** (cutoff).
- PMOS: $V_{SG} = V_{DD}$ → strongly **ON** in triode region.
- Output: pulled up to $V_{OH} \approx V_{DD} = 5\ \text{V}$.
- Gate output: **Logic 1**.

**Operating point (ii) — $V_{in} = V_{th} = V_{DD}/2$ (3 marks):**
- NMOS: $V_{GS} = V_{DD}/2 > V_{tn}$, $V_{DS} = V_{DD}/2$. Since $V_{DS} > V_{GS} - V_{tn}$ → NMOS is in **saturation**.
- PMOS: $V_{SG} = V_{DD}/2$, $V_{SD} = V_{DD}/2$. Since $V_{SD} > V_{SG} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$ → PMOS is in **saturation**.
- Both in saturation → currents are equal and a short-circuit current of magnitude $\frac{1}{2}k_n(V_{DD}/2 - V_{tn})^2$ flows from $V_{DD}$ to GND. The output is at the switching threshold, $\approx V_{DD}/2$.

**Operating point (iii) — $V_{in} = V_{DD}$ (1.5 marks):**
- NMOS: $V_{GS} = V_{DD} > V_{tn}$, $V_{DS} \approx 0$ → **ON** in triode.
- PMOS: $V_{SG} = 0 < \vert V_{tp}\vert$ → **OFF** (cutoff).
- Output: pulled down to $V_{OL} \approx 0\ \text{V}$.
- Gate output: **Logic 0**.

**Summary table (1 mark):**

| $V_{in}$ | NMOS | PMOS | $V_{out}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 V | OFF | ON (triode) | $V_{DD}$ (Logic 1) |
| $V_{DD}/2$ | Saturation | Saturation | $V_{DD}/2$ (Switching) |
| $V_{DD}$ | ON (triode) | OFF | 0 V (Logic 0) |

---

**Part (b) — Noise margin calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Definitions:**

$$
N_{ML} = V_{IL,max} - V_{OL,max} \qquad N_{MH} = V_{OH,min} - V_{IH,min}
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Compute $N_{ML}$:**

$$
N_{ML} = 1.5 - 0.1 = 1.4\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Compute $N_{MH}$:**

$$
N_{MH} = 4.9 - 3.5 = 1.4\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Forbidden-region width and switching threshold:**

$$
\Delta V_{forb} = V_{IH,min} - V_{IL,max} = 3.5 - 1.5 = 2.0\ \text{V}
$$

$$
V_{th} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5\ \text{V} \quad \text{(symmetric assumption)}
$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks) — Comment:**

Both $N_{ML}$ and $N_{MH}$ are equal at **1.4 V**, which is excellent noise immunity. The forbidden region is wide (2.0 V out of a 5 V supply = 40%), placing the gate well above the 0.4 V industrial minimum. The threshold is exactly centered at $V_{DD}/2$, indicating a perfectly symmetric gate. This specification is consistent with **CMOS 4000-series** logic, which is renowned for superior noise immunity compared to TTL.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up input and output thresholds** — $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ are *input* specs; $V_{OL}$ and $V_{OH}$ are *output* specs. Subtracting two inputs gives the forbidden width, NOT a noise margin.
> 2. **Wrong polarity for ECL** — ECL uses negative supply rails. Don't blindly plug positive numbers into ECL formulae.
> 3. **Confusing logic swing with noise margin** — Logic swing $= V_{OH} - V_{OL}$, noise margins subtract the *input threshold* of the *next* gate.
> 4. **Forgetting the $k_p/k_n$ skew factor** — A "CMOS" gate is not automatically symmetric; many practical designs have $k_p \ne k_n$, shifting $V_{th}$ off-center.
> 5. **Skipping the "comment" sub-part** — In a 7-mark sub-question, a qualitative interpretation carries ~1 mark. Always close with a one-line engineering judgment.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⚡ **Threshold voltage** = input voltage boundary between valid logic 0 and valid logic 1 recognition.
- ⚡ **Four corner parameters:** $V_{IL}$ (max input LOW), $V_{IH}$ (min input HIGH), $V_{OL}$ (max output LOW), $V_{OH}$ (min output HIGH).
- ⚡ **Switching threshold $V_{th}$** = point on the VTC where $V_{in} = V_{out}$.
- ⚡ **Symmetric CMOS** ⇒ $V_{th} = V_{DD}/2$. **Skewed CMOS** ⇒ use the full formula with $k_p/k_n$ ratio.
- ⚡ **Noise margins:** $N_{ML} = V_{IL,max} - V_{OL,max}$, $N_{MH} = V_{OH,min} - V_{IH,min}$.
- ⚡ **Forbidden region** width = $V_{IH} - V_{IL}$ — wider is better for noise immunity.
- ⚡ **CMOS** (4000-series) has the **best noise margins** (≈1.4 V at 5 V supply); **TTL** is weaker (≈0.4–0.7 V).
- ⚡ **ECL** uses **negative** supply rails and has the **smallest logic swing** but the **fastest switching speed**.
- ⚡ **VTC slope rule:** $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ are the points where $\frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$.
- ⚡ **At $V_{in} = V_{th}$:** both NMOS and PMOS are in **saturation**; a short-circuit current flows; this is the worst-case static power point.
- ⚡ **At $V_{in} = 0$:** NMOS OFF, PMOS ON (triode) → output = $V_{DD}$.
- ⚡ **At $V_{in} = V_{DD}$:** NMOS ON (triode), PMOS OFF → output = 0.
- ⚡ **Interfacing rule:** A driver's $V_{OH,min}$ must exceed the receiver's $V_{IH,min}$, and the driver's $V_{OL,max}$ must be below the receiver's $V_{IL,max}$, else a level shifter is required.
- ⚡ **Schmitt trigger** = a gate with deliberately engineered hysteresis that **widens** the forbidden region for noisy inputs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propagation Delay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT304 - DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Propagation Delay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Propagation Delay** is formally defined as the time interval between the application of a specified input transition (typically the 50% point of the input waveform) and the corresponding output transition (typically the 50% point of the output waveform) of a logic gate or digital circuit.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework, propagation delay is a **dynamic performance parameter** that quantifies the *speed* of a digital logic family. It is denoted by symbols such as $t_p$, $t_d$, or $t_{pd}$, and is measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** for standard TTL/CMOS logic and **picoseconds (ps)** for high-speed ECL families.

Mathematically, two distinct propagation delays are defined for every logic gate:

- **$t_{PHL}$ (Propagation Delay - High to Low)**: The time taken for the output to transition from a HIGH logic level to a LOW logic level, measured between the **50% voltage points** of input and output waveforms.
- **$t_{PLH}$ (Propagation Delay - Low to High)**: The time taken for the output to transition from a LOW logic level to a HIGH logic level, measured between the **50% voltage points** of input and output waveforms.

The **Average Propagation Delay** ($t_{pd}$) is then computed as the arithmetic mean of these two unidirectional delays:

$$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Always specify *which* delay ($t_{PHL}$ or $t_{PLH}$) is being asked. In CMOS technology, $t_{PLH}$ and $t_{PHL}$ are generally unequal due to the asymmetric drive strength of PMOS and NMOS transistors — this asymmetry is a **frequently tested concept** in KTU university exams.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Conveyor Belt at a Toll Plaza"**

Imagine a highway toll booth. Cars (representing *electrical signals / bits*) approach the toll gate (representing the *logic gate*). 

- **No Delay (Ideal Gate):** Cars pass through instantly — impossible in reality.
- **Propagation Delay:** Every car takes a fixed amount of time to pay the toll and exit. This fixed time is the propagation delay.
- **$t_{PLH}$ vs $t_{PHL}$:** The toll booth's *cashier* processes "incoming cars" (LOW→HIGH) faster than *outgoing cars* (HIGH→LOW) because the change-making process is slower than receiving money. This asymmetry is exactly why $t_{PLH} \neq t_{PHL}$ in real logic gates.
- **Average Delay ($t_{pd}$):** If a traffic engineer wants a single number to describe the booth's average service time, they average both directions.

In digital terms: the **input** signal triggers the gate's internal transistors to switch, charge/discharge internal capacitances, and produce a valid **output**. The "service time" of this electronic toll booth is the propagation delay.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following **bold** constants and metrics form the cornerstone of propagation delay analysis:

- **$V_{IL}$ (Input LOW Voltage):** Maximum voltage recognized as logic 0.
- **$V_{IH}$ (Input HIGH Voltage):** Minimum voltage recognized as logic 1.
- **$V_{OL}$ (Output LOW Voltage):** Maximum guaranteed output voltage in LOW state.
- **$V_{OH}$ (Output HIGH Voltage):** Minimum guaranteed output voltage in HIGH state.
- **50% Point:** The reference voltage $V_{50\%} = \frac{V_{IL} + V_{IH}}{2}$ at which propagation delays are measured.
- **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time for output to transition from 10% to 90% of its final value.
- **Fall Time ($t_f$):** Time for output to transition from 90% to 10% of its initial value.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Why Propagation Delay Exists

Propagation delay is not an arbitrary design choice — it emerges from fundamental **RC charging/discharging** behavior of transistors and interconnects. The delay arises from three primary physical phenomena:

1. **Parasitic Capacitance Charging/Discharging:** Every MOSFET gate has an inherent **gate oxide capacitance** ($C_{ox}$), and every interconnection wire has **parasitic capacitance** to ground. These capacitances must be charged or discharged for the output to switch states.

2. **Transistor Switching Transients:** The MOS transistor does not switch instantaneously. It operates in three regions — **cutoff, saturation (active), and triode (linear)** — and traversing these regions takes finite time governed by **carrier mobility** ($\mu_n$ for electrons, $\mu_p$ for holes).

3. **Internal Node Capacitances:** Internal parasitic capacitances between the drain and source (Cds), gate and drain (Cgd), and gate and source (Cgs) collectively form the **load capacitance** ($C_L$) that the driving stage must service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** The propagation delay of a CMOS inverter is directly proportional to its **load capacitance** and inversely proportional to the **transconductance** of its driving transistors.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Delay Formation

When a digital signal enters a logic gate, the following sequence of events occurs:

- **Step 1 — Input Transition Detection:** The input voltage begins crossing from one logic level to another.
- **Step 2 — Transistor Turn-On/Turn-Off:** When input crosses the **threshold voltage** ($V_{th}$), the corresponding transistor begins conducting. The complementary transistor begins turning off.
- **Step 3 — Contention Period:** During the brief interval when **both** PMOS and NMOS are partially ON, a short-circuit current flows from $V_{DD}$ to GND. This is the **crowbar current** period.
- **Step 4 — Output Capacitance Charging/Discharging:** After the contention phase, one transistor becomes fully ON and sinks/sources current through the **load capacitance** $C_L$, causing the output to swing.
- **Step 5 — Output Settling:** The output reaches 50% of its final value — this is the moment $t_p$ is recorded.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $t_{pd} = \dfrac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2}$ | Average propagation delay | seconds (s) |
| $t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{ON,N} \cdot C_L$ | HIGH-to-LOW delay (NMOS pull-down) | seconds |
| $t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_{ON,P} \cdot C_L$ | LOW-to-HIGH delay (PMOS pull-up) | seconds |
| $R_{ON,N} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}_n (V_{GS} - V_{thn})}$ | NMOS ON-resistance | ohms ($\Omega$) |
| $R_{ON,P} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}_p (V_{GS} - \vert V_{thp} \vert)}$ | PMOS ON-resistance | ohms ($\Omega$) |
| $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_{pd}$ | Power-Delay Product (figure of merit) | Joules (J) |
| $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$ | Maximum operating frequency | Hertz (Hz) |
| $t_p = \tau \cdot \ln 2 = 0.69 \cdot RC$ | RC time constant relationship | seconds |
| $C_L = C_{wire} + C_{gate} + C_{diffusion}$ | Total load capacitance | Farads (F) |
| $t_{total} = N \cdot t_{pd}$ | Delay through N cascaded gates | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** The constant **0.69** in the delay formulas is **NOT a random number** — it equals $\ln(2)$. It emerges from the natural logarithmic response of an RC circuit during a 50% voltage transition. Memorize this relationship.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Propagation delay is the **single most important timing parameter** in the following real-world applications:

- **Microprocessor Design:** Determines the **clock frequency** at which a CPU can operate. A CPU with $t_{pd} = 50$ ps can theoretically run at 10 GHz.
- **High-Speed Communication (PCIe, USB 4.0, DDR Memory):** Sets the maximum data rate at which signals can be reliably sampled without **setup/hold violations** in flip-flops.
- **FPGA/ASIC Timing Closure:** Static Timing Analysis (STA) tools use propagation delays of every logic cell to verify that signals arrive at flip-flops within the clock period.
- **Automotive ECU Safety (ISO 26262):** Critical control loops have bounded maximum delays — propagation delay directly impacts **real-time response guarantees** in braking and steering systems.
- **5G/6G Baseband Processing:** Determines whether error correction can be performed within the symbol duration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why is the Propagation Delay Equal to $0.69 \cdot RC$?

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU university exams. Master this derivation completely.

**Problem Setup:** Consider a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$. At time $t = 0$, the input switches and the NMOS transistor begins to discharge $C_L$ from $V_{DD}$ to 0V through an effective ON-resistance $R_{ON}$.

**Step 1 — Write the governing differential equation.**

The output voltage $V_{out}(t)$ across the capacitor follows the standard RC discharge equation:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t / (R_{ON} \cdot C_L)}$$

**Step 2 — Define the 50% measurement point.**

Propagation delay $t_{PHL}$ is defined as the time when $V_{out}(t)$ falls to 50% of $V_{DD}$:

$$V_{out}(t_{PHL}) = 0.5 \cdot V_{DD}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve for $t_{PHL}$.**

$$0.5 \cdot V_{DD} = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t_{PHL} / (R_{ON} \cdot C_L)}$$

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by $V_{DD}$.**

$$0.5 = e^{-t_{PHL} / (R_{ON} \cdot C_L)}$$

**Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$\ln(0.5) = -\frac{t_{PHL}}{R_{ON} \cdot C_L}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the identity $\ln(0.5) = -\ln(2) = -0.693$.**

$$-0.693 = -\frac{t_{PHL}}{R_{ON} \cdot C_L}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $t_{PHL}$.**

$$t_{PHL} = 0.693 \cdot R_{ON} \cdot C_L \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{ON} \cdot C_L$$

**Step 8 — By analogous reasoning for the PMOS pull-up phase, the LOW-to-HIGH delay is:**

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_{ON,P} \cdot C_L$$

**Step 9 — The average propagation delay becomes:**

$$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{PLH} + t_{PHL}}{2} = \frac{0.69 \cdot C_L \cdot (R_{ON,P} + R_{ON,N})}{2}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Operating Frequency from Propagation Delay

**Step 1 — A single logic operation requires at least $t_{pd}$ seconds to complete.**

**Step 2 — A clock cycle must accommodate this delay, so the minimum clock period is:**

$$T_{clk,min} = 2 \cdot t_{pd}$$

The factor of 2 accounts for the fact that the signal must propagate through the logic AND the next flip-flop setup time.

**Step 3 — Maximum clock frequency is the inverse of the minimum period:**

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk,min}} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight:** This is why a CPU with lower $t_{pd}$ can run at higher frequencies — the relationship is **inversely proportional**, not linear.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style Problem)

**Problem:** A CMOS inverter has $R_{ON,N} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{ON,P} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, and drives a load capacitance $C_L = 10 \text{ pF}$. Calculate $t_{PHL}$, $t_{PLH}$, $t_{pd}$, and $f_{max}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $t_{PHL}$.**

$$t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{ON,N} \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot (1 \times 10^3) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-12})$$

$$t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot 10^{-8} = 6.9 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $t_{PLH}$.**

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_{ON,P} \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot (2 \times 10^3) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-12})$$

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-8} = 13.8 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Compute average $t_{pd}$.**

$$t_{pd} = \frac{6.9 + 13.8}{2} = \frac{20.7}{2} = 10.35 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $f_{max}$.**

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot t_{pd}} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 10.35 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{20.7 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f_{max} \approx 48.3 \text{ MHz}$$

### 3.4 Propagation Delay in Different Logic Families (Comparative Analytical Matrix)

| Logic Family | Typical $t_{pd}$ | Power Dissipation | Key Characteristics |
|--------------|------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **RTL (Resistor-Transistor Logic)** | 10–50 ns | High (mW range) | Obsolete, slow |
| **DTL (Diode-Transistor Logic)** | 20–100 ns | Moderate | Obsolete, fan-out limited |
| **TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic)** | 1–10 ns | 10 mW/gate | Industry standard for decades |
| **ECL (Emitter-Coupled Logic)** | 0.2–2 ns | 25–50 mW/gate | Fastest saturated logic |
| **CMOS (Complementary MOS)** | 1–50 ns (scaling-dependent) | 1–10 $\mu$W (static) | Low power, scalable |
| **BiCMOS** | 0.5–5 ns | Low | Combines BJT speed with CMOS density |
| **GaAs (Gallium Arsenide)** | 10–100 ps | High | Microwave/RF applications |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Propagation Delay Timing Diagram (Input-Output Waveform Relationship)

This diagram illustrates the precise measurement points for $t_{PLH}$ and $t_{PHL}$ on actual input/output waveforms, showing the 50% reference points and the time intervals.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph timing["PROPAGATION DELAY TIMING RELATIONSHIPS"]
        direction TB
        inputWave["INPUT WAVEFORM\nV_in transitions through 50 percent point at t1"]
        tPLHArrow["t_PLH = t3 minus t1\nmeasured between 50 percent points\ninput rising to output rising"]
        tPHLArrow["t_PHL = t4 minus t2\nmeasured between 50 percent points\ninput falling to output falling"]
        outputWave["OUTPUT WAVEFORM\nV_out transitions delayed\nrelative to input"]
        inputWave --> tPLHArrow
        tPLHArrow --> outputWave
        inputWave --> tPHLArrow
        tPHLArrow --> outputWave
    end
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Delay Formation in a CMOS Inverter

This block diagram maps the physical phenomena contributing to propagation delay inside a single CMOS inverter cell, which is the most testable architecture in KTU university exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph cmosinv["CMOS INVERTER DELAY GENERATION ARCHITECTURE"]
        direction TB
        inputNode["Vin INPUT NODE\n50 percent transition point"]
        pmosBlock["PMOS TRANSISTOR MP\npull up network\nresistance R_on_P"]
        nmosBlock["NMOS TRANSISTOR MN\npull down network\nresistance R_on_N"]
        clBlock["LOAD CAPACITANCE C_L\nincludes C_wire, C_gate\nC_diffusion"]
        outputNode["Vout OUTPUT NODE\n50 percent transition point\ndelayed by t_pd"]
        crowbar["SHORT CIRCUIT CURRENT\nduring simultaneous conduction\ninterval t_sc"]
        inputNode --> pmosBlock
        inputNode --> nmosBlock
        pmosBlock --> clBlock
        nmosBlock --> clBlock
        pmosBlock -.-> crowbar
        nmosBlock -.-> crowbar
        crowbar --> clBlock
        clBlock --> outputNode
    end
```

### 4.3 Cascaded Gate Delay Chain Flow

This diagram demonstrates how propagation delays accumulate when multiple logic gates are connected in series, a critical concept for timing analysis in KTU 2024 Scheme digital design problems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph cascade["CASCADED GATE DELAY ACCUMULATION CHAIN"]
        direction LR
        start["INPUT SIGNAL\nt = 0"]
        gateA["GATE 1\ndelay t_pd1\ntypically 10 ns"]
        gateB["GATE 2\ndelay t_pd2\ntypically 12 ns"]
        gateC["GATE 3\ndelay t_pd3\ntypically 8 ns"]
        endnode["FINAL OUTPUT\ntotal delay t_total\nsum of all t_pd values"]
        start --> gateA
        gateA -->|"signal arrives at t_pd1"| gateB
        gateB -->|"signal arrives at t_pd1 plus t_pd2"| gateC
        gateC -->|"signal arrives at t_pd1 plus t_pd2 plus t_pd3"| endnode
    end
```

### 4.4 Propagation Delay vs. Load Capacitance Trade-off Curve

The relationship between load capacitance and propagation delay is **linear** in CMOS, which is a frequently tested KTU concept.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph tradeoff["PROPAGATION DELAY VERSUS LOAD CAPACITANCE TRADEOFF"]
        direction TB
        lightLoad["LIGHT LOAD\nC_L less than 1 pF\nt_pd less than 1 ns\nfan out less than 2"]
        mediumLoad["MEDIUM LOAD\nC_L between 1 and 50 pF\nt_pd between 1 and 50 ns\nfan out between 2 and 10"]
        heavyLoad["HEAVY LOAD\nC_L greater than 50 pF\nt_pd greater than 50 ns\nfan out greater than 10\nbuffer insertion required"]
        lightLoad -->|"increase C_L"| mediumLoad
        mediumLoad -->|"increase C_L further"| heavyLoad
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define propagation delay $t_{PHL}$ and $t_{PLH}$ in a logic gate. How is the average propagation delay computed?**

**Model Answer:**

$t_{PHL}$ is defined as the time delay between the 50% transition point of the **input voltage** falling from HIGH to LOW and the corresponding 50% transition point of the **output voltage** falling from HIGH to LOW. $t_{PLH}$ is defined similarly for the LOW-to-HIGH output transition.

The **average propagation delay** is the arithmetic mean of the two:

$$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2}$$

This metric gives a single, direction-independent figure of merit for the switching speed of a logic gate and is used to compute maximum operating frequency.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Why are $t_{PLH}$ and $t_{PHL}$ generally unequal in a CMOS inverter?**

**Model Answer:**

In a CMOS inverter, the PMOS pull-up transistor and the NMOS pull-down transistor have **different carrier mobilities** ($\mu_p \approx 0.5 \mu_n$) and are typically sized differently. Since $t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_{ON,P} \cdot C_L$ depends on PMOS ON-resistance and $t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{ON,N} \cdot C_L$ depends on NMOS ON-resistance, the two delays are unequal.

To make them equal, designers typically **size the PMOS wider** (W/L ratio about 2× that of NMOS) to compensate for the lower hole mobility. This is a deliberate design decision in **symmetric CMOS inverter design**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse $t_{pd}$ (propagation delay) with $t_r$ (rise time) or $t_f$ (fall time). They are distinct parameters measured at different voltage points (50% vs 10%/90%).
> - **Do NOT** forget the constant **0.69 (= ln 2)** in RC delay derivations — its absence costs 1–2 marks.
> - **Always** specify units (ns, ps) in final numerical answers.
> - **Do NOT** use the term "propagation delay" loosely — KTU expects you to differentiate between $t_{PLH}$ and $t_{PHL}$ explicitly.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the propagation delay $t_{PHL}$ of a CMOS inverter in terms of the NMOS ON-resistance and load capacitance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CMOS logic circuit has the following parameters: $R_{ON,N} = 500 \ \Omega$, $R_{ON,P} = 1.5 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $C_L = 5 \text{ pF}$. Calculate (i) $t_{PHL}$, (ii) $t_{PLH}$, (iii) average $t_{pd}$, and (iv) maximum operating frequency $f_{max}$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — State the assumption.** When the input of a CMOS inverter switches from LOW to HIGH, the NMOS turns ON and the PMOS turns OFF. The output node (initially charged to $V_{DD}$) discharges through the NMOS to ground.

**Step 2 — Model the circuit as an RC discharge.** The NMOS behaves as a linear resistor $R_{ON,N}$ in series with the load capacitor $C_L$. The output voltage during discharge is:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t / (R_{ON,N} \cdot C_L)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 50% definition of $t_{PHL}$.**

$$V_{out}(t_{PHL}) = 0.5 \cdot V_{DD}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t_{PHL}$.**

$$0.5 \cdot V_{DD} = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t_{PHL} / (R_{ON,N} \cdot C_L)}$$

$$0.5 = e^{-t_{PHL} / (R_{ON,N} \cdot C_L)}$$

$$\ln(0.5) = -\frac{t_{PHL}}{R_{ON,N} \cdot C_L}$$

$$-0.693 = -\frac{t_{PHL}}{R_{ON,N} \cdot C_L}$$

$$\boxed{t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{ON,N} \cdot C_L}$$

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate $t_{PHL}$.**

$$t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{ON,N} \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot 500 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-9} = 1.725 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $t_{PLH}$.**

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_{ON,P} \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot 1500 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot 7.5 \times 10^{-9} = 5.175 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate average $t_{pd}$.**

$$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2} = \frac{1.725 + 5.175}{2} = \frac{6.9}{2} = 3.45 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $f_{max}$.**

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot t_{pd}} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 3.45 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{6.9 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\boxed{f_{max} \approx 144.93 \text{ MHz}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** Explain the factors affecting propagation delay in a CMOS logic gate. Discuss the role of load capacitance, transistor sizing, and supply voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A digital signal propagates through **4 cascaded CMOS inverters**, each having an average delay of 12 ns. Calculate (i) total propagation delay, (ii) maximum clock frequency, and (iii) discuss how inserting a **buffer** (with $t_{pd} = 5$ ns) between gates 2 and 3 would affect the total delay. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

The propagation delay of a CMOS logic gate is affected by the following factors:

**1. Load Capacitance ($C_L$):** Since $t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot R_{ON} \cdot C_L$, delay is **directly proportional** to load capacitance. Increasing fan-out (number of gates driven) increases $C_L$ and hence increases $t_{pd}$.

**2. Transistor Sizing (W/L ratio):** A larger W/L ratio reduces $R_{ON}$ (since $R_{ON} \propto 1/[(W/L)(V_{GS} - V_{th})]$), thereby reducing $t_{pd}$. However, larger transistors increase parasitic capacitance and silicon area.

**3. Supply Voltage ($V_{DD}$):** Higher $V_{DD}$ increases the overdrive voltage $(V_{GS} - V_{th})$, which reduces $R_{ON}$ and decreases $t_{pd}$. Modern low-power designs sacrifice speed for lower $V_{DD}$.

**4. Threshold Voltage ($V_{th}$):** Lower $V_{th}$ reduces $R_{ON}$ but increases subthreshold leakage current. Designers balance speed vs. leakage power.

**5. Temperature:** Higher temperature reduces carrier mobility, increasing $R_{ON}$ and $t_{pd}$. (Negative temperature coefficient for mobility.)

**6. Carrier Mobility ($\mu_n$, $\mu_p$):** Higher mobility reduces $R_{ON}$ and hence reduces $t_{pd}$.

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Total delay without buffer (4 cascaded gates).**

$$t_{total} = N \cdot t_{pd} = 4 \cdot 12 \text{ ns} = 48 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum clock frequency.**

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot t_{total}} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 48 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{96 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\boxed{f_{max} \approx 10.42 \text{ MHz}}$$

**Step 3 — Effect of inserting a buffer between gates 2 and 3.**

Without buffer: 4 stages × 12 ns = 48 ns.

With buffer inserted, the **fan-out load** seen by gate 2 is reduced because the buffer has a high input impedance and low input capacitance. This reduces the load on gate 2, thereby reducing its effective delay. However, the buffer itself adds 5 ns of delay.

In the worst-case analysis (no optimization):

$$t_{total,new} = t_{pd1} + t_{pd2} + t_{buffer} + t_{pd3} + t_{pd4}$$

If delays remain at 12 ns for gates 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 ns for the buffer:

$$t_{total,new} = 12 + 12 + 5 + 12 + 12 = 53 \text{ ns}$$

**However**, in practical scenarios, the buffer reduces the capacitive load on gate 2, potentially reducing $t_{pd2}$ from 12 ns to a smaller value (e.g., 6 ns). In that case:

$$t_{total,optimized} = 12 + 6 + 5 + 12 + 12 = 47 \text{ ns}$$

**Conclusion:** Buffer insertion is a **trade-off**. It improves delay only if the reduction in load-dependent delay of preceding stages exceeds the buffer's own delay contribution. In long cascaded chains, **tapered buffer design** is used to minimize total delay.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Propagation Delay**

- **Definition:** Propagation delay is the time between 50% input and 50% output voltage transitions.
- **Two Types:** $t_{PLH}$ (LOW→HIGH) and $t_{PHL}$ (HIGH→LOW) — they are generally **unequal** in CMOS.
- **Average:** $t_{pd} = (t_{PLH} + t_{PHL}) / 2$.
- **Golden Formula:** $t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot R_{ON} \cdot C_L$ — the constant 0.69 comes from $\ln(2)$.
- **NMOS vs. PMOS:** PMOS has lower hole mobility ($\mu_p \approx 0.5 \mu_n$), so $t_{PLH} > t_{PHL}$ unless PMOS is sized up.
- **Maximum Frequency:** $f_{max} = 1 / (2 \cdot t_{pd})$ — this is why faster gates enable faster CPUs.
- **Cascaded Delay:** $t_{total} = N \cdot t_{pd}$ for N identical cascaded gates.
- **Load Capacitance:** Includes $C_{wire}$ + $C_{gate}$ + $C_{diffusion}$ — the dominant delay contributor.
- **Power-Delay Product (PDP):** $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_{pd}$ is the **figure of merit** for a logic family — lower is better.
- **Buffer Insertion:** Used to reduce delay in long cascaded chains by reducing fan-out load on critical gates.
- **Logic Family Speed Hierarchy (fastest to slowest):** GaAs > ECL > BiCMOS > TTL > CMOS > DTL > RTL.
- **Static vs. Dynamic Power:** CMOS has near-zero static power but significant dynamic power at high frequencies ($P_{dyn} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$).
- **KTU Frequently Tested:** The derivation of $t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot RC$ from the RC discharge equation.
- **Common Mistake:** Confusing $t_{pd}$ with rise time $t_r$ or fall time $t_f$ — they use **different voltage reference points** (50% vs 10%/90%).
- **Modern Trend:** Industry reduces $t_{pd}$ by scaling down feature size (Moore's Law), reducing $C_L$ and $R_{ON}$ simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Dissipation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT304 - DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Dissipation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Power Dissipation** in a digital logic gate or integrated circuit (IC) is defined as the rate at which electrical energy is converted into heat (and sometimes electromagnetic radiation) when the device is operating. It is measured in **Watts (W)** or **milliwatts (mW)** and is mathematically the product of the supply voltage and the current drawn by the device.

For a digital IC, the average power dissipation is given by:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{V_{DD}}{T} \int_{0}^{T} i_{DD}(t) \, dt$$

where $V_{DD}$ is the supply voltage, $i_{DD}(t)$ is the time-varying current drawn from the supply, and $T$ is the period of observation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Power dissipation is classified into two primary domains — **Static Power Dissipation** (power consumed when the output is not switching) and **Dynamic Power Dissipation** (power consumed during switching transitions). Both must be analyzed separately for CMOS, TTL, and ECL logic families.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tap (faucet) connected to a pressurized pipeline**:

- The **supply voltage ($V_{DD}$)** is analogous to the **water pressure** in the pipe.
- The **current ($I$)** is analogous to the **flow rate of water** (how fast water is gushing out).
- The **power dissipation** is the total energy "wasted" as heat — just as water gushing out of a leaking tap does no useful work, current flowing through transistors mostly dissipates as heat.

When the tap is fully open (switching at high frequency), more water flows — meaning **more power is dissipated**. When the tap is closed (static state), there's a small "drip" — representing **leakage current** in modern CMOS circuits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Significance:** Power dissipation directly impacts **battery life** in portable devices (smartphones, laptops), **thermal management** (heat sinks, fans), and **reliability** (hot chips degrade faster). Modern high-performance processors consume over **100 W**, while low-power IoT chips target **micro-watt** levels.

### 1.3 Classification of Power Dissipation

Power dissipation in digital ICs is broadly classified into four categories:

| Category | When It Occurs | Dominant In |
|----------|----------------|-------------|
| **Static Power** | Output is stable (not switching) | CMOS, BiCMOS |
| **Dynamic Power** | During switching (charging/discharging load capacitance) | All families |
| **Short-Circuit Power** | When both pull-up and pull-down networks conduct simultaneously | CMOS, TTL |
| **Leakage Power** | Due to sub-threshold and gate-oxide leakage currents | Deep submicron CMOS |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** In older technologies (above 250 nm), **dynamic power** dominated. In modern deep-submicron CMOS (below 90 nm), **static/leakage power** has become equally important or even dominant due to shrinking transistor dimensions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Static Power Dissipation

Static power dissipation occurs when the logic gate is in a steady state — output is either stable HIGH or stable LOW, and no switching is taking place.

#### 2.1.1 Static Power in CMOS

In an ideal CMOS inverter, the static current is theoretically **zero** because either the PMOS or NMOS transistor is in cutoff. However, in real CMOS circuits:

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \times I_{static}$$

where $I_{static}$ includes:
- Reverse-bias leakage current of PN-junctions
- Sub-threshold conduction current ($I_{sub}$)
- Gate-oxide tunneling leakage current ($I_{gate}$)

#### 2.1.2 Static Power in TTL

In TTL families, a constant current flows from $V_{CC}$ to ground even in the static state due to the totem-pole output stage and the multi-emitter input transistor. This makes TTL inherently more power-hungry than CMOS.

$$P_{static,TTL} = V_{CC} \times I_{CCL} \quad \text{(output LOW)}$$
$$P_{static,TTL} = V_{CC} \times I_{CCH} \quad \text{(output HIGH)}$$

#### 2.1.3 Static Power in ECL

ECL (Emitter-Coupled Logic) is the **most power-hungry** family because it operates with transistors in the **linear (active) region**, never fully switching off. A constant current $I_{EE}$ flows through the emitter-coupled pair.

$$P_{static,ECL} = V_{EE} \times I_{EE} = (V_{EE} - V_{BE}) \times I_{EE} / R_E$$

### 2.2 Dynamic Power Dissipation

Dynamic power is consumed when the output node capacitance charges and discharges during logic transitions. This is the **dominant component** in most CMOS designs.

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (0 to 1)
- $C_L$ = load capacitance at the output node
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage
- $f$ = switching frequency (clock frequency)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Square-Law Dependence:** Notice that dynamic power is proportional to the **square** of the supply voltage. This is why reducing $V_{DD}$ is the most effective technique for low-power design — halving $V_{DD}$ reduces dynamic power by **4×**.

### 2.3 Short-Circuit Power Dissipation

During a logic transition, there is a brief interval when both the PMOS and NMOS transistors in a CMOS inverter are simultaneously ON, creating a direct current path from $V_{DD}$ to GND.

$$P_{SC} = \frac{\beta}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \cdot \tau \cdot f$$

where:
- $\beta$ = transconductance parameter of the transistors
- $V_{th}$ = threshold voltage
- $\tau$ = rise/fall time at the input

### 2.4 Leakage Power Dissipation

In modern deep-submicron technologies, leakage currents have become a major concern. The total leakage power is:

$$P_{leak} = V_{DD} \times I_{leakage}$$

where $I_{leakage}$ includes sub-threshold current, gate-oxide tunneling current, and reverse-biased junction leakage.

### 2.5 Total Power Dissipation

$$P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{dynamic} + P_{SC} + P_{leak}$$

### 2.6 Power-Delay Product (PDP)

PDP is a **figure of merit** that combines speed and power consumption into a single metric. It represents the **energy consumed per switching event**.

$$PDP = P_{avg} \times t_p \quad \text{(in Joules)}$$

where $t_p$ is the propagation delay.

### 2.7 Energy-Delay Product (EDP)

EDP is a more comprehensive metric that accounts for both energy efficiency and performance:

$$EDP = PDP \times t_p \quad \text{(in Joule-seconds)}$$

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Unit | Applicable To |
|---------|-------------|------|---------------|
| $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{static}$ | Static power | W | CMOS, TTL, ECL |
| $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | Switching power | W | All families |
| $P_{SC} = \frac{\beta}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \cdot \tau \cdot f$ | Short-circuit power | W | CMOS |
| $P_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leakage}$ | Leakage power | W | Submicron CMOS |
| $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_p$ | Power-delay product | J | Figure of merit |
| $EDP = PDP \cdot t_p$ | Energy-delay product | J·s | Figure of merit |
| $P_{avg} = \frac{V_{DD}}{T} \int_{0}^{T} i_{DD}(t) \, dt$ | Average power | W | General |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** In mobile SoC (System-on-Chip) design, power dissipation analysis is performed at the **register-transfer level (RTL)** and **gate level** to estimate battery life, optimize thermal envelopes, and meet power budgets for performance bins.

### 2.9 Comparison of Logic Families by Power Dissipation

| Logic Family | Static Power | Dynamic Power | Total Power (per gate) | Speed |
|--------------|--------------|---------------|------------------------|-------|
| **CMOS (4000 series)** | Negligible | Low (depends on freq.) | ~10 nW (static) | Slow |
| **TTL (74xx)** | High (~10 mW) | Moderate | ~10 mW | Moderate |
| **ECL (10K/100K)** | Very High | Moderate | ~25–50 mW | Very Fast |
| **BiCMOS** | Moderate | Low–Moderate | ~1–5 mW | Fast |
| **Modern CMOS (90 nm)** | Significant (leakage) | Moderate | ~1–10 μW/gate (static) | Very Fast |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Dynamic Power Dissipation in CMOS

The dynamic power arises from charging and discharging the load capacitance $C_L$ at the output node of a CMOS gate. Let us derive this rigorously.

**Step 1: Energy Stored in the Capacitor During Charging**

When the output transitions from LOW to HIGH, the load capacitance $C_L$ is charged from 0 to $V_{DD}$. The charge supplied by the power supply is:

$$Q = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$$

The energy delivered by the power supply is:

$$E_{supplied} = Q \cdot V_{DD} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 2: Energy Stored in the Capacitor**

The energy actually stored in the capacitor at the end of charging is:

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 3: Energy Dissipated During Charging**

The energy dissipated as heat in the PMOS transistor during charging is the difference:

$$E_{charging} = E_{supplied} - E_{stored} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 4: Energy Dissipated During Discharging**

When the output transitions from HIGH to LOW, the capacitor discharges through the NMOS transistor. The energy stored $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is completely dissipated as heat in the NMOS transistor (the supply does not deliver any energy during discharge — energy flows back to ground through the NMOS).

$$E_{discharging} = \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 5: Total Energy Dissipated Per Switching Cycle**

One complete switching cycle (0 → 1 → 0) dissipates:

$$E_{cycle} = E_{charging} + E_{discharging} = \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 6: Average Dynamic Power**

If the gate switches at a frequency $f$ (number of cycles per second), the energy dissipated per second is:

$$P_{dynamic} = E_{cycle} \cdot f = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

**Step 7: Including Switching Activity Factor**

In real circuits, not every clock cycle causes a transition. The switching activity factor $\alpha$ (0 ≤ α ≤ 1) accounts for this:

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** The energy dissipated is **independent of the rise/fall time** of the signal. It depends only on $C_L$, $V_{DD}$, and the number of transitions. This is a fundamental result in CMOS power analysis.

### 3.2 Derivation: Short-Circuit Power Dissipation

**Step 1: Define the Short-Circuit Current**

During a transition, when $V_{in}$ is between $V_{tn}$ and $V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert$, both NMOS and PMOS are ON. The short-circuit current is approximately:

$$i_{SC}(t) \approx \beta \left[ (V_{in}(t) - V_{tn}) - \frac{V_{DD}}{2} \right] \cdot \frac{V_{DSAT}}{2}$$

**Step 2: Integrate Over the Transition Interval**

Assuming a linear input ramp with rise time $\tau$:

$$I_{SC,avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{\tau} i_{SC}(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3: Apply Symmetry and Simplify**

After integration and simplification (a standard result in CMOS VLSI design):

$$I_{SC,avg} = \frac{\beta}{12} (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \cdot \frac{\tau}{V_{DD}}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Average Short-Circuit Power**

$$P_{SC} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{SC,avg} = \frac{\beta}{12} (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \cdot \tau \cdot f$$

### 3.3 Numerical Example: Dynamic Power Calculation

**Problem:** A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance of $C_L = 50 \text{ pF}$ at a clock frequency of $f = 100 \text{ MHz}$ with a supply voltage of $V_{DD} = 3.3 \text{ V}$. The switching activity factor is $\alpha = 0.2$. Calculate the dynamic power dissipation.

**Step 1: Identify the given values**

$$C_L = 50 \text{ pF} = 50 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$$
$$f = 100 \text{ MHz} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$$
$$V_{DD} = 3.3 \text{ V}, \quad \alpha = 0.2$$

**Step 2: Apply the dynamic power formula**

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values**

$$P_{dynamic} = 0.2 \times (50 \times 10^{-12}) \times (3.3)^2 \times (100 \times 10^6)$$

**Step 4: Compute step by step**

$$P_{dynamic} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-12} \times 10.89 \times 100 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{dynamic} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10.89 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P_{dynamic} = 0.2 \times 54450 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P_{dynamic} = 10890 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{dynamic} = 10.89 \text{ mW}}$$

**Step 5: Interpret the result**

This 10.89 mW of power is dissipated **per gate** at this operating point. For a chip with one million such inverters switching at the same rate, the total dynamic power would be approximately **10.89 kW** — illustrating why power management is critical in VLSI design.

### 3.4 Numerical Example: Effect of Voltage Scaling

**Problem:** The same inverter is now operated at $V_{DD} = 1.8 \text{ V}$. Find the new dynamic power.

**Step 1: Apply the formula with the new voltage**

$$P_{dynamic}' = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot (V_{DD}')^2 \cdot f$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$P_{dynamic}' = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-12} \times (1.8)^2 \times 100 \times 10^6$$

**Step 3: Compute**

$$P_{dynamic}' = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.24 \times 100 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{dynamic}' = 0.2 \times 16200 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P_{dynamic}' = 3240 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{dynamic}' = 3.24 \text{ mW}}$$

**Step 4: Compute the reduction ratio**

$$\frac{P_{dynamic}'}{P_{dynamic}} = \frac{3.24}{10.89} = 0.2975 \approx 0.30$$

**Power reduction ≈ 70%** — demonstrating the dramatic impact of voltage scaling on dynamic power.

### 3.5 Power-Delay Product (PDP) Numerical Example

**Problem:** A logic gate has an average power dissipation of $P_{avg} = 5 \text{ mW}$ and a propagation delay of $t_p = 2 \text{ ns}$. Calculate the PDP.

**Step 1: Apply the PDP formula**

$$PDP = P_{avg} \times t_p$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$PDP = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\boxed{PDP = 10 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J} = 10 \text{ pJ}}$$

This means **10 picojoules of energy** is consumed per switching event — a critical metric for high-performance and low-power design.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Power Reduction Techniques

| Technique | Reduces $P_{static}$? | Reduces $P_{dynamic}$? | Trade-off |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|-----------|
| **Voltage Scaling ($V_{DD}$ ↓)** | Yes (linearly) | Yes (quadratically) | Slower gates |
| **Frequency Scaling ($f$ ↓)** | No | Yes (linearly) | Lower throughput |
| **Capacitance Reduction** | No | Yes (linearly) | Smaller transistors, leakage ↑ |
| **Clock Gating** | No | Yes (selectively) | Clock tree complexity |
| **Power Gating** | Yes (drastically) | No | Sleep transistor overhead |
| **Multi-Threshold CMOS (MTCMOS)** | Yes | No | Higher delay |
| **Adiabatic Logic** | No | Yes (theoretically to 0) | Complex clocking |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Power Dissipation Components in CMOS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[Total Power Dissipation P total] --> A[Static Power P static]
    PWR --> B[Dynamic Power P dynamic]
    PWR --> C[Short Circuit Power P SC]
    PWR --> D[Leakage Power P leak]

    A --> A1[Reverse Bias Leakage]
    A --> A2[Sub threshold Conduction]
    A --> A3[Gate Oxide Tunneling]

    B --> B1[Charging Load Capacitance]
    B --> B2[Discharging Load Capacitance]
    B1 --> B1a[Energy Supplied by VDD = C L VDD squared]
    B2 --> B2a[Energy Dissipated in NMOS = half C L VDD squared]

    C --> C1[Simultaneous PMOS and NMOS ON]
    C --> C2[Direct VDD to GND Path]
    C1 --> C2

    D --> D1[Sub threshold Leakage I sub]
    D --> D2[Junction Leakage I junc]
    D --> D3[Gate Leakage I gate]

    A --> SUM[Summed for Total P static]
    B --> SUM2[Multiplied by alpha f for P dynamic]
    C --> SUM3[Integrated over transition for P SC]
    D --> SUM4[Computed as VDD times I leak]

    SUM --> OUT[Final P total in Watts]
    SUM2 --> OUT
    SUM3 --> OUT
    SUM4 --> OUT

    OUT --> APP1[Battery Life Estimation]
    OUT --> APP2[Thermal Design Power TDP]
    OUT --> APP3[Reliability Analysis MTBF]
    OUT --> APP4[Chip Cooling Requirements]
```

### 4.2 CMOS Inverter Switching Waveform and Power Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Signal V in]
        I1[Rising Edge from 0 to VDD]
        I2[Steady HIGH]
        I3[Falling Edge from VDD to 0]
        I4[Steady LOW]
    end

    subgraph REGION1[Region 1 Both Transistors OFF]
        R1[Static State]
        R1P[P static Approx Zero]
    end

    subgraph REGION2[Region 2 Both Transistors ON]
        R2[Short Circuit Current Flows]
        R2P[P SC Dissipated]
    end

    subgraph REGION3[Region 3 Capacitor Charging]
        R3[PMOS ON NMOS OFF]
        R3P[Energy from VDD half stored half dissipated]
    end

    subgraph REGION4[Region 4 Both Transistors OFF]
        R4[Static State]
        R4P[P static Approx Zero]
    end

    subgraph REGION5[Region 5 Both Transistors ON]
        R5[Short Circuit Current Flows]
        R5P[P SC Dissipated]
    end

    subgraph REGION6[Region 6 Capacitor Discharging]
        R6[NMOS ON PMOS OFF]
        R6P[Stored Energy Dissipated in NMOS]
    end

    I1 --> REGION2
    REGION2 --> REGION3
    REGION3 --> I2
    I2 --> REGION1
    REGION1 --> I3
    I3 --> REGION5
    REGION5 --> REGION6
    REGION6 --> I4
    I4 --> REGION1

    REGION3 --> RES[Total Energy per Cycle equals C L VDD squared]
    REGION6 --> RES
    RES --> FOUT[Average Power equals C L VDD squared times f]
```

### 4.3 Power Optimization Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Power Optimization Goal] --> L1[System Level Optimization]
    L1 --> L1a[Algorithm Selection]
    L1 --> L1b[Architecture Choice]

    L1a --> L2[Logic Level Optimization]
    L1b --> L2
    L2 --> L2a[Logic Minimization]
    L2 --> L2b[Fan out Reduction]

    L2a --> L3[Circuit Level Optimization]
    L2b --> L3
    L3 --> L3a[Transistor Sizing]
    L3 --> L3b[Voltage Scaling]

    L3a --> L4[Layout Level Optimization]
    L3b --> L4
    L4 --> L4a[Placement and Routing]
    L4 --> L4b[Clock Tree Synthesis]

    L4a --> RESULT[Minimum Power Dissipation Achieved]
    L4b --> RESULT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Define static power dissipation and dynamic power dissipation in a CMOS inverter. Which one is dominant in modern submicron technologies?**

**Model Answer:**

**Static Power Dissipation:** Static power dissipation is the power consumed by a CMOS circuit when the output is not switching — i.e., when the logic gate is in a steady HIGH or steady LOW state. In an ideal CMOS inverter, the static current is zero because either the PMOS or NMOS transistor is in cutoff. However, in real circuits, leakage currents cause:

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \times I_{leakage}$$

**Dynamic Power Dissipation:** Dynamic power dissipation is the power consumed during switching transitions due to the charging and discharging of load capacitances:

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

**Dominance in Modern Submicron Technologies:** In modern submicron CMOS (below 90 nm), **static power dissipation (leakage power) has become equally important or even dominant** over dynamic power due to aggressive scaling of transistor dimensions, which increases sub-threshold and gate-oxide leakage currents.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**What is the Power-Delay Product (PDP)? Why is it considered a better figure of merit than either power or delay alone?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Power-Delay Product (PDP) is the product of the average power dissipation and the propagation delay of a logic gate. It represents the energy consumed per switching event.

$$PDP = P_{avg} \times t_p \quad \text{(measured in Joules)}$$

**Why PDP is a Better Figure of Merit:**

1. **Combines Speed and Power:** A gate can be fast but power-hungry, or slow but low-power. PDP quantifies the **energy efficiency** of switching — a single number that captures both metrics.

2. **Technology Independent:** PDP allows fair comparison between different logic families and technology nodes.

3. **Design Trade-off Insight:** Two gates with the same PDP consume the same energy per operation, regardless of whether one is fast-and-powerful or slow-and-efficient.

4. **Energy per Operation:** PDP directly represents the **switching energy**, which is fundamental for battery-powered and energy-constrained applications.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for dynamic power dissipation in a CMOS inverter. Clearly show the charging and discharging phases, and explain why dynamic power is proportional to the square of the supply voltage. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance of 100 pF at a clock frequency of 50 MHz with a supply voltage of 5 V. If the switching activity factor is 0.3, calculate: (i) the dynamic power dissipation, and (ii) the energy dissipated per switching cycle. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Dynamic Power:**

The derivation proceeds through the following steps:

**Step 1:** When the output transitions from 0 to $V_{DD}$, the PMOS transistor charges the load capacitance $C_L$. The charge supplied by $V_{DD}$ is $Q = C_L V_{DD}$, and the energy supplied is $E_{supplied} = C_L V_{DD}^2$.

**Step 2:** At the end of charging, the energy stored in the capacitor is $E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$.

**Step 3:** The energy dissipated as heat in the PMOS during charging is:

$$E_{charging} = C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 4:** During discharge (HIGH to LOW), the stored energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is dissipated entirely in the NMOS transistor.

**Step 5:** Total energy per cycle:

$$E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 6:** With switching activity $\alpha$ and frequency $f$:

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

**Square-Law Dependence Explanation:** During charging, both the energy supplied ($C_L V_{DD}^2$) and the energy stored ($\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$) scale as $V_{DD}^2$. The difference (dissipated energy) therefore also scales as $V_{DD}^2$. This quadratic dependence makes voltage scaling the most effective power-reduction technique — reducing $V_{DD}$ by half reduces dynamic power by a factor of 4.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:** $C_L = 100 \text{ pF} = 100 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$, $f = 50 \text{ MHz} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$, $V_{DD} = 5 \text{ V}$, $\alpha = 0.3$

**(i) Dynamic Power:**

$$P_{dynamic} = 0.3 \times 100 \times 10^{-12} \times (5)^2 \times 50 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{dynamic} = 0.3 \times 100 \times 10^{-12} \times 25 \times 50 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{dynamic} = 0.3 \times 125000 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{P_{dynamic} = 37.5 \text{ mW}}$$

**(ii) Energy per Cycle:**

The energy dissipated per switching cycle (including activity factor):

$$E_{cycle} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 = 0.3 \times 100 \times 10^{-12} \times 25$$

$$E_{cycle} = 0.3 \times 2500 \times 10^{-12} = 750 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{E_{cycle} = 750 \text{ pJ}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the sources of power dissipation in CMOS circuits. Discuss in detail the short-circuit power dissipation and the conditions under which it becomes significant. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare the power dissipation characteristics of CMOS, TTL, and ECL logic families. Justify why ECL is the fastest and CMOS is the most power-efficient. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Sources of Power Dissipation in CMOS:**

Power dissipation in CMOS circuits arises from four primary sources:

**1. Dynamic Switching Power:** When the output node capacitance charges and discharges during logic transitions. This is given by $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ and is the dominant component in most CMOS designs.

**2. Short-Circuit Power:** During a logic transition, both PMOS and NMOS transistors are simultaneously ON for a brief interval, creating a direct current path from $V_{DD}$ to GND:

$$P_{SC} = \frac{\beta}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \cdot \tau \cdot f$$

**Conditions where $P_{SC}$ becomes significant:**
- When the **input rise/fall time ($\tau$) is large** (slow input edges)
- When the **load capacitance is small** (so output switches faster than input)
- When the **transistor sizes are large** (high $\beta$)
- Short-circuit power can be **minimized** by ensuring that the output rise/fall time is much faster than the input rise/fall time.

**3. Static (Leakage) Power:** In real CMOS, leakage currents flow even in the static state:

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \times I_{leakage}$$

Leakage sources include sub-threshold conduction, gate-oxide tunneling, and reverse-biased junction leakage. This is increasingly dominant in deep submicron technologies.

**4. DC Path Power:** In some CMOS circuits (transmission gates, pseudo-nMOS logic), there is a static DC current path from $V_{DD}$ to GND due to a pull-up PMOS that is always partially ON.

**Part (b) — Comparison of CMOS, TTL, and ECL:**

| Parameter | CMOS | TTL | ECL |
|-----------|------|-----|-----|
| **Static Power** | Near zero (nW range) | High (~10 mW) | Very high (~25–50 mW) |
| **Dynamic Power** | Dominant, $\propto V_{DD}^2 f$ | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Supply Voltage** | 3.3 V – 5 V (modern: 0.7–1.2 V) | 5 V | -5.2 V (or -4.5 V) |
| **Speed (Propagation Delay)** | Moderate to fast | Moderate | Very fast (sub-ns) |
| **Noise Margin** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Fan-out** | Very high | Limited (~10) | Limited |

**Why ECL is the Fastest:**

ECL achieves the highest speed because:
1. Transistors operate in the **linear (active) region**, never saturating — this eliminates the **saturation delay** (storage time) that limits TTL and CMOS speeds.
2. It uses **differential signaling** (emitter-coupled pair) and **constant current biasing**, which provides fast switching and small voltage swings (~0.8 V).
3. The output uses an **emitter follower** which has low output impedance, enabling fast charging of load capacitances.
4. The trade-off is **high static power** because current flows continuously through the emitter-coupled pair.

**Why CMOS is the Most Power-Efficient:**

1. **Zero static current** in the ideal case — the complementary PMOS/NMOS structure ensures one transistor is always OFF in the steady state.
2. **Dynamic power scales with $V_{DD}^2$**, allowing aggressive voltage scaling for low-power operation.
3. **High noise immunity** allows operation at low voltages without errors.
4. **Scalability** — modern CMOS processes allow billions of transistors on a single chip with manageable power budgets.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Students often confuse **mW (milliwatts)** with **μW (microwatts)** or **nW (nanowatts)**. Always check the order of magnitude in power calculations.
> 2. **Forgetting the Switching Activity Factor:** In dynamic power problems, students sometimes write $P = C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ instead of $P = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$. The activity factor $\alpha$ is **critical** and must be included.
> 3. **Confusing Static and Dynamic Power Sources:** Examiners often ask students to identify which power component is dominant. In **modern submicron CMOS (< 90 nm)**, **leakage (static) power** is dominant, NOT dynamic power. In older technologies (> 250 nm), dynamic power dominates.
> 4. **Missing the "0.5" Factor:** In the derivation of dynamic power, students often forget that only **half** the energy from $V_{DD}$ is dissipated during charging, while the other half is stored in the capacitor. The full $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is dissipated only during discharge.
> 5. **Power-Delay Product Units:** PDP is in **Joules (J)**, not Watts. Many students incorrectly write the unit as Watts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Power dissipation** in digital ICs is the conversion of electrical energy into heat, measured in **Watts (W)**.
- ✅ **Static power** = $V_{DD} \times I_{static}$ — dominant in deep submicron CMOS due to leakage.
- ✅ **Dynamic power** = $\alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ — dominant in older CMOS technologies.
- ✅ **Short-circuit power** occurs when both PMOS and NMOS are simultaneously ON during transitions.
- ✅ **Energy per cycle** = $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ (independent of rise/fall time).
- ✅ **Voltage scaling** is the most effective power-reduction technique ($P_{dynamic} \propto V_{DD}^2$).
- ✅ **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** = $P_{avg} \times t_p$ — the energy per switching event (in Joules).
- ✅ **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** = $PDP \times t_p$ — a more comprehensive figure of merit.
- ✅ **ECL** is the fastest but most power-hungry family (transistors operate in active region).
- ✅ **CMOS** is the most power-efficient (near-zero static current in ideal case).
- ✅ **TTL** has moderate power dissipation and speed (saturated transistor operation).
- ✅ In modern deep-submicron CMOS, **leakage (static) power has become dominant** over dynamic power.
- ✅ The **switching activity factor $\alpha$** reduces the effective switching frequency: average switching frequency = $\alpha \cdot f$.
- ✅ **Power gating**, **clock gating**, and **multi-threshold CMOS (MTCMOS)** are key low-power design techniques.
- ✅ For battery-powered devices, minimizing $P_{dynamic}$ and $P_{leak}$ is critical for extending battery life.
- ✅ **Thermal Design Power (TDP)** is the maximum sustained power a chip is expected to dissipate, which determines cooling requirements.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise Margin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT304 - DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Noise Margin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Noise Margin (NM)** is a quantitative measure of the immunity of a digital logic gate against unwanted electrical disturbances (noise). It is defined as the maximum amplitude of an external noise voltage that can be superimposed on a logic signal without causing the gate to interpret an incorrect logic level at its input.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **CMOS Logic Circuits** (Module 4), noise margin is evaluated by superimposing the **Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC)** of a logic gate with its **mirror-image VTC** rotated 180°. The four critical intersection points of these curves are:

| Parameter | Full Form | Meaning |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| $V_{OH}$ | Output High Voltage | Minimum output voltage recognized as a valid logic-1 |
| $V_{OL}$ | Output Low Voltage | Maximum output voltage recognized as a valid logic-0 |
| $V_{IH}$ | Input High Voltage | Minimum input voltage recognized as a valid logic-1 |
| $V_{IL}$ | Input Low Voltage | Maximum input voltage recognized as a valid logic-0 |

The two derived noise margins are formally defined as:

- **High-Level Noise Margin:** $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$
- **Low-Level Noise Margin:** $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Whispering in a Stadium" Analogy:** Imagine you are shouting instructions to a friend at the far end of a noisy football stadium. Your friend's hearing sensitivity is limited — they can only respond if the message reaches them at a certain loudness threshold. The **Noise Margin is the "safety buffer"** between how loudly you must speak and the minimum loudness your friend needs to clearly understand. If a noise spike louder than this buffer occurs, your instructions will be misheard.

In the electrical domain:

- The **stadium noise** = external electrical interference, crosstalk, ground bounce, or power supply ripple.
- The **shouted message** = the digital output voltage ($V_O$).
- The **friend's hearing threshold** = the input voltage required to register a logic high or low ($V_{IH}$ or $V_{IL}$).
- The **buffer** = Noise Margin.

A larger noise margin means the circuit is **more robust** and **less likely to malfunction** in real-world noisy environments — a critical requirement for industrial, automotive, and embedded systems.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a symmetric CMOS inverter with matched pull-up and pull-down networks, the ideal noise margin target is:
> $$NM_H = NM_L = 0.5 \cdot V_{DD} \approx 2.5 \text{ V (for } V_{DD} = 5 \text{ V)}$$
> For modern sub-micron CMOS ($V_{DD} = 1.8 \text{ V}$ or lower), the absolute NM shrinks, which is a major reliability concern addressed by **Voltage Scaling** techniques.

The dimensions used in CMOS derivation:

- $V_{DD}$ = Supply voltage (**bold** = standard parameter)
- $V_{Tn}$ = NMOS threshold voltage
- $V_{Tp}$ = PMOS threshold voltage
- $V_t = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$ (thermal voltage at 300 K)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The concept of noise margin is best understood by analyzing a **CMOS Inverter's Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC)** curve. The VTC is a plot of $V_{out}$ vs. $V_{in}$ which exhibits five distinct regions of operation:

1. **Region 1 — $0 \le V_{in} < V_{Tn}$:** NMOS is OFF, PMOS is in triode. Output is pulled to $V_{DD}$. → Logic High
2. **Region 2 — $V_{Tn} \le V_{in} < V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert$:** Both NMOS and PMOS are in **saturation**. The high-gain transition occurs here.
3. **Region 3 — Switching Threshold ($V_M$):** The point where $V_{in} = V_{out}$ and the gain is momentarily at its most negative value.
4. **Region 4 — $V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert$:** PMOS enters triode, NMOS remains in saturation.
5. **Region 5 — $V_{in} > V_{DD}$:** Logic Low output.

### The 'Why' Behind Noise Margins

In the **transition region** (Region 2), the curve has a **steep slope** (large negative gain). Outside this region, the gain drops to nearly zero (either saturated high or saturated low). The **inflection boundaries** between these regions define the operational limits of the gate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** A logic gate is "fault-tolerant" only when its input lies in the **flat regions** of the VTC (above $V_{IH}$ for High, below $V_{IL}$ for Low). Any noise that pushes the input voltage into the transition region can cause **metastability** or **logic errors**.

### Determination of $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$

The exact boundaries are defined by the **unity-gain points** on the VTC:

> **$V_{IL}$** = Value of $V_{in}$ at which the slope $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ on the lower transition.
>
> **$V_{IH}$** = Value of $V_{in}$ at which the slope $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ on the upper transition.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Unit |
|--------|---------|-------------|------|
| $NM_H$ | $V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | High-Level Noise Margin | Volts (V) |
| $NM_L$ | $V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Low-Level Noise Margin | Volts (V) |
| $V_{OH}$ | $\approx V_{DD}$ (ideal) | Output High Voltage | V |
| $V_{OL}$ | $\approx 0$ (ideal) | Output Low Voltage | V |
| $V_{IL}$ | $\dfrac{3V_{DD} + 2V_{Tp} - V_{Tn}}{8}$ (approx) | Input Low Threshold | V |
| $V_{IH}$ | $\dfrac{7V_{DD} - 2V_{Tp} + V_{Tn}}{8}$ (approx) | Input High Threshold | V |
| $V_M$ | $\dfrac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$ | Switching Threshold | V |
| $k_n$ | $\dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2}\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n$ | NMOS transconductance parameter | A/V² |
| $k_p$ | $\dfrac{\mu_p C_{ox}}{2}\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p$ | PMOS transconductance parameter | A/V² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetry Condition for Maximum Noise Margin:** When $V_M = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$ and $k_n = k_p$, the noise margins are balanced and maximized. This is achieved by sizing the PMOS transistor as $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p = 2 \text{ to } 3 \times \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n$ to compensate for the lower hole mobility ($\mu_p \approx 0.5 \mu_n$).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> **Application Domains:**
>
> - **Industrial Control Systems:** PLCs and motor drives operate in electrically noisy environments with VFDs, relays, and solenoids. High NM prevents false triggering.
> - **Automotive Electronics (CAN/LIN Bus):** ISO 26262 functional safety requires predictable noise immunity.
> - **Aerospace and Defense:** Radiation-hardened ICs use advanced CMOS design to ensure noise margins survive cosmic ray upsets.
> - **Mixed-Signal SoCs:** Digital logic blocks must not be corrupted by switching noise from adjacent analog/RF blocks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CMOS Inverter VTC — Region 2 (Saturation-Saturation)

### Step 1: Drain Current Equations in Saturation

The NMOS and PMOS transistors, when in saturation, obey the square-law equation:

$$I_{Dn,sat} = k_n (V_{GSn} - V_{Tn})^2 = k_n (V_{in} - V_{Tn})^2$$

$$I_{Dp,sat} = k_p (V_{SGp} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2 = k_p (V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2$$

> **Logic:** Since the transistors are in series between $V_{DD}$ and GND, the currents must be equal: $I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}$.

### Step 2: Equating the Currents

$$k_n (V_{in} - V_{Tn})^2 = k_p (V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2$$

Taking the square root of both sides (positive root chosen for physical relevance):

$$\sqrt{k_n} (V_{in} - V_{Tn}) = \sqrt{k_p} (V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)$$

### Step 3: Solving for the Switching Threshold $V_M$

At the switching threshold, $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_M$. Substituting:

$$\sqrt{k_n} (V_M - V_{Tn}) = \sqrt{k_p} (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)$$

$$V_M \sqrt{k_n} + V_M \sqrt{k_p} = V_{Tn} \sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)$$

$$V_M \left( \sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p} \right) = V_{Tn} \sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)$$

$$\boxed{V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} \sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)}{\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}}}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{k_n}$:

$$V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of $V_{IL}$ (Unity-Gain Point on Lower Transition)

### Step 1: Output Voltage in Saturation Region

The output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = V_{DD} - \sqrt{\dfrac{k_n}{k_p}} (V_{in} - V_{Tn}) - \dfrac{V_{in} - V_{Tn}}{2}$$

### Step 2: Differentiate to Find the Slope

$$\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -\sqrt{\dfrac{k_n}{k_p}} - \dfrac{1}{2}$$

### Step 3: Apply Unity-Gain Condition

Setting $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$:

$$-\sqrt{\dfrac{k_n}{k_p}} - \dfrac{1}{2} = -1$$

$$\sqrt{\dfrac{k_n}{k_p}} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

### Step 4: Solve for $V_{IL}$

Substituting back and solving yields the standard CMOS approximation:

$$\boxed{V_{IL} = \dfrac{1}{8} \left( 3V_{DD} + 2V_{Tp} - V_{Tn} \right)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of $V_{IH}$ (Unity-Gain Point on Upper Transition)

By symmetry of analysis, applying the same unity-gain condition in the region where NMOS is in saturation and PMOS is in triode:

$$\boxed{V_{IH} = \dfrac{1}{8} \left( 7V_{DD} - 2V_{Tp} + V_{Tn} \right)}$$

## 3.4 Final Noise Margin Formulas

### High-Level Noise Margin

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$$

Substituting $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ and $V_{IH}$:

$$NM_H = V_{DD} - \dfrac{1}{8} (7V_{DD} - 2V_{Tp} + V_{Tn})$$

$$NM_H = \dfrac{8V_{DD} - 7V_{DD} + 2V_{Tp} - V_{Tn}}{8}$$

$$\boxed{NM_H = \dfrac{V_{DD} + 2V_{Tp} - V_{Tn}}{8}}$$

### Low-Level Noise Margin

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$$

Substituting $V_{OL} = 0$ and $V_{IL}$:

$$\boxed{NM_L = \dfrac{3V_{DD} + 2V_{Tp} - V_{Tn}}{8}}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A CMOS inverter has $V_{DD} = 5 \text{ V}$, $V_{Tn} = 0.6 \text{ V}$, $V_{Tp} = -0.7 \text{ V}$. Calculate the noise margins.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate $V_{IL}$**

$$V_{IL} = \dfrac{3(5) + 2(-0.7) - 0.6}{8} = \dfrac{15 - 1.4 - 0.6}{8} = \dfrac{13}{8} = 1.625 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Calculate $V_{IH}$**

$$V_{IH} = \dfrac{7(5) - 2(-0.7) + 0.6}{8} = \dfrac{35 + 1.4 + 0.6}{8} = \dfrac{37}{8} = 4.625 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Assume $V_{OH} = V_{DD} = 5 \text{ V}$ and $V_{OL} = 0 \text{ V}$**

**Step 4: Calculate Noise Margins**

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = 5 - 4.625 = 0.375 \text{ V}$$

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 1.625 - 0 = 1.625 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The asymmetric noise margins ($NM_L \gg NM_H$) are typical of standard CMOS with equal NMOS/PMOS sizing. To equalize, increase $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p$ so that $k_p \approx k_n$, which boosts $NM_H$ and reduces $NM_L$ until both approach $V_{DD}/8 \cdot 4 = 2.5 \text{ V}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
