# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Noise Margin in a digital logic gate. Explain the significance of $NM_H$ and $NM_L$.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Noise Margin** is the maximum amplitude of unwanted noise voltage that can be tolerated by a logic gate without causing a change in its output logic state.
>
> - **$NM_H$ (High-Level Noise Margin):** Defined as $V_{OH} - V_{IH}$. It represents the maximum noise that can be added to a high-level output signal without being misinterpreted as a low.
> - **$NM_L$ (Low-Level Noise Margin):** Defined as $V_{IL} - V_{OL}$. It represents the maximum noise that can be added to a low-level output signal without being misinterpreted as a high.
>
> **Significance:** Higher noise margins indicate greater immunity to electrical noise, ensuring reliable operation in real-world environments.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is the Switching Threshold ($V_M$) of a CMOS inverter? Write the expression for $V_M$.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> The **Switching Threshold ($V_M$)** is the point on the VTC curve where $V_{in} = V_{out}$. It is the point of maximum gain (steepest slope) and defines the logical decision boundary of the inverter.
>
> $$V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$$
>
> For a symmetric inverter where $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp} \vert$ and $k_n = k_p$, the threshold is $V_M = V_{DD}/2$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the switching threshold voltage $V_M$ of a CMOS inverter. State the conditions under which $V_M = V_{DD}/2$. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1:** In the saturation-saturation region, equate the drain currents of NMOS and PMOS:
>
> $$k_n (V_{in} - V_{Tn})^2 = k_p (V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2$$
>
> **Step 2:** At $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_M$, solve for $V_M$:
>
> $$V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} \sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)}{\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}}$$
>
> **Step 3:** Dividing numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{k_n}$:
>
> $$V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$$
>
> **Conditions for $V_M = V_{DD}/2$:**
> - $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp} \vert$ (matched threshold voltages)
> - $k_p = k_n$ (matched transconductance, which requires $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p = 2.5 \times \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n$ to compensate for hole mobility)

**(b) For a CMOS inverter with $V_{DD} = 3.3 \text{ V}$, $V_{Tn} = 0.5 \text{ V}$, $V_{Tp} = -0.6 \text{ V}$, calculate the noise margins $NM_H$ and $NM_L$. Assume ideal $V_{OH}$ and $V_{OL}$. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1: Calculate $V_{IL}$**
>
> $$V_{IL} = \dfrac{3(3.3) + 2(-0.6) - 0.5}{8} = \dfrac{9.9 - 1.2 - 0.5}{8} = \dfrac{8.2}{8} = 1.025 \text{ V}$$
>
> **Step 2: Calculate $V_{IH}$**
>
> $$V_{IH} = \dfrac{7(3.3) - 2(-0.6) + 0.5}{8} = \dfrac{23.1 + 1.2 + 0.5}{8} = \dfrac{24.8}{8} = 3.1 \text{ V}$$
>
> **Step 3: Calculate Noise Margins**
>
> $$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 1.025 - 0 = 1.025 \text{ V}$$
>
> $$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = 3.3 - 3.1 = 0.2 \text{ V}$$
>
> **Step 4: Interpretation**
>
> The low $NM_H$ value indicates the inverter is more susceptible to noise on high-level signals. This asymmetry is typical and is corrected by adjusting PMOS sizing.

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) With a neat VTC diagram of a CMOS inverter, explain the regions of operation and mark the noise margin parameters. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Region 1** ($0 \le V_{in} < V_{Tn}$): NMOS is cut off, PMOS is in triode, $V_{out} = V_{DD}$.
>
> **Region 2** ($V_{Tn} \le V_{in} < V_{DD} + V_{Tp}$): Both transistors in saturation; transition region with high gain.
>
> **Region 3** ($V_{in} \approx V_M$): Switching threshold, where $V_{in} = V_{out}$.
>
> **Region 4** ($V_{in} > V_{DD} + V_{Tp}$): PMOS in triode, NMOS in saturation.
>
> **Region 5** ($V_{in} \ge V_{DD}$): PMOS cut off, $V_{out} = 0$.
>
> **Marked points:** $V_{OL}$ (Region 1 plateau), $V_{IL}$ (lower unity-gain point), $V_M$ (midpoint), $V_{IH}$ (upper unity-gain point), $V_{OH}$ (Region 5 plateau).
>
> **Noise margins shown:** $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ and $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ as horizontal distances on the $V_{in}$ axis.

**(b) Discuss the effects of device sizing on noise margins in a CMOS inverter. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Effect of PMOS Sizing:**
>
> When $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p$ increases, $k_p$ increases, and the ratio $k_p/k_n$ increases. This raises $V_M$ towards $V_{DD}$ and increases $NM_H$ while decreasing $NM_L$. Sizing the PMOS at 2-3× the NMOS width balances the noise margins.
>
> **Effect of Threshold Voltage:**
>
> Reducing $|V_{Tp}|$ and $V_{Tn}$ lowers the voltage required to turn on the transistors, sharpening the VTC transition and improving both $NM_H$ and $NM_L$. However, this increases **leakage current** in modern sub-micron devices.
>
> **Effect of Supply Voltage ($V_{DD}$):**
>
> Higher $V_{DD}$ provides larger absolute noise margins. For example, at $V_{DD} = 5 \text{ V}$, ideal $NM = 2.5 \text{ V}$. At $V_{DD} = 1.8 \text{ V}$, ideal $NM = 0.9 \text{ V}$. Modern low-power designs suffer reduced noise immunity.
>
> **Effect of Channel Length Modulation ($\lambda$):**
>
> Non-zero $\lambda$ slightly degrades the saturation characteristics, causing the VTC to have non-ideal slopes in the saturation regions. This marginally reduces the effective noise margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write $V_{IL} = V_{Tn}$ or $V_{IH} = V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|$ — these are INCORRECT. The exact values are derived from the unity-gain condition, not the threshold voltages directly.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing $V_{OH}/V_{OL}$ (output levels) with $V_{IH}/V_{IL}$ (input thresholds). They are NOT the same — the output levels are ideal ($V_{DD}$ and $0$), but the input thresholds are points on the VTC curve.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting that $NM_L$ is a positive quantity — it is $V_{IL} - V_{OL}$, NOT $V_{OL} - V_{IL}$.
> - **Mark Loss Zone:** In numerical problems, not substituting $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ and $V_{OL} = 0$ explicitly leads to partial credit loss. Always state the assumption clearly.
> - **Unit Error:** Reporting noise margins in mV when the supply is in V range leads to zero marks — ensure dimensional consistency.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Noise Margin** = Maximum noise voltage tolerable without logic error.
> - **$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$** and **$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$**.
> - **$V_{OH} = V_{DD}$** (ideal) and **$V_{OL} = 0$** (ideal).
> - **$V_{IL}$** and **$V_{IH}$** are determined by the **unity-gain points** where $dV_{out}/dV_{in} = -1$.
> - **Switching Threshold** $V_M$ is where $V_{in} = V_{out}$.
> - Formula for $V_{IL} = (3V_{DD} + 2V_{Tp} - V_{Tn})/8$ (approximate).
> - Formula for $V_{IH} = (7V_{DD} - 2V_{Tp} + V_{Tn})/8$ (approximate).
> - **Symmetric design** (matched $k_n$, $k_p$ and $V_{Tn} = |V_{Tp}|$) yields $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ and maximum balanced noise margins.
> - **PMOS sizing** of 2-3× NMOS compensates for hole mobility and equalizes $NM_H$ and $NM_L$.
> - **Lower $V_{DD}$** → Smaller absolute noise margins → Reduced reliability.
> - **Lower threshold voltage** → Better noise margin but higher leakage.
> - The **higher the noise margin**, the **more robust** the logic family.
> - Noise margin is **always positive** for a properly designed gate.
> - **Unity-gain derivation** is the KTU-favored method for $V_{IL}/V_{IH}$ problems.
> - CMOS noise margins typically range from **0.3V to 1.5V** depending on $V_{DD}$ and process technology.
> - **Crosstalk, ground bounce, and supply ripple** are the primary real-world noise sources.
> - KTU **commonly asks** to derive $V_M$ and compute $NM_H$, $NM_L$ for given parameters.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT304 - DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In digital electronics, a **Logic Voltage Level** is defined as the specific range of DC voltages that a digital circuit uses to unambiguously represent the two binary states — **Logic HIGH (1)** and **Logic LOW (0)**. Because real-world electronic components are non-ideal, every logic family (TTL, CMOS, LVCMOS, ECL, etc.) defines four critical threshold parameters:

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Minimum output HIGH voltage | $V_{OH}$ | The smallest voltage a gate is guaranteed to output when driving a Logic 1 |
| Minimum input HIGH voltage | $V_{IH}$ | The smallest input voltage that is guaranteed to be recognized as a Logic 1 |
| Maximum output LOW voltage | $V_{OL}$ | The largest voltage a gate is guaranteed to output when driving a Logic 0 |
| Maximum input LOW voltage | $V_{IL}$ | The largest input voltage that is guaranteed to be recognized as a Logic 0 |

These four boundaries form a **Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) "forbidden zone"** — also called the indeterminate or undefined region — where the logic state of the circuit cannot be reliably determined.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The understanding of $V_{IH}$, $V_{IL}$, $V_{OH}$, $V_{OL}$ and their derived **DC Noise Margins** is a *mandatory* Module 4 outcome. Questions frequently appear on noise immunity, logic family interfacing, and threshold voltage diagrams.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Staircase Rule"

Imagine you are standing at the bottom of a staircase, and a friend is shouting instructions to you from the top:

- If your friend shouts **LOUDLY** (above a certain loudness threshold), you hear them clearly → this is a **Logic HIGH** input.
- If your friend whispers **SOFTLY** (below another threshold), you hear nothing → this is a **Logic LOW** input.
- The **middle zone** — where the voice is neither loud nor soft — is where you get confused. You cannot tell whether they spoke or not. This is the **forbidden / indeterminate region**.

In electronics, the "voice loudness" is replaced by **voltage in volts**. Just as your ears have a hearing threshold, every digital input pin has its own **recognition threshold** ($V_{IH}$ and $V_{IL}$). The cleaner the separation between these thresholds, the more reliable the digital system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** For 5V TTL logic, the standard thresholds are:
> - $V_{OH} \geq 2.7\text{ V}$, $V_{IH} \geq 2.0\text{ V}$
> - $V_{OL} \leq 0.4\text{ V}$, $V_{IL} \leq 0.8\text{ V}$
>
> For 3.3V LVCMOS: $V_{IH} \geq 2.0\text{ V}$, $V_{IL} \leq 0.8\text{ V}$, $V_{OH} \geq 2.4\text{ V}$, $V_{OL} \leq 0.4\text{ V}$.

## 1.3 Why Logic Voltage Levels Exist

Digital systems are inherently **analog at the physical layer** — transistors operate with continuous voltage and current. The digital abstraction (0s and 1s) is a *layer of interpretation* imposed on top of the analog signals. The logic voltage levels are the **bridge** that translates continuous voltages into discrete, noise-tolerant binary decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A digital signal is **not** a single value; it is a **range** of voltages. This range-based interpretation is what gives digital systems their remarkable **noise immunity**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Threshold Voltages — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **$V_{OH}$ — Output HIGH Voltage (minimum guaranteed):**
   - When a gate *outputs* a logic 1, it cannot magically produce exactly $V_{CC}$. Due to internal transistor voltage drops ($V_{CE(sat)}$ for BJT in TTL, $R_{DS(on)}$ drops in CMOS), the actual output voltage is slightly less.
   - $V_{OH}$ is the **manufacturer-guaranteed minimum** of this output. If you measure a higher voltage, great — but the spec only guarantees "at least this much."

2. **$V_{IH}$ — Input HIGH Voltage (minimum guaranteed):**
   - This is the smallest voltage at the *input pin* that the receiving gate will treat as a clean Logic 1. Anything below $V_{IH}$ is suspicious and risks being misinterpreted.

3. **$V_{OL}$ — Output LOW Voltage (maximum guaranteed):**
   - When a gate outputs a Logic 0, it cannot pull the line all the way to exactly 0 V (ground). Internal resistance leaves a small residual voltage.
   - $V_{OL}$ is the **manufacturer-guaranteed maximum** residual voltage when pulling LOW.

4. **$V_{IL}$ — Input LOW Voltage (maximum guaranteed):**
   - This is the largest voltage at the input pin that the receiving gate will *still* treat as a clean Logic 0. Anything above $V_{IL}$ is questionable.

## 2.2 The Indeterminate (Forbidden) Region

Between $V_{IL}$ (max LOW) and $V_{IH}$ (min HIGH) lies the **forbidden band**:

$$V_{IL} \;<\; V_{in} \;<\; V_{IH} \quad \text{— Indeterminate Region}$$

Any voltage in this band is **undefined** and must be avoided by circuit design. Proper digital design ensures that all signal transitions **swing cleanly** through this region with sufficient margin.

## 2.3 DC Noise Margins — The Heart of Noise Immunity

A **noise margin** quantifies how much external electrical noise can corrupt a signal before it falls into the indeterminate region. There are two noise margins:

**High-Level Noise Margin (NM$_H$):**
$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$$

This tells you how much the output HIGH voltage can *drop* (due to noise) before the next stage misinterprets it as anything other than a clean HIGH.

**Low-Level Noise Margin (NM$_L$):**
$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$$

This tells you how much the output LOW voltage can *rise* (due to noise) before the next stage misinterprets it as anything other than a clean LOW.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Rule:** A *good* digital logic family has **higher noise margins** (typically $> 0.4\text{ V}$). CMOS families generally offer better noise immunity than TTL because of their larger $NM_H$ and $NM_L$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| High Noise Margin | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Volts (V) | Must be **positive** for reliable HIGH detection |
| Low Noise Margin | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Volts (V) | Must be **positive** for reliable LOW detection |
| Total Noise Immunity | $NM = \min(NM_H,\, NM_L)$ | Volts (V) | The *worst-case* tolerance of the logic family |
| Indeterminate Bandwidth | $\Delta V_{ind} = V_{IH} - V_{IL}$ | Volts (V) | Width of the forbidden voltage region |
| Output Swing | $V_{Swing} = V_{OH} - V_{OL}$ | Volts (V) | Total useful voltage excursion of the output |

> [!NOTE]
> When interfacing two **different logic families**, the receiver's $V_{IH}$ and $V_{IL}$ must be respected by the driver's $V_{OH}$ and $V_{OL}$. Failure to do so leads to the dreaded **logic-level incompatibility** problem — a frequently tested KTU concept.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mixed-voltage PCB design**: Modern SoCs run at 1.8 V, while legacy peripherals use 3.3 V or 5 V. Logic voltage level matching (via level shifters, pull-ups, or voltage-tolerant I/O) is essential.
- **EMI / Crosstalk analysis**: Knowing noise margins lets a PCB designer predict whether nearby noisy traces will flip a logic state.
- **Industrial / Automotive electronics**: ISO 26262 and IEC 61508 standards mandate minimum noise margins for safety-critical digital logic.
- **Battery-powered IoT devices**: Lower voltage logic (1.0 V, 1.2 V) minimizes dynamic power ($P \propto V_{DD}^{\,2}$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Noise Margin from Voltage Threshold Definitions

We will derive the high-level noise margin from first principles using the four threshold voltages.

**Step 1 — State the logical condition for a valid HIGH detection.**

For a receiver to register a *clean* Logic 1, the voltage arriving at its input pin ($V_{in}$) must satisfy:

$$V_{in} \;\geq\; V_{IH}$$

**Step 2 — Express the input voltage as the driver's HIGH output corrupted by noise.**

The worst-case input voltage the receiver sees is the driver's minimum guaranteed HIGH output, minus any worst-case noise:

$$V_{in,\;worst} = V_{OH} - V_{noise}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the validity condition to the worst-case scenario.**

For detection to still succeed in the worst case, we require:

$$V_{OH} - V_{noise} \;\geq\; V_{IH}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the maximum tolerable noise.**

Rearranging the inequality:

$$V_{noise} \;\leq\; V_{OH} - V_{IH}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the bound as the High Noise Margin.**

By definition, the right-hand side is the High Noise Margin:

$$\boxed{\,NM_H \;=\; V_{OH} - V_{IH}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Repeat the derivation for the LOW state.**

For a Logic 0 detection, the condition is $V_{in} \leq V_{IL}$. Worst-case input:

$$V_{in,\;worst} = V_{OL} + V_{noise}$$

Apply validity:

$$V_{OL} + V_{noise} \;\leq\; V_{IL}$$

Solve:

$$V_{noise} \;\leq\; V_{IL} - V_{OL}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,NM_L \;=\; V_{IL} - V_{OL}\,}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A TTL logic gate datasheet specifies $V_{OH} = 2.7\text{ V}$, $V_{OL} = 0.4\text{ V}$, $V_{IH} = 2.0\text{ V}$, $V_{IL} = 0.8\text{ V}$ at $V_{CC} = 5.0\text{ V}$.
> **(a)** Compute $NM_H$, $NM_L$, and the overall noise immunity.
> **(b)** If the supply droops to $V_{CC} = 4.5\text{ V}$ and all thresholds shift down by $0.4\text{ V}$, find the new noise margins.

### Part (a) — Nominal Conditions

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = 2.7 - 2.0 = 0.7\text{ V}$$

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 0.8 - 0.4 = 0.4\text{ V}$$

$$NM = \min(NM_H,\, NM_L) = \min(0.7,\, 0.4) = 0.4\text{ V}$$

> **Interpretation:** The TTL gate can tolerate **up to 0.4 V of injected noise** on a LOW signal before misreading it. This is the *worst-case* immunity (limited by the LOW side).

### Part (b) — Degraded Supply

New thresholds: $V_{OH}' = 2.3\text{ V}$, $V_{OL}' = 0.0\text{ V}$, $V_{IH}' = 1.6\text{ V}$, $V_{IL}' = 0.4\text{ V}$.

$$NM_H' = 2.3 - 1.6 = 0.7\text{ V}$$

$$NM_L' = 0.4 - 0.0 = 0.4\text{ V}$$

$$NM' = \min(0.7,\, 0.4) = 0.4\text{ V}$$

> **Observation:** TTL logic noise margins are remarkably **insensitive to $V_{CC}$ variations** — this is a hallmark design feature of saturated-logic TTL families.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Popular Logic Families

| Logic Family | $V_{CC}$ (V) | $V_{OH}$ (V) | $V_{OL}$ (V) | $V_{IH}$ (V) | $V_{IL}$ (V) | $NM_H$ (V) | $NM_L$ (V) |
|--------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|------------|------------|
| TTL (5 V) | **5.0** | 2.7 | 0.4 | 2.0 | 0.8 | 0.7 | 0.4 |
| CMOS 5 V | **5.0** | 4.95 | 0.05 | 3.5 | 1.5 | 1.45 | 1.45 |
| LVTTL (3.3 V) | **3.3** | 2.4 | 0.4 | 2.0 | 0.8 | 0.4 | 0.4 |
| LVCMOS (3.3 V) | **3.3** | 3.2 | 0.1 | 2.0 | 0.8 | 1.2 | 0.7 |
| LVCMOS (1.8 V) | **1.8** | 1.7 | 0.1 | 0.99 | 0.63 | 0.71 | 0.53 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Lower $V_{CC}$ CMOS *shrinks* noise margins. This is the fundamental trade-off in modern low-power design: you save energy at the cost of noise immunity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Logic Voltage Level Band Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VCC["VCC = 5.0 V"]
        A["VHIGH Output Band\nV >= 2.7 V\nRecognized as Logic 1"]
    end
    subgraph NMH["High Noise Margin Band"]
        B["2.0 V <= V < 2.7 V\nNMH = 0.7 V\nSafe HIGH Zone"]
    end
    subgraph FORBIDDEN["INDETERMINATE FORBIDDEN REGION"]
        C["0.8 V < V < 2.0 V\nWidth = 1.2 V\nUNDEFINED STATE"]
    end
    subgraph NML["Low Noise Margin Band"]
        D["0.4 V < V <= 0.8 V\nNML = 0.4 V\nSafe LOW Zone"]
    end
    subgraph GND["GND = 0 V"]
        E["VLOW Output Band\nV <= 0.4 V\nRecognized as Logic 0"]
    end
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VIN["Input Voltage VIN\nSweeps 0 to VCC"]
    STAGE1["Stage 1: VIN <= VIL\nOutput = VOH (HIGH Saturation)"]
    STAGE2["Stage 2: VIL < VIN < VIH\nLinear Transition Region\nSlope = -Gain"]
    STAGE3["Stage 3: VIN >= VIH\nOutput = VOL (LOW Saturation)"]
    NOISE["Noise Margin Extraction\nNMH = VOH - VIH\nNML = VIL - VOL"]
    DECISION["Receiver Decision:\nV >= VIH  ->  LOGIC 1\nV <= VIL  ->  LOGIC 0\nOtherwise -> UNDEFINED"]
    VIN --> STAGE1
    VIN --> STAGE2
    VIN --> STAGE3
    STAGE1 --> NOISE
    STAGE3 --> NOISE
    NOISE --> DECISION
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Logic Family Interfacing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DRIVER["Driver Gate\nVOH_drive / VOL_drive"]
    LINE["Interconnect Line\n+ Noise Injection"]
    RX["Receiver Gate\nVIH_rx / VIL_rx"]
    CHECK{"Compatibility Check:\nVOH_drive >= VIH_rx?\nVOL_drive <= VIL_rx?"}
    PASS["PASS\nReliable Logic Transfer"]
    FAIL["FAIL\nRequires Level Shifter"]
    DRIVER --> LINE --> RX --> CHECK
    CHECK -- Yes --> PASS
    CHECK -- No --> FAIL
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Noise Margin Decision Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["Signal Input Stage"]
        S1["Sample VIN at Receiver Pin"]
    end
    subgraph COMPARE["Threshold Comparator Block"]
        S2["Comparator A:\nVIN vs VIH"]
        S3["Comparator B:\nVIN vs VIL"]
    end
    subgraph LOGIC["Decoder Logic Block"]
        S4["If VIN >= VIH  -> Q = 1"]
        S5["If VIN <= VIL  -> Q = 0"]
        S6["Else Q = X (Undefined)"]
    end
    subgraph MARGIN["Noise Margin Monitor"]
        S7["Compute NMH = VOH - VIH"]
        S8["Compute NML = VIL - VOL"]
        S9["Report NM = min(NMH, NML)"]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> S4
    S2 --> S6
    S3 --> S5
    S3 --> S6
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S9
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**Define the terms $V_{IH}$, $V_{IL}$, $V_{OH}$, and $V_{OL}$ as applied to a digital logic gate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **$V_{IH}$ (Input HIGH Voltage):** The *minimum* voltage level that is guaranteed to be recognized as a valid Logic 1 at the input of a logic gate.
- **$V_{IL}$ (Input LOW Voltage):** The *maximum* voltage level that is guaranteed to be recognized as a valid Logic 0 at the input of a logic gate.
- **$V_{OH}$ (Output HIGH Voltage):** The *minimum* voltage level that the output of a logic gate is guaranteed to produce when the output represents a Logic 1.
- **$V_{OL}$ (Output LOW Voltage):** The *maximum* voltage level that the output of a logic gate is guaranteed to produce when the output represents a Logic 0.

> *(1 mark for each correct definition with proper "minimum/maximum" qualifier — students often lose marks by omitting the qualifier.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**What is meant by noise margin in a digital logic family? Derive the expression for high-level and low-level noise margins.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The noise margin quantifies the maximum unwanted voltage (noise) that can be superimposed on a logic signal without causing the receiving gate to misinterpret the logic level.

**High-Level Noise Margin:**

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$$

**Low-Level Noise Margin:**

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$$

A larger noise margin means better noise immunity. *(2 marks for the two equations, 1 mark for the qualitative interpretation.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 4 — Internal Choice Provision** (KTU ESE Pattern: Answer ONE full question)

### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Draw the ideal voltage transfer characteristic (VTC) of a CMOS inverter and clearly label the regions: HIGH output, LOW output, transition region, $V_{IH}$, $V_{IL}$, $V_{OH}$, $V_{OL}$. Define noise margin and explain why CMOS offers superior noise immunity compared to TTL.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3.3 V LVCMOS driver is to be interfaced with a 5 V TTL receiver. The following specifications apply:

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Driver $V_{OH}$ | 2.4 V |
| Driver $V_{OL}$ | 0.4 V |
| Receiver $V_{IH}$ | 2.0 V |
| Receiver $V_{IL}$ | 0.8 V |

Compute $NM_H$ and $NM_L$ for this interface. Is the interface reliable? Justify your answer with reference to the noise margins. If the driver's $V_{OH}$ degrades to 1.9 V due to a heavy load, what happens and why?

**(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### Part (a) — VTC and Noise Immunity (7 marks)

**Diagram (3 marks):**

The VTC plots $V_{out}$ (y-axis) vs $V_{in}$ (x-axis). Key labeled points:

- Point A: $(V_{IL}, V_{OH})$ — corner where output begins to fall
- Point B: $(V_{IH}, V_{OL})$ — corner where output has fully fallen
- Point M: $(V_{M}, V_{M})$ — switching threshold (typically $V_{CC}/2$ for symmetric CMOS)

```
       VOH |XXXXXXX
           |       XX
           |         XX
           |           XX
        VM |-------------XX  (switching midpoint)
           |               XX
           |                 XX
        VOL |___________________XX
           |                       X
           +--------|----|---------|------> Vin
           0       VIL   VM       VIH   VCC
```

**Definitions (2 marks):**

- **$V_{OH}$:** Minimum output HIGH voltage
- **$V_{OL}$:** Maximum output LOW voltage
- **$V_{IH}$:** Minimum input HIGH threshold
- **$V_{IL}$:** Maximum input LOW threshold

**CMOS vs TTL Noise Immunity (2 marks):**

CMOS has near-rail-to-rail output swing ($V_{OH} \approx V_{CC}$, $V_{OL} \approx 0\text{ V}$) and threshold voltages centered around $V_{CC}/2$. This yields **symmetric and large noise margins** ($NM_H \approx NM_L \approx 0.45 \times V_{CC}$). TTL, due to its BJT saturation drops and internal voltage divider biasing, has smaller, asymmetric noise margins (typically $NM_H = 0.7\text{ V}$, $NM_L = 0.4\text{ V}$ at 5 V). Hence CMOS provides superior noise immunity, especially at higher $V_{CC}$.

---

#### Part (b) — Interface Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the noise margins (3 marks):**

$$NM_H = V_{OH,\,driver} - V_{IH,\,rx} = 2.4 - 2.0 = 0.4\text{ V}$$

$$NM_L = V_{IL,\,rx} - V_{OL,\,driver} = 0.8 - 0.4 = 0.4\text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Reliability verdict (2 marks):**

Both noise margins are equal and positive ($0.4\text{ V}$). The interface is **reliable**, but only marginally so. The overall noise immunity is:

$$NM = \min(NM_H,\, NM_L) = 0.4\text{ V}$$

This is the typical LVTTL-to-TTL threshold, and it works in practice but leaves little headroom.

**Step 3 — Degraded $V_{OH}$ scenario (2 marks):**

If the driver's $V_{OH}$ falls to 1.9 V:

$$NM_H^{new} = 1.9 - 2.0 = -0.1\text{ V}$$

The new $NM_H$ is **negative**, which is *physically meaningless* and indicates **interface failure**. The driver can no longer guarantee a valid Logic 1 at the receiver — the signal will sit inside or below the indeterminate region. The remedy is to use a **level shifter** or to **reduce the load** on the LVCMOS driver so that $V_{OH}$ recovers above 2.0 V.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define the terms $V_{IH}$, $V_{IL}$, $V_{OH}$, $V_{OL}$. Explain the concept of the **forbidden region** in a digital logic gate. How does its width affect the reliability of the logic family?

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two logic families, **Family A** and **Family B**, have the following specifications:

| Parameter | Family A | Family B |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| $V_{OH}$ | 4.9 V | 2.7 V |
| $V_{OL}$ | 0.1 V | 0.4 V |
| $V_{IH}$ | 3.5 V | 2.0 V |
| $V_{IL}$ | 1.5 V | 0.8 V |
| $V_{CC}$ | 5.0 V | 5.0 V |

Compute and compare the noise margins of both families. Which family is more suitable for **industrial, high-noise** environments? Justify with numerical evidence.

**(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Forbidden Region Concept (7 marks)

**Definitions (3 marks):**

*(Same as Question 1 Part A — award full marks for correctly stating $V_{IH}$, $V_{IL}$, $V_{OH}$, $V_{OL}$ with their "min/max" qualifiers.)*

**Forbidden Region Explanation (2 marks):**

The **forbidden region** (also called the indeterminate or undefined region) is the voltage band lying between $V_{IL}$ (max valid LOW) and $V_{IH}$ (min valid HIGH). Its width is:

$$\Delta V_{forbidden} = V_{IH} - V_{IL}$$

Any voltage in this band is *unrecognized* — the gate's output behavior becomes unpredictable, potentially causing **logic errors, metastability, or oscillation** in cascaded stages.

**Effect on Reliability (2 marks):**

- A **narrow** forbidden region → tighter input thresholds → lower noise margins → less noise-tolerant family.
- A **wide** forbidden region → larger indeterminate band, but if the output swings are rail-to-rail, the *effective* noise margins (which depend on $V_{OH}$ and $V_{OL}$) can still be large.

The *real* reliability metric is the noise margin, not just the forbidden width. CMOS deliberately places its forbidden region **centered** at $V_{CC}/2$ with wide margins on both sides.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparative Noise Margin Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Family A (CMOS-like) noise margins (2 marks):**

$$NM_H^A = V_{OH}^A - V_{IH}^A = 4.9 - 3.5 = 1.4\text{ V}$$

$$NM_L^A = V_{IL}^A - V_{OL}^A = 1.5 - 0.1 = 1.4\text{ V}$$

$$NM^A = \min(1.4,\, 1.4) = 1.4\text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Family B (TTL-like) noise margins (2 marks):**

$$NM_H^B = V_{OH}^B - V_{IH}^B = 2.7 - 2.0 = 0.7\text{ V}$$

$$NM_L^B = V_{IL}^B - V_{OL}^B = 0.8 - 0.4 = 0.4\text{ V}$$

$$NM^B = \min(0.7,\, 0.4) = 0.4\text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison and Selection (3 marks):**

| Metric | Family A | Family B |
|--------|----------|----------|
| $NM_H$ | **1.4 V** | 0.7 V |
| $NM_L$ | **1.4 V** | 0.4 V |
| Overall NM | **1.4 V** | 0.4 V |

**Conclusion:** **Family A (CMOS)** is more suitable for industrial, high-noise environments. It offers:

- **3.5× larger overall noise margin** (1.4 V vs 0.4 V)
- **Symmetric noise immunity** on both HIGH and LOW states
- **Rail-to-rail output swing**, leaving more headroom for signal degradation

Family B (TTL) would require extra shielding, filtering, or strict PCB layout discipline to operate reliably in noisy industrial settings.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Omitting "minimum" or "maximum"** in $V_{IH}$/$V_{IL}$/$V_{OH}$/$V_{OL}$ definitions → loses 1 mark per term.
> 2. **Swapping the noise margin formula** — writing $NM_H = V_{IH} - V_{OH}$ (a *negative* quantity!) → full 3-mark deduction.
> 3. **Ignoring the degraded-condition sub-part** in interfacing questions → fails to demonstrate understanding of practical reliability.
> 4. **Forgetting units** ("0.4" instead of "0.4 V") → minor 0.5-mark deduction per occurrence.
> 5. **Confusing $V_{CC}$ with $V_{OH}$** — these are *different* quantities; $V_{OH}$ is the *actual output*, $V_{CC}$ is the *supply rail*.
> 6. **Not stating "the interface is reliable only if both noise margins are positive"** — examiners specifically look for this condition.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Logic Voltage Levels**

- **Four core thresholds:** $V_{OH}$, $V_{IH}$, $V_{OL}$, $V_{IL}$ — always specify whether they are *minimum* or *maximum*.
- **Noise Margins:**
  - $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$
  - $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$
  - Both **must be positive** for a valid logic family.
- **Forbidden / Indeterminate Region:** lies strictly between $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ — never let a signal settle here.
- **CMOS > TTL** in noise immunity due to rail-to-rail output swing and symmetric thresholds.
- **Lower $V_{CC}$ ⇒ smaller noise margins** — the unavoidable trade-off in low-voltage digital design.
- **Logic family interfacing rule:** For reliable interface, the *driver's* $V_{OH} \geq$ *receiver's* $V_{IH}$ AND the *driver's* $V_{OL} \leq$ *receiver's* $V_{IL}$.
- **Total noise immunity** of a family = $\min(NM_H, NM_L)$ — the *worst-case* protection.
- **Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC)** is the visual representation of all four thresholds on a single plot.
- **Metastability risk:** signals lingering in the forbidden region can cause unpredictable flip-flop behavior — a classic Module-4 / Module-5 cross-topic question.
- **Units discipline:** all voltages in **Volts (V)**; always annotate units in numerical answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fan-In
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT304 - DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fan-In

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fan-In** of a logic gate is defined as the **maximum number of input signals** that the gate is designed to accept (i.e., the maximum number of inputs that can be reliably driven into a single logic gate) without degrading its specified electrical performance parameters such as noise margin, propagation delay, and power dissipation.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme Digital Electronics syllabus (Module 4 — Logic Families and Interfacing), **Fan-In** is a **static electrical characteristic** of a digital IC belonging to a particular logic family (TTL, CMOS, ECL, RTL, DTL, I²L, etc.). It quantifies the *input loading* presented by the gate to its driving source.

Mathematically, the **effective input loading factor** is normalized such that one standard input of the same family is defined as **1 Unit Load (UL)**. Hence, a gate whose every input draws one unit load is said to have a fan-in of **N** if it has **N such input pins**.

**Standard Metric:** Fan-In is a **dimensionless integer** (pure count of inputs).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of Fan-In as the **number of USB ports on a single laptop docking station**. The docking station (the logic gate) has a fixed amount of internal electrical "bandwidth" (current sourcing/sinking capability at its inputs). Each connected device (input signal line) draws a small amount of this bandwidth. The manufacturer specifies the maximum number of devices (the **fan-in rating**) you can plug in simultaneously before the station overheats or underperforms.

A simpler, more relatable analogy: imagine a **water tap (logic gate input)** connected to a **municipal water pipe (logic signal line)**. The pipe has a fixed pressure. If you try to draw water from too many taps (i.e., present too many inputs to a logic family not designed for that many), the pressure (voltage level) drops below the valid logic threshold, and the system fails. The maximum number of taps the pipe can reliably feed equals the **Fan-In**.

## 1.3 Why Fan-In Matters in KTU Examinations

Examiners frequently test the following linked triad of properties (all static parameters of a logic family):

- **Fan-In (N_IN)** — number of inputs accepted by the gate
- **Fan-Out (N_OUT)** — number of similar gates that the output can drive
- **Noise Margin (NM_H, NM_L)** — immunity to electrical noise, which is directly *correlated* to fan-in

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU 2024 trick question: *"If the fan-in of a logic gate is increased, what happens to its propagation delay and noise margin?"* The correct answer is that **both propagation delay and noise margin degrade** (worsen) because each additional input adds internal parasitic capacitance and additional input transistor stages.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Why Fan-In is Limited

The fan-in of a logic gate is **not arbitrary**; it is fundamentally constrained by the **internal circuit topology** of the logic family. Each additional input requires an additional **input transistor** (or an additional diode/emitter/CMOS pair) wired in parallel inside the IC. Let us dissect the consequences:

### Step-by-Step Reasoning:
1. **Physical Construction:** A multi-input TTL NAND gate, for example, uses a **multi-emitter transistor** at its input. The number of emitters is exactly the fan-in (typically 2, 3, or 4 for standard TTL). ECL gates use paralleled emitter-follower inputs; CMOS uses parallel NMOS/PMOS transistor pairs.
2. **Parasitic Capacitance Accumulation:** Every extra input pin contributes its own junction capacitance, lead capacitance, and PCB trace capacitance. These capacitances add up in **parallel**, so total input capacitance scales linearly with fan-in:
   $$C_{in,total} = N_{in} \times C_{in,per-input}$$
3. **Current Loading:** Each input draws a small input current $I_{IL}$ (LOW-level) or $I_{IH}$ (HIGH-level). The driving gate's output transistor must sink/source this cumulative current while still maintaining valid $V_{OL}$ or $V_{OH}$ levels.
4. **Noise Margin Erosion:** As fan-in grows, the series resistance of internal transistors (especially in the multi-emitter BJT of TTL) introduces additional voltage drops, shrinking the **DC noise margin**.
5. **Propagation Delay Inflation:** Charging/discharging the larger input capacitance takes more time:
   $$t_{pd} \propto R_{out} \times C_{in,total} = R_{out} \times (N_{in} \times C_{in,per-input})$$
6. **Power Budget:** Static and dynamic power both rise with fan-in because of the extra active devices and the increased charging current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Takeaway:** There is a fundamental **trade-off triangle** — *Fan-In ↔ Speed ↔ Noise Margin*. Increasing fan-in always degrades at least one of the other two.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Unit | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------------------|------|---------|
| 1 | **Fan-In ($N_{in}$)** | $N_{in} = \dfrac{I_{IL(driver \, max)}}{I_{IL(per \, input)}}$ | Dimensionless (count) | Ratio of available drive current to per-input load |
| 2 | **Total Input Capacitance** | $C_{in,total} = N_{in} \times C_{in}$ | Farads (F) | Capacitances add in parallel |
| 3 | **Propagation Delay vs. Fan-In** | $t_{pd} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot N_{in} \cdot C_{in}$ | Seconds (s) | First-order RC delay approximation |
| 4 | **DC Noise Margin (LOW)** | $NM_L = V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)}$ | Volts (V) | Decreases as $N_{in}$ rises |
| 5 | **DC Noise Margin (HIGH)** | $NM_H = V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)}$ | Volts (V) | Decreases as $N_{in}$ rises |
| 6 | **Power Dissipation (Dynamic)** | $P_d = C_{in,total} \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | Watts (W) | Rises with $N_{in}$ |
| 7 | **Standard Unit Load (UL)** | 1 UL ≡ 1.6 mA (TTL) or 1 UL ≡ 10 µA (CMOS) | mA or µA | Family-specific reference |
| 8 | **TTL Standard Fan-In** | $N_{in} = 1$ to $8$ (typically 2–4 for NAND gates) | Integer | Limited by multi-emitter transistor |
| 9 | **CMOS Standard Fan-In** | $N_{in} = 2$ to $8$ (practically up to 4 for speed) | Integer | Each input adds a CMOS pair |
| 10 | **ECL Standard Fan-In** | $N_{in} = 2$ to $5$ (OR/NOR gates) | Integer | High speed, but limited fan-in due to biasing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Valuation:** When calculating fan-in, examiners accept the **integer count of inputs** unless they specifically ask for the *loading fan-in* (which uses the current ratio formula). Read the question stem carefully.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ASIC Design:** Fan-in constraints dictate how many signals can be wired-OR'd or AND'd together inside an IC without using a buffer tree.
- **FPGA Routing:** Modern FPGAs use **LUTs (Look-Up Tables)** with a fixed fan-in (typically 4, 6, or 8 inputs). Exceeding this requires cascading multiple LUTs.
- **PCB Design:** A logic output driving many gate inputs in parallel must respect the cumulative fan-in loading.
- **Standard Cell Libraries:** Each cell's datasheet lists its fan-in and fan-out so that place-and-route tools can ensure electrical correctness.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Propagation Delay as a Function of Fan-In

**Goal:** Show that the propagation delay of a multi-input gate scales **linearly** with its fan-in.

**Step 1 — Equivalent Circuit:**
Model each input pin of a logic gate as a small capacitor $C_{in}$ to ground. All inputs appear in parallel from the perspective of the driving source.

**Step 2 — Total Capacitive Load:**
For a gate with $N_{in}$ inputs, the total input capacitance seen at the driver's output is:

$$
C_{L,total} = N_{in} \cdot C_{in} + C_{stray}
$$

where $C_{stray}$ captures the wiring/trace capacitance (often small compared to $C_{in}$).

**Step 3 — First-Order RC Charging Equation:**
The driver's output resistance $R_{out}$ charges this capacitor. The voltage across the capacitor as a function of time follows:

$$
V_{C}(t) = V_{final} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t / \tau}\right)
$$

with the **time constant** $\tau = R_{out} \cdot C_{L,total}$.

**Step 4 — Solve for the 50 % Switching Point:**
The propagation delay is conventionally measured at the **50 % point** of the voltage swing. Setting $V_C(t_{pd}) = 0.5 \cdot V_{final}$:

$$
0.5 = 1 - e^{-t_{pd}/\tau} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad e^{-t_{pd}/\tau} = 0.5
$$

**Step 5 — Take the Natural Logarithm:**

$$
-\frac{t_{pd}}{\tau} = \ln(0.5) = -\ln 2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad t_{pd} = \tau \cdot \ln 2
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\tau$:**

$$
t_{pd} = R_{out} \cdot C_{L,total} \cdot \ln 2
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $C_{L,total}$:**

$$
t_{pd} = R_{out} \cdot \ln 2 \cdot (N_{in} \cdot C_{in} + C_{stray})
$$

**Step 8 — Recognize the Approximation:**
For $C_{stray} \ll N_{in} \cdot C_{in}$, this simplifies to:

$$
\boxed{\,t_{pd} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{out} \cdot N_{in} \cdot C_{in}\,}
$$

Since $\ln 2 \approx 0.693 \approx 0.69$, the constant is verified. The relationship is **linear in $N_{in}$**: doubling the fan-in doubles the propagation delay (everything else equal).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fan-In from Input Current Loading (TTL Case)

**Goal:** Derive the input fan-in rating using TTL current specifications.

**Step 1 — Identify Standard TTL Parameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| $I_{IL}$ (input LOW current) | $-1.6 \, \text{mA}$ (sinks into the driver) |
| $I_{IH}$ (input HIGH current) | $40 \, \mu A$ (drawn from the driver) |
| $I_{OL(max)}$ (driver output LOW) | $16 \, \text{mA}$ |
| $I_{OH(max)}$ (driver output HIGH) | $-400 \, \mu A$ |

**Step 2 — Apply the Fan-In Definition for the LOW State:**

$$
N_{in,LOW} = \frac{I_{OL(max)}}{\vert I_{IL} \vert} = \frac{16 \, \text{mA}}{1.6 \, \text{mA}} = 10
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Fan-In Definition for the HIGH State:**

$$
N_{in,HIGH} = \frac{\vert I_{OH(max)} \vert}{I_{IH}} = \frac{400 \, \mu A}{40 \, \mu A} = 10
$$

**Step 4 — Take the Conservative Limit:**

$$
\boxed{\,N_{in,fan-in} = \min(N_{in,LOW}, \, N_{in,HIGH}) = 10\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value of 10 is the **standard TTL unit load** specification. It is a *loading* fan-in (how many similar inputs a single output can drive), but the same arithmetic framework applies when analyzing the *number of input pins* on a single multi-input gate, which is constrained by internal topology.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Fan-In Across Logic Families

| Logic Family | Typical Fan-In Range | Dominant Limiting Factor | Remarks |
|--------------|----------------------|--------------------------|---------|
| **RTL (Resistor-Transistor Logic)** | 2 – 4 | Base current distribution; poor noise margin | Obsolete, mentioned historically |
| **DTL (Diode-Transistor Logic)** | 2 – 8 | Diode forward drop stacking | Replaced by TTL |
| **TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic)** | 2 – 8 (multi-emitter BJT) | Multi-emitter transistor breakdown; leakage | Standard $N_{in}$ = 2, 3, 4, 8 |
| **ECL (Emitter-Coupled Logic)** | 2 – 5 | Bias network reference current; speed-critical | Uses paralleled emitter-followers |
| **CMOS (Complementary MOS)** | 2 – 8 (practically ≤ 4) | $R_{on} \cdot C_{in}$ delay product | Each input = 2 transistors |
| **I²L (Integrated Injection Logic)** | 1 (single input per inverter) | Inverter structure is single-input | Fan-in built by wired-AND |
| **BiCMOS** | 2 – 4 | Mix of BJT drive & CMOS input structure | Hybrid trade-off |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fan-In Concept Visualized

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeSrc1[Input Signal A] --> nodeGate
    nodeSrc2[Input Signal B] --> nodeGate
    nodeSrc3[Input Signal C] --> nodeGate
    nodeSrc4[Input Signal D] --> nodeGate
    nodeSrc5[Input Signal E] --> nodeGate
    nodeSrc6[Input Signal F] --> nodeGate
    nodeGate[AND Gate Fan-In equals 6]
    nodeGate --> nodeOut[Single Output Y]
    
    nodeCap1[Capacitance per input C in] -.-> nodeGate
    nodeCap2[Total Cin equals N in times C in] -.-> nodeGate
    nodeDel[Propagation Delay t pd increases with N in] -.-> nodeGate
    nodeNM[DC Noise Margin decreases with N in] -.-> nodeGate
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The left column shows $N_{in}$ independent input signal lines converging into a single AND gate (Fan-In = 6 in this example). The right column shows the resulting single output. The dashed feedback annotations highlight the three degradation effects (input capacitance accumulation, propagation delay growth, and noise margin erosion) that scale with $N_{in}$.

## 4.2 Mermaid Trade-off Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeFI[Fan In N in] --> nodeSP[Speed t pd]
    nodeFI --> nodeNM[Noise Margin NM]
    nodeSP --> nodePWR[Power Dissipation P d]
    nodeNM --> nodePWR
    nodeSP <--> nodeNM
    nodePWR <--> nodeFI
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This triangle is a frequently tested KTU concept: **you cannot simultaneously maximize fan-in, maximize speed, and maximize noise margin** in any single logic family. Each family chooses a different vertex of this triangle as its optimization target (e.g., ECL optimizes Speed, CMOS optimizes Power, TTL balances all three).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Fan-In of a logic gate. Why is the fan-in of practical digital ICs limited to small values (typically 2 to 8)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Fan-In of a logic gate is defined as the **maximum number of input signals** that the gate can accept, i.e., the number of input terminals provided by the manufacturer on a single logic gate package.

**Reason for Limitation (2 marks):** The fan-in is limited because:
1. Each additional input pin requires an extra input transistor (or multi-emitter in TTL / extra CMOS pair), increasing the **input capacitance** in parallel.
2. Increased capacitance raises the **propagation delay** of the gate.
3. The **DC noise margin** of the gate degrades due to additional series voltage drops inside the multi-input structure.
4. Hence, a practical trade-off is enforced between fan-in, speed, and noise margin, restricting standard fan-in to the range 2 – 8.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**How does increasing the fan-in of a CMOS NAND gate affect its (i) propagation delay, (ii) power dissipation, and (iii) noise margin?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a CMOS NAND gate with fan-in $N_{in}$:

| Parameter | Effect of Increasing $N_{in}$ | Justification |
|-----------|-------------------------------|---------------|
| **(i) Propagation delay** | **Increases** (linearly, to a first order) | Each added PMOS in series increases the pull-up on-resistance; each added input adds a parasitic capacitance. |
| **(ii) Power dissipation** | **Increases** (dynamic component rises) | $P_d = C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ where $C_L$ grows with $N_{in}$. |
| **(iii) Noise margin** | **Decreases** | Series PMOS transistors stack up, reducing effective $V_{OH}$ and the HIGH-state noise margin. |

---

## 📗 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Fan-In with Fan-Out.** Fan-In = inputs accepted *by* a gate; Fan-Out = similar gates driven *from* an output. Mixing these up is the single most common cause of losing 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Show the formula with units.** Examiners award partial credit when the formula and its dimensionless nature are stated explicitly.
> 3. **Mention the trade-off triangle** (Fan-In ↔ Speed ↔ Noise Margin) somewhere in the answer — it is a high-yield "free-mark" statement.
> 4. **In derivations, write every algebraic step.** A single skipped step can cost 1 mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Define Fan-In and Fan-Out of a digital logic gate. Compare the typical fan-in values of TTL, CMOS, and ECL logic families, with justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a standard TTL logic family, the following specifications are given:
$I_{IL} = 1.6 \text{ mA}$, $I_{IH} = 40 \text{ µA}$, $I_{OL(max)} = 16 \text{ mA}$, $I_{OH(max)} = 400 \text{ µA}$.

Calculate the **fan-in (input loading)** and the **fan-out (output drive capability)** of the gate. Discuss what happens to the propagation delay if the fan-in of a multi-input TTL NAND gate is increased from 2 to 8. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Fan-In:** The maximum number of input signals that a logic gate can accept at its input terminals.
- **Fan-Out:** The maximum number of similar logic gates (in terms of unit load) that the output of a single gate can reliably drive.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Logic Family | Typical Fan-In | Justification |
|--------------|----------------|---------------|
| TTL | 2 to 8 | Limited by the multi-emitter BJT input structure; going beyond 8 causes excessive leakage and noise margin loss. |
| CMOS | 2 to 4 (practically) | Each input requires a complementary NMOS-PMOS pair; series PMOS stacking in NAND gates degrades pull-up strength. |
| ECL | 2 to 5 | Biasing network and emitter-follower inputs limit fan-in; speed optimization is prioritized over input count. |

**Conclusion (2 marks):** ECL has the smallest fan-in because it sacrifices input count for ultra-high speed. CMOS and TTL are roughly comparable, with CMOS often preferred for low-power applications. Fan-in is universally limited by the trade-off triangle of speed, power, and noise margin.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Fan-In (input loading) — 2 marks:**
Using the LOW-state loading (worst case):
$$
N_{in} = \frac{I_{OL(max)}}{\vert I_{IL} \vert} = \frac{16 \, \text{mA}}{1.6 \, \text{mA}} = 10
$$

Using the HIGH-state loading:
$$
N_{in} = \frac{\vert I_{OH(max)} \vert}{I_{IH}} = \frac{400 \, \mu A}{40 \, \mu A} = 10
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{in} = 10 \, \text{unit loads}}
$$

**Fan-Out (output drive) — 2 marks:** The fan-out is also 10 (same calculation, but applied to the *driving* gate's perspective).

**Propagation Delay Discussion — 3 marks:**
For a multi-input TTL NAND gate, the equivalent input capacitance scales with fan-in:
$$
C_{in,total}(N_{in}=2) = 2 \cdot C_{in}
$$
$$
C_{in,total}(N_{in}=8) = 8 \cdot C_{in}
$$
Hence the capacitance increases by a factor of **4**. Using the first-order RC delay model:
$$
\frac{t_{pd}(N_{in}=8)}{t_{pd}(N_{in}=2)} = \frac{8 \cdot C_{in}}{2 \cdot C_{in}} = 4
$$
So the **propagation delay quadruples** when fan-in grows from 2 to 8. This confirms the linear scaling law:
$$
t_{pd} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{out} \cdot N_{in} \cdot C_{in}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of Fan-In. Discuss how Fan-In influences the three primary static parameters of a logic family: **propagation delay, noise margin, and power dissipation**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CMOS logic gate has a per-input capacitance of $C_{in} = 5 \text{ pF}$ and an equivalent output resistance of $R_{out} = 200 \, \Omega$. If the fan-in is increased from 2 to 6, calculate the **ratio of propagation delays** and the **increase in dynamic power dissipation** (assuming $V_{DD} = 5 \text{ V}$ and clock frequency $f = 10 \text{ MHz}$). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Diagram (2 marks):** A multi-input AND gate with $N_{in}$ inputs converging to a single output $Y$ (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes).

**Concept (2 marks):** Fan-In is the count of inputs a gate can accept. Each input adds an internal transistor pair (CMOS) or emitter (TTL), contributing parasitic capacitance in parallel.

**Effect on Static Parameters (3 marks):**

1. **Propagation Delay:** Increases linearly with $N_{in}$ because $t_{pd} \propto R_{out} \cdot C_{in,total} = R_{out} \cdot N_{in} \cdot C_{in}$.
2. **Noise Margin:** Decreases with $N_{in}$ because internal voltage drops (e.g., series PMOS in CMOS NAND, multi-emitter leakage in TTL) erode both $NM_H$ and $NM_L$.
3. **Power Dissipation:** Dynamic power rises as $P_d = C_{in,total} \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$, and static leakage power also grows with the number of input transistors.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute the ratio of propagation delays (3 marks):**

$$
\frac{t_{pd}(N_{in}=6)}{t_{pd}(N_{in}=2)} = \frac{R_{out} \cdot 6 \cdot C_{in}}{R_{out} \cdot 2 \cdot C_{in}} = \frac{6}{2} = 3
$$

So the propagation delay becomes **3 times larger** when fan-in grows from 2 to 6.

**Step 2 — Compute the total input capacitance for both cases (1 mark):**

$$
C_{in,total}(N_{in}=2) = 2 \times 5 \, \text{pF} = 10 \, \text{pF}
$$
$$
C_{in,total}(N_{in}=6) = 6 \times 5 \, \text{pF} = 30 \, \text{pF}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute dynamic power dissipation for both cases (2 marks):**

$$
P_d = C_{in,total} \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f
$$

For $N_{in}=2$:
$$
P_d(2) = 10 \times 10^{-12} \times (5)^2 \times 10 \times 10^{6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{W} = 2.5 \, \text{mW}
$$

For $N_{in}=6$:
$$
P_d(6) = 30 \times 10^{-12} \times (5)^2 \times 10 \times 10^{6} = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{W} = 7.5 \, \text{mW}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the increase (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta P_d = 7.5 \, \text{mW} - 2.5 \, \text{mW} = 5.0 \, \text{mW}
$$

The power dissipation **increases by 5 mW**, which is **3× the original value** — matching the same 3× ratio we saw in propagation delay.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fan-In = number of inputs a gate accepts**; **Fan-Out = number of gates an output can drive**. Never confuse them.
- Fan-In is a **dimensionless integer** (count of inputs), but the *loading* fan-in is computed as a **current ratio**.
- Each additional input adds **parasitic capacitance in parallel** → directly inflates propagation delay.
- The **trade-off triangle**: Fan-In ↔ Speed ↔ Noise Margin — increasing one degrades the others.
- **TTL fan-in** is constrained by the **multi-emitter BJT** structure (typically 2, 3, 4, or 8).
- **CMOS fan-in** is constrained by **series PMOS stacking** in NAND gates (practically ≤ 4 for speed).
- **ECL fan-in** is small (2–5) because the family prioritizes **speed** over input count.
- Standard TTL unit load: $I_{IL} = 1.6 \, \text{mA}$, $I_{IH} = 40 \, \mu A$, giving **10 UL** per output.
- Propagation delay scales as $t_{pd} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{out} \cdot N_{in} \cdot C_{in}$ (first-order linear).
- Dynamic power scales as $P_d = C_{in,total} \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ (also linear in $N_{in}$).
- Noise margin **shrinks** with fan-in because of accumulated internal voltage drops and leakage.
- Fan-in is a **static parameter** of the logic family; it is **fixed at design time** and cannot be increased without modifying the IC.
- For KTU 14-mark questions, always draw a **diagram**, write **formulas with units**, and mention the **trade-off triangle** for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fan-Out
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT304 - DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fan-Out

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fan-Out** is a fundamental static and dynamic electrical specification of a digital logic family that quantifies the maximum number of **identical logic gate inputs** that a single logic gate output can reliably drive, while still maintaining the guaranteed logic-level voltage thresholds (VIH, VIL, VOH, VOL) and meeting the specified propagation delay (tpd) under worst-case DC and AC loading conditions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBBMT304 – Digital Electronics, Module 4: Logic Families & Interfacing), Fan-Out is classified into two distinct operational categories:

- **DC Fan-Out (Static Fan-Out):** Determined by the current sourcing (HIGH state) and current sinking (LOW state) capabilities of the driving output versus the current demands of the driven inputs.
- **AC Fan-Out (Dynamic Fan-Out):** Determined by the switching speed (propagation delay) degradation caused by the cumulative load capacitance of multiple driven inputs and any interconnect (trace) capacitance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Terminology:** Always use the phrase *"Number of standard loads that can be driven"* when describing Fan-Out in exam answers. Never refer to it as a "multiplier" or "gain" — these are analog terminology and are strictly penalized.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy: The "Water Tap & Buckets" Intuition

Imagine a single **water tap (logic gate output)** connected to a long pipeline that must fill several **buckets (logic gate inputs)** placed downstream.

- Each bucket needs a fixed minimum amount of water (logic current **IIL** in LOW state or **IIH** in HIGH state) to be considered "full" (i.e., the next logic stage recognizes a valid logic level).
- The tap has a maximum discharge rate — it can only push out **IOL** in the LOW state and **IOL** corresponds to sinking current, while in HIGH state it can only source **IOH** before the water pressure (voltage) drops below acceptable limits.
- If you connect too many buckets, the tap cannot fill them all fast enough, and the water level (voltage) at the bucket inlets will fall below the "full" threshold, causing logic errors.
- The maximum number of buckets that can be filled simultaneously **without any bucket going below its required level** is the **Fan-Out**.

This analogy directly maps to the electrical reality:
- **Tap pressure drop** → Logic HIGH voltage drop below VOH(min)
- **Tap suction limit** → Logic LOW voltage rise above VOL(max)
- **Bucket fill level** → Input recognition threshold (VIH, VIL)

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Term | Symbol | Standard Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard TTL Unit Load (UL)** | 1 UL | **40 µA** for HIGH input, **1.6 mA** for LOW input |
| **CMOS Input Leakage (typical)** | IIH, IIL | **±1 µA** (negligible DC loading) |
| **TTL Output Current (sinking)** | IOL | **16 mA** (for 74LS series) |
| **TTL Output Current (sourcing)** | IOH | **−400 µA** (negative sign = sourcing) |

> [!NOTE]
> In CMOS families, Fan-Out is theoretically limited **only by capacitive loading** (AC Fan-Out), not by DC current, because CMOS gate inputs draw only leakage-level current (typically in nanoamperes).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Fan-Out Logic

The Fan-Out of a logic gate is governed by **two independent constraints** that must *both* be satisfied. The KTU examiner awards full marks only when both conditions are demonstrated in a numerical problem.

### 2.1.1 Constraint #1: HIGH-State (Source) Loading

When the driving output is in the **logic HIGH** state, it must source current to all the inputs tied to it. Each input draws a small current (IIH) out of the driving gate's output transistor.

- If the total current demanded by all the driven inputs exceeds **IOH (max source current)**, the output voltage collapses below **VOH (min)**.
- Result: The driven gates no longer see a valid logic HIGH, leading to **false triggering** or **logic race conditions**.

### 2.1.2 Constraint #2: LOW-State (Sink) Loading

When the driving output is in the **logic LOW** state, it must sink the current flowing out of every driven input (IIL flows *into* each driven input and *into* the driving output's sink transistor).

- If the total current to be sunk exceeds **IOL (max sink current)**, the output voltage rises above **VOL (max)**.
- Result: The driven gates may interpret the LOW as an indeterminate or HIGH level, causing **logic failure**.

### 2.1.3 The "Minimum Rule" in Fan-Out Computation

The KTU 2024 board examiner expects the student to compute **both** ratios and select the **smaller** of the two:

$$\text{Fan-Out} = \min \left( \frac{I_{OH}}{\vert I_{IH} \vert},\; \frac{I_{OL}}{\vert I_{IL} \vert} \right)$$

The smaller ratio is the **bottleneck** and dictates the actual usable Fan-Out.

> [!TIP]
> **Why minimum?** Because the driving gate is a single physical device. If it can only drive 10 gates in the HIGH state but 8 gates in the LOW state, then attaching 10 gates would cause the LOW-state condition to fail first. Therefore, the **worst-case** loading in *either* state must be respected.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Variable Definitions | Typical Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $FO_{DC} = \min \left( \dfrac{I_{OH}}{\vert I_{IH} \vert},\; \dfrac{I_{OL}}{\vert I_{IL} \vert} \right)$ | IOH = source current, IIH = HIGH input current, IOL = sink current, IIL = LOW input current | mA / mA = dimensionless | Calculate Fan-Out for TTL NAND/NOR gates |
| 2 | $FO_{AC} = \dfrac{t_{PD(\max)} - t_{PD(\text{intrinsic})}}{C_{in} \cdot \Delta V / I_{O(\max)}}$ | tPD = propagation delay, Cin = input capacitance, IO = output drive | Pure number | Used when capacitive loading dominates (CMOS) |
| 3 | $C_{L(\text{total})} = N \cdot C_{in} + C_{\text{wire}}$ | N = number of driven gates, Cin = per-gate input capacitance, Cwire = interconnect | pF | AC loading in CMOS |
| 4 | $FO_{\text{CMOS}} = \dfrac{t_{PD(\max)} - t_{PD(\text{gate})}}{0.69 \cdot R_{O} \cdot C_{L(\text{per gate})}}$ | RO = output resistance of CMOS driver | Pure number | CMOS Fan-Out with RC delay model |
| 5 | $I_{IL} = -1.6\;\text{mA},\; I_{IH} = 40\;\mu\text{A}$ | Standard TTL unit load definition | mA, µA | 74LS, 74AS, 74ALS series |
| 6 | $I_{OL} = 16\;\text{mA},\; I_{OH} = -400\;\mu\text{A}$ | Standard 74LS output drive | mA, µA | 74LS00 family |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern VLSI and PCB design, Fan-Out dictates critical design decisions:

- **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS):** A single clock buffer (e.g., in an FPGA or ASIC) must drive thousands of flip-flops. High-Fan-Out buffers are inserted at strategic nodes.
- **Bus Driver Selection:** A microprocessor's address/data bus must drive multiple memory chips, peripherals, and bus transceivers — Fan-Out determines whether a buffer is needed.
- **PCB Trace Routing:** Long traces add parasitic capacitance (Cwire), which reduces AC Fan-Out. Fan-Out calculations must include this Cwire term in production designs.
- **Logic Family Migration:** When migrating from TTL (74LS) to CMOS (74HC), designers must re-verify Fan-Out because the DC loading profile changes from milliamps to microamps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently frame a 7-mark problem as: *"A 74LS00 NAND gate drives 12 identical 74LS00 inputs. Determine whether the gate is operating within its Fan-Out specification."* The student must show **both** ratios explicitly before concluding.

---

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem: DC Fan-Out Calculation for 74LS00 (NAND Gate)

**Given (74LS00 standard specifications from Texas Instruments datasheet):**
- $I_{OH} = -400\;\mu\text{A}$ (the negative sign indicates the gate is **sourcing** current)
- $I_{OL} = 8\;\text{mA}$ (the gate is **sinking** current)
- $I_{IH} = 20\;\mu\text{A}$ (each driven input draws 20 µA in HIGH state)
- $I_{IL} = -0.4\;\text{mA}$ (each driven input sources 0.4 mA into the driving output in LOW state)

**Find:** The DC Fan-Out of this 74LS00 NAND gate.

---

### Step 1: Compute the HIGH-State (Source) Fan-Out Ratio

The driving output can source at most 400 µA. Each driven input consumes 20 µA when in HIGH state.

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{HIGH} &= \frac{I_{OH}}{\vert I_{IH} \vert} \\[6pt]
N_{HIGH} &= \frac{400\;\mu\text{A}}{20\;\mu\text{A}} \\[6pt]
N_{HIGH} &= 20
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The gate can support **20** identical inputs in the HIGH state before its output voltage sags below VOH(min).

---

### Step 2: Compute the LOW-State (Sink) Fan-Out Ratio

The driving output can sink at most 8 mA. Each driven input sources 0.4 mA into the driving output in LOW state.

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{LOW} &= \frac{I_{OL}}{\vert I_{IL} \vert} \\[6pt]
N_{LOW} &= \frac{8\;\text{mA}}{0.4\;\text{mA}} \\[6pt]
N_{LOW} &= 20
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The gate can support **20** identical inputs in the LOW state before its output voltage rises above VOL(max).

---

### Step 3: Take the Worst-Case (Minimum) of Both Ratios

$$\begin{aligned}
FO_{DC} &= \min(N_{HIGH},\; N_{LOW}) \\[6pt]
FO_{DC} &= \min(20,\; 20) \\[6pt]
FO_{DC} &= 20
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The 74LS00 NAND gate has a **DC Fan-Out of 20**.

---

## 3.2 Problem: Verifying Design Feasibility (14-Mark Exam Style)

**Scenario:** A digital control system uses a single 74LS04 (Hex Inverter) output to drive **15** identical inputs of various TTL sub-circuits. Each sub-circuit presents a load equivalent to **2 unit loads (UL)** in the LOW state and **1 UL** in the HIGH state. (Recall: 1 UL = 1.6 mA sink / 40 µA source in classic 74 series TTL.)

**Given:**
- Driving gate: 74LS04
- $I_{OL} = 8\;\text{mA}$, $I_{OH} = -400\;\mu\text{A}$
- Number of driven gates, N = 15
- Load per gate: 2 UL in LOW, 1 UL in HIGH

---

### Step 4: Convert Loads to Absolute Currents

**HIGH state load per gate:**
$$\begin{aligned}
I_{IH} &= 1\;\text{UL} \times 40\;\mu\text{A/UL} = 40\;\mu\text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

**LOW state load per gate:**
$$\begin{aligned}
I_{IL} &= 2\;\text{UL} \times 1.6\;\text{mA/UL} = 3.2\;\text{mA}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Step 5: Total Current Demanded by 15 Gates

**Total HIGH-state current demanded:**
$$\begin{aligned}
I_{IH(\text{total})} &= N \times I_{IH} = 15 \times 40\;\mu\text{A} = 600\;\mu\text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Total LOW-state current demanded:**
$$\begin{aligned}
I_{IL(\text{total})} &= N \times I_{IL} = 15 \times 3.2\;\text{mA} = 48\;\text{mA}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Step 6: Compare with Driving Gate's Capability

**HIGH state check:**
$$\begin{aligned}
I_{IH(\text{total})} \stackrel{?}{\le} \vert I_{OH} \vert \\
600\;\mu\text{A} \le 400\;\mu\text{A} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{FALSE}
\end{aligned}$$

→ The driving gate **CANNOT** source enough current. **HIGH-state loading fails.**

**LOW state check:**
$$\begin{aligned}
I_{IL(\text{total})} \stackrel{?}{\le} I_{OL} \\
48\;\text{mA} \le 8\;\text{mA} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{FALSE}
\end{aligned}$$

→ The driving gate **CANNOT** sink enough current. **LOW-state loading also fails.**

---

### Step 7: Compute Maximum Allowable Number of Gates

**Allowed by HIGH state:**
$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\max,HIGH} &= \frac{400\;\mu\text{A}}{40\;\mu\text{A}} = 10\;\text{gates}
\end{aligned}$$

**Allowed by LOW state:**
$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\max,LOW} &= \frac{8\;\text{mA}}{3.2\;\text{mA}} = 2.5 \rightarrow 2\;\text{gates (floor value)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Bottleneck:**
$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\max} &= \min(10,\; 2) = 2\;\text{gates}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Verdict:** The design with 15 gates is **NOT feasible**. The designer must either:
1. Insert a **buffer** (e.g., 74LS244) to split the load into smaller fan-out groups, or
2. Replace the driving gate with a **higher-drive** part (e.g., 74AS04 or 74ALS04).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Design Parameter | Symbolic Limit | Computed Value | KTU Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| DC Fan-Out (HIGH state) | $\dfrac{I_{OH}}{\vert I_{IH} \vert}$ | 10 | Marginal |
| DC Fan-Out (LOW state) | $\dfrac{I_{OL}}{\vert I_{IL} \vert}$ | 2 | **Bottleneck** |
| Composite DC Fan-Out | $\min(N_{HIGH}, N_{LOW})$ | 2 | **Critical design limit** |
| AC Fan-Out (assume $C_{in} = 5\;\text{pF}$, $C_{wire} = 25\;\text{pF}$) | $\dfrac{t_{PD(\max)}}{0.69 \cdot R_O \cdot C_{L(\text{per gate})}}$ | ≈ 25 | Sufficient |
| **Overall Fan-Out** | $\min(FO_{DC}, FO_{AC})$ | **2** | **DC-limited design** |

> [!NOTE]
> In **pure CMOS** logic (e.g., 74HC, 74HCT, 74AHC), DC Fan-Out is practically **infinite** (>50), but AC Fan-Out is bounded by RC delay. The KTU board frequently tests this distinction by giving a "mixed TTL + CMOS" problem.

---

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Fan-Out Feasibility Check

The following Mermaid flow chart shows the standard KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation algorithm a student must apply when solving a Fan-Out design problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Design has 1 Driver + N Loads]) --> Step1[Compute IOH and IOL of Driver Gate]
    Step1 --> Step2[Compute IIH and IIL of Each Load Gate]
    Step2 --> Step3[Multiply Load Current by N to Get Total Demand]
    Step3 --> Step4{Is IOH_total less than or equal to IOH driver?}
    Step4 -- Yes --> CheckLow{High-state check passed: proceed}
    Step4 -- No --> Fail1[FAILURE: Insert Buffer or Use Higher-Drive Gate]
    CheckLow --> Step5{Is IIL_total less than or equal to IOL driver?}
    Step5 -- Yes --> Pass[BOTH CHECKS PASSED: Design is Feasible]
    Step5 -- No --> Fail2[FAILURE: LOW-state overload detected]
    Fail1 --> End([End: Redesign Required])
    Fail2 --> End
    Pass --> End

    classDef pass fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class Fail1,Fail2 fail

```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fan-Out Mitigation Strategies

When a single driving gate cannot support the required load, professional VLSI/PCB designers use one of the following architecture patterns.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STRATEGY_A[STRATEGY A: BUFFER INSERTION]
        Src1[Source Gate] --> Buf1[Buffer Gate 1]
        Src1 --> Buf2[Buffer Gate 2]
        Buf1 --> LoadGroupA[Load Group A: 5 Gates]
        Buf2 --> LoadGroupB[Load Group B: 5 Gates]
    end

    subgraph STRATEGY_B[STRATEGY B: HIGHER-DRIVE GATE REPLACEMENT]
        Src2[74LS00 Driver] -->|REPLACE WITH| Driver2[74ALS00 or 74AS00]
        Driver2 --> LoadGroupC[Combined Load: 10 Gates]
    end

    subgraph STRATEGY_C[STRATEGY C: CMOS SUBSTITUTION]
        Src3[Original TTL] -->|REPLACE WITH| Driver3[74HCT or 74AHC CMOS]
        Driver3 --> LoadGroupD[High-Capacitance CMOS Load: 50 Gates DC-limited only by Leakage]
    end

    classDef strategyBox fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gate fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    class STRATEGY_A,STRATEGY_B,STRATEGY_C strategyBox
    class Src1,Src2,Src3,Buf1,Buf2,Driver2,Driver3 gate
    class LoadGroupA,LoadGroupB,LoadGroupC,LoadGroupD load
```

## 4.4 Current-Path Schematic (HIGH vs LOW States)

The following diagram shows the *internal* transistor current flow direction for a totem-pole TTL output stage, illustrating why both source and sink limits exist.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Q1: Upper Output Transistor Sourcing IOH]
    Q2[Q2: Lower Output Transistor Sinking IOL]
    VCCNODE[VCC]
    GNDNODE[GND]
    OutNode[OUTPUT NODE]

    VCCNODE --- Q1
    Q1 ===|IOH source| OutNode
    OutNode ===|IOL sink| Q2
    Q2 --- GNDNODE

    classDef upper fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lower fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rail fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2.5px,color:#000
    class Q1 upper
    class Q2 lower
    class VCCNODE,GNDNODE rail
    class OutNode node
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **TTL totem-pole output**, the upper transistor (Q1, typically a Darlington pair) limits the **IOH (source) current**, and the lower transistor (Q2) limits the **IOL (sink) current**. The Fan-Out bottleneck can be in *either* state depending on which transistor is weaker.
