# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Fan-Out. Differentiate between DC Fan-Out and AC Fan-Out.**

**Model Answer:**

**Fan-Out** is the maximum number of standard logic inputs that a single logic gate output can reliably drive without degrading its output voltage levels (VOH, VOL) beyond the specified limits.

| Parameter | DC Fan-Out | AC Fan-Out |
|---|---|---|
| **Cause of limitation** | Static current sourcing/sinking capability of the output stage | Switching speed degradation due to capacitive loading |
| **Governed by** | $I_{OH},\; I_{OL},\; I_{IH},\; I_{IL}$ | $C_{in}$ (input capacitance), $t_{PD(\max)}$ (max propagation delay) |
| **Dominant in** | TTL families (74LS, 74AS, 74ALS) | CMOS families (74HC, 74HCT, 74AHC) |
| **Formula** | $FO_{DC} = \min \left( \dfrac{I_{OH}}{\vert I_{IH} \vert},\; \dfrac{I_{OL}}{\vert I_{IL} \vert} \right)$ | $FO_{AC} = \dfrac{t_{PD(\max)} - t_{PD(intrinsic)}}{0.69 \cdot R_O \cdot C_{in}}$ |
| **Typical value (TTL)** | 10–20 | 25–50 |
| **Typical value (CMOS)** | >50 (practically infinite) | 20–50 (capacitively limited) |

**(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for differentiation table.)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is the Fan-Out of a CMOS gate higher than that of a TTL gate?**

**Model Answer:**

The Fan-Out of a CMOS gate is significantly higher (typically >50) than that of a TTL gate (typically 10–20) for the following reasons:

1. **Negligible DC Input Current:** CMOS gate inputs are connected to the gate terminal of MOSFETs, which are insulated by a thin SiO₂ layer. As a result, the input draws only **leakage current** (in nanoamperes), whereas TTL inputs require **1.6 mA in LOW state and 40 µA in HIGH state** to bias the input transistor.

2. **Very Low Static Power:** CMOS gates consume power only during switching (dynamic power), so the output stage is not thermally limited.

3. **DC Loading is Negligible:** With $I_{IH} \approx I_{IL} \approx \pm 1\;\mu\text{A}$, the DC Fan-Out ratio $\dfrac{I_{OH}}{I_{IH}}$ approaches **infinity** in theory.

4. **AC Loading Still Applies:** However, each CMOS input adds a **gate capacitance** of 3–10 pF, which slows the transition time. This makes CMOS Fan-Out **AC-limited**, not DC-limited.

**(3 marks: 1 mark per significant reason, 1 mark for the AC caveat.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Internal Choice Option 1)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of **Fan-Out** in digital logic families. Discuss the role of **IOH, IOL, IIH,** and **IIL** in determining the Fan-Out of a TTL gate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 74LS00 NAND gate has the following specifications: **IOH = −400 µA, IOL = 8 mA, IIH = 20 µA, IIL = −0.4 mA**. Calculate its DC Fan-Out. If this gate is used to drive **18 identical 74LS00 inputs**, determine whether the design is feasible. Suggest a suitable modification if it is not. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Definition of Fan-Out:** *(1 mark)*

Fan-Out is the maximum number of standard inputs that can be connected to a single output of a logic gate while the output still maintains its specified HIGH and LOW voltage levels.

**Block Diagram Description:** *(2 marks)*

A driving gate output is connected to N driven gate inputs. In the **HIGH state**, current $I_{OH}$ flows from the driving gate output into each driven input (each consuming $I_{IH}$). In the **LOW state**, current $I_{IL}$ flows from each driven input into the driving gate output (which sinks a total of $I_{OL}$).

**Role of Each Current Parameter:** *(3 marks)*

| Current | Direction | Role in Fan-Out |
|---|---|---|
| $I_{OH}$ | Flows **out** of the driving gate in HIGH state | Maximum current the gate can source; limits HIGH-state fan-out |
| $I_{OL}$ | Flows **into** the driving gate in LOW state | Maximum current the gate can sink; limits LOW-state fan-out |
| $I_{IH}$ | Flows **into** each driven gate in HIGH state | Current load per input in HIGH state |
| $I_{IL}$ | Flows **out of** each driven gate in LOW state | Current load per input in LOW state |

**Formula and Bottleneck Concept:** *(1 mark)*

$$FO_{DC} = \min\left(\dfrac{I_{OH}}{\vert I_{IH} \vert},\; \dfrac{I_{OL}}{\vert I_{IL} \vert}\right)$$

The smaller of the two ratios determines the usable Fan-Out.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Step 1: Calculate the HIGH-state Fan-Out ratio.** *(2 marks)*

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{HIGH} &= \frac{\vert I_{OH} \vert}{\vert I_{IH} \vert} = \frac{400\;\mu\text{A}}{20\;\mu\text{A}} = 20\;\text{gates}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the LOW-state Fan-Out ratio.** *(2 marks)*

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{LOW} &= \frac{I_{OL}}{\vert I_{IL} \vert} = \frac{8\;\text{mA}}{0.4\;\text{mA}} = 20\;\text{gates}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Composite DC Fan-Out.** *(1 mark)*

$$\begin{aligned}
FO_{DC} = \min(20,\; 20) = 20
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Check feasibility for N = 18.** *(1 mark)*

Since $N = 18 < FO_{DC} = 20$, the design is **feasible** under static DC loading conditions.

**Step 5: Modification comment.** *(1 mark)*

If the design had required **>20 gates**, a 74ALS00 (with IOL = 8 mA, IOH = −400 µA but lower input loading) or a 74AS00 (high-drive) would be recommended. Alternatively, a buffer such as the 74LS244 could be inserted to split the load.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Option 2)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **AC Fan-Out**. Why is it the dominant constraint in CMOS logic families? Derive the expression for AC Fan-Out in terms of load capacitance and propagation delay. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CMOS inverter (74HC04) has an output resistance of **200 Ω** in the LOW state and a maximum allowable propagation delay of **15 ns**. The intrinsic (unloaded) delay of the gate is **5 ns**, and each input of the same family presents a capacitance of **7 pF**. Determine the **AC Fan-Out** of the gate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Definition of AC Fan-Out:** *(1 mark)*

AC Fan-Out is the maximum number of gate inputs that can be driven by a single output while ensuring the propagation delay does not exceed the manufacturer's specification. It is determined by the **RC time constant** of the output stage charging and discharging the cumulative load capacitance.

**Why AC Dominates in CMOS:** *(2 marks)*

1. CMOS gate inputs draw negligible DC current, so DC Fan-Out is essentially unlimited.
2. However, each CMOS input has a **gate capacitance** (typically 3–10 pF) of the MOSFET gate.
3. When N such capacitances are connected in parallel to a single output, the total load capacitance $C_L = N \cdot C_{in}$ increases.
4. Since the output stage has a finite drive current, the **rise/fall time** (and hence propagation delay) scales with $C_L$.
5. Therefore, the **speed degradation** is what limits the number of CMOS inputs that can be safely connected.

**Derivation of AC Fan-Out:** *(4 marks)*

The propagation delay of a CMOS gate driving a load capacitance $C_L$ is modeled as:

$$t_{PD} = t_{PD(\text{intrinsic})} + 0.69 \cdot R_O \cdot C_L$$

where $R_O$ is the equivalent output resistance and $C_L = N \cdot C_{in}$ for N identical driven gates.

For the delay to remain within $t_{PD(\max)}$:

$$t_{PD(\max)} = t_{PD(\text{intrinsic})} + 0.69 \cdot R_O \cdot (N \cdot C_{in})$$

Solving for N:

$$\begin{aligned}
N \cdot C_{in} &= \frac{t_{PD(\max)} - t_{PD(\text{intrinsic})}}{0.69 \cdot R_O} \\[6pt]
N &= \frac{t_{PD(\max)} - t_{PD(\text{intrinsic})}}{0.69 \cdot R_O \cdot C_{in}}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$FO_{AC} = \frac{t_{PD(\max)} - t_{PD(\text{intrinsic})}}{0.69 \cdot R_O \cdot C_{in}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given:**
- $R_O = 200\;\Omega$
- $t_{PD(\max)} = 15\;\text{ns}$
- $t_{PD(\text{intrinsic})} = 5\;\text{ns}$
- $C_{in} = 7\;\text{pF}$

**Step 1: Substitute into the AC Fan-Out formula.** *(3 marks)*

$$\begin{aligned}
FO_{AC} &= \frac{t_{PD(\max)} - t_{PD(\text{intrinsic})}}{0.69 \cdot R_O \cdot C_{in}} \\[6pt]
FO_{AC} &= \frac{15\;\text{ns} - 5\;\text{ns}}{0.69 \times 200\;\Omega \times 7\;\text{pF}} \\[6pt]
FO_{AC} &= \frac{10 \times 10^{-9}}{0.69 \times 200 \times 7 \times 10^{-12}} \\[6pt]
FO_{AC} &= \frac{10 \times 10^{-9}}{9.66 \times 10^{-10}} \\[6pt]
FO_{AC} &= 10.35
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Round down to nearest integer.** *(1 mark)*

Since Fan-Out must be a **whole number of gates**, we take the **floor**:

$$FO_{AC} = 10\;\text{gates}$$

**Step 3: Comment.** *(1 mark)*

The 74HC04 inverter can reliably drive **10** identical 74HC04 inputs while staying within the 15 ns propagation delay budget. This relatively low AC Fan-Out (compared to the near-infinite DC Fan-Out) confirms that CMOS Fan-Out is **capacitively limited**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on Fan-Out Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value signs:** $I_{OH}$ is conventionally negative (sourcing), and $I_{IL}$ is conventionally negative (flowing out of the input). If you do not use $\vert I_{OH} \vert$ and $\vert I_{IL} \vert$ in the formula, your ratio will be **negative** and you will be marked wrong.
>
> 2. **Not taking the minimum of both ratios:** A common mistake is to report $N_{HIGH}$ *and* $N_{LOW}$ and stop. KTU specifically tests the **bottleneck identification** (min operation). Always end with: $FO = \min(N_{HIGH}, N_{LOW})$.
>
> 3. **Confusing IIL with the input sourcing current:** Students often write $I_{IL} = +1.6\;\text{mA}$ instead of $-1.6\;\text{mA}$. The sign convention matters.
>
> 4. **Ignoring AC Fan-Out in CMOS problems:** If a problem specifies CMOS gates and a propagation delay budget, you **must** compute AC Fan-Out. Reporting only the DC Fan-Out (which is huge) will result in zero credit for the speed analysis.
>
> 5. **Not suggesting a design fix:** When a Fan-Out is exceeded, the model answer always includes a mitigation: buffer insertion, gate replacement, or load splitting. Skipping this loses the "design thinking" marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Final-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ✅ **Fan-Out = Number of standard inputs a single output can drive reliably.**
- ✅ **Two flavors:** DC Fan-Out (current-limited) and AC Fan-Out (capacitance-limited).
- ✅ **Master formula:** $FO_{DC} = \min \left( \dfrac{I_{OH}}{\vert I_{IH} \vert},\; \dfrac{I_{OL}}{\vert I_{IL} \vert} \right)$.
- ✅ **Always take the minimum** of the HIGH-state and LOW-state ratios.
- ✅ **TTL is DC-limited** (typical FO = 10–20).
- ✅ **CMOS is AC-limited** (DC FO > 50, AC FO ≈ 20–50 depending on $C_{in}$).
- ✅ **Unit Load (UL):** 40 µA in HIGH, 1.6 mA in LOW (standard 74 series).
- ✅ **74LS standard values:** $I_{OH} = -400\;\mu\text{A}$, $I_{OL} = 8\;\text{mA}$, $I_{IH} = 20\;\mu\text{A}$, $I_{IL} = -0.4\;\text{mA}$.
- ✅ **AC Fan-Out formula:** $FO_{AC} = \dfrac{t_{PD(\max)} - t_{PD(\text{intrinsic})}}{0.69 \cdot R_O \cdot C_{in}}$.
- ✅ **Capacitive load is additive:** $C_L = N \cdot C_{in} + C_{wire}$.
- ✅ **Mitigation strategies:** Buffer insertion (74LS244), higher-drive gate replacement (74ALS, 74AS), or migration to CMOS (74HCT).
- ✅ **Sign convention:** $I_{OH}$ and $I_{IL}$ are negative by convention; use absolute values in ratios.
- ✅ **Designer rule of thumb:** Never exceed **80% of rated Fan-Out** in production designs to account for temperature, voltage, and aging derating.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT304 - DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Digital Electronics, **Speed** is formally defined as the *rate at which a digital logic gate or circuit can respond to a change in its input and produce a stable, valid output*. It is quantitatively measured by the **Propagation Delay** ($t_{pd}$), which is the time interval between the 50% transition point of the input pulse and the corresponding 50% transition point of the output pulse.

Mathematically, the speed of a logic gate is the reciprocal of its average propagation delay:

$$\text{Speed} \propto \frac{1}{t_{pd}}$$

Where $t_{pd}$ is the **average propagation delay** defined as:

$$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2}$$

Here, $t_{pHL}$ is the **High-to-Low propagation delay** and $t_{pLH}$ is the **Low-to-High propagation delay**, both measured at the 50% voltage level of the input/output waveforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The standard unit of speed-related time parameters is **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)**. Faster logic families (like ECL) operate in the range of **0.1 ns to 1 ns**, while standard CMOS operates at **5 ns to 50 ns**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two **sliding doors** at a supermarket entrance:
- **Door A (Slow / CMOS):** Heavy, takes a long time to fully open after you press the button. It conserves battery (low power) but is sluggish.
- **Door B (Fast / ECL):** Lightweight, spring-loaded, and snaps open almost instantly. It uses more power but responds with lightning speed.

In digital electronics, a **logic gate** is the door, the **input signal change** is the button press, and the **time taken for the output to switch** is the propagation delay. The faster the gate switches, the higher its **speed** rating. This speed is critical in:
- High-frequency microprocessors
- Real-time signal processing
- High-speed communication protocols (USB, HDMI, PCIe)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - Standard TTL propagation delay: **10 ns**
> - Standard CMOS propagation delay: **10 ns to 50 ns** (older) / **< 1 ns** (modern nanometer CMOS)
> - ECL (Emitter-Coupled Logic) propagation delay: **0.2 ns to 1 ns**
> - Maximum toggling frequency of a gate: $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$

## 1.3 Why "Speed" is a Critical Performance Parameter

In any synchronous digital system, a clock signal orchestrates the flow of data. If the gates are too slow, the output of one stage won't stabilize before the next clock edge arrives, leading to:
- **Setup time violations**
- **Metastability**
- **System failure (race conditions)**

Hence, **speed dictates the maximum operating frequency** of the entire digital system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Switching Speed

The switching speed of a digital gate is governed by **two fundamental time parameters** that determine how quickly the output can transition between logic states:

### A. Rise Time ($t_r$)
The time required for the output to transition from **10% to 90%** of its final logic level (typically from $0.1 V_{OH}$ to $0.9 V_{OH}$).

### B. Fall Time ($t_f$)
The time required for the output to transition from **90% to 10%** of its logic level (from $0.9 V_{OH}$ to $0.1 V_{OH}$).

> [!NOTE]
> Rise and fall times are influenced by the **RC time constant** at the output node: $t_r \approx 2.2 \cdot R_{out} \cdot C_L$, where $R_{out}$ is the effective output resistance and $C_L$ is the load capacitance.

### C. Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$)
The time between the **50% transition of the input** and the **50% transition of the output**. This is the most commonly used metric for speed comparison.

## 2.2 Logic Levels & The 50% Measurement Point

The 50% measurement point is chosen because:
- It avoids the non-linear regions near $V_{IL}$ and $V_{OH}$
- It provides a consistent reference regardless of logic family
- It accounts for asymmetric rise/fall behavior

$$V_{50\%} = \frac{V_{IH} + V_{IL}}{2}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Propagation Delay (Avg) | $t_{pd}$ | $t_{pd} = (t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}) / 2$ | ns | Average switching time |
| High-to-Low Delay | $t_{pHL}$ | Time from $V_{in}=50\%$ to $V_{out}=50\%$ (falling) | ns | Turn-OFF delay |
| Low-to-High Delay | $t_{pLH}$ | Time from $V_{in}=50\%$ to $V_{out}=50\%$ (rising) | ns | Turn-ON delay |
| Rise Time | $t_r$ | Time from $10\%$ to $90\%$ of output | ns | Charging time |
| Fall Time | $t_f$ | Time from $90\%$ to $10\%$ of output | ns | Discharging time |
| Maximum Frequency | $f_{max}$ | $f_{max} = 1 / (2 \cdot t_{pd})$ | Hz, MHz, GHz | Highest clock rate |
| Speed-Power Product | $SPP$ | $SPP = t_{pd} \times P_D$ | pJ (picojoules) | Figure of Merit |
| RC Charging Limit | $\tau$ | $\tau = R_{out} \cdot C_L$ | ns | Time constant |
| Transition Frequency | $f_T$ | $f_T = g_m / (2\pi C_{be})$ | GHz | BJT unity-gain freq |
| Gate Delay Equation | $t_{pd}$ | $t_{pd} \approx 0.69 R_{eq} C_L$ | ns | Empirical formula |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Note:** The **Speed-Power Product (SPP)** is a *figure of merit*. A *low SPP* indicates a balanced gate (good speed AND low power). A *high SPP* indicates either slow operation or excessive power consumption. For modern VLSI design, SPP minimization is the primary goal.

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Switching Speed

The speed of a digital gate is limited by **physical and electrical constraints**:

1. **Internal Parasitic Capacitances:** $C_{bc}$, $C_{be}$ (BJT), $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$ (MOSFET) — these must charge/discharge during switching.
2. **Load Capacitance ($C_L$):** External wiring + input capacitances of fan-out gates.
3. **Output Resistance ($R_{out}$):** Determined by transistor sizing and topology.
4. **Transistor Transit Time ($\tau_T$):** Time for carriers to cross the base/channel.
5. **Power Supply Voltage ($V_{CC}$ / $V_{DD}$):** Higher voltage = faster charging but more power.
6. **Logic Family Architecture:** Saturated (TTL) vs. Non-saturated (ECL) logic.
7. **Temperature:** Higher temperature generally *decreases* speed (increases $t_{pd}$).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microprocessor Design:** CPU clock speed is directly limited by $t_{pd}$ of the slowest gate in the critical path.
- **FPGA/ASIC Timing Closure:** Static Timing Analysis (STA) tools measure $t_{pd}$ to verify timing constraints.
- **High-Speed Communication:** Serializer/Deserializer (SerDes) blocks need sub-nanosecond switching.
- **Memory Access Time:** RAM speed is a manifestation of internal gate propagation delays.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT):** Real-time image reconstruction requires GHz-speed digital logic.

## 2.6 Speed Comparison Across Logic Families

| **Logic Family** | **Typical $t_{pd}$** | **Speed Rating** | **Power Dissipation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard TTL (74xx) | 10 ns | Medium | 10 mW |
| Schottky TTL (74Sxx) | 3 ns | Fast | 20 mW |
| Low-Power Schottky (74LSxx) | 9 ns | Medium-Slow | 2 mW |
| Advanced Schottky (74ASxx) | 1.5 ns | Very Fast | 20 mW |
| Fast TTL (74Fxx) | 2 ns | Very Fast | 5 mW |
| Standard CMOS (4000 series) | 50 ns | Slow | 0.01 mW (static) |
| HC-CMOS (74HCxx) | 8 ns | Medium | 0.5 mW |
| ECL (10K series) | 2 ns | Very Fast | 25 mW |
| ECL (100K series) | 0.75 ns | Ultra-Fast | 40 mW |
| GaAs MESFET | 0.1 ns | Extreme | 100 mW |

> [!NOTE]
> **ECL (Emitter-Coupled Logic)** is the fastest because it operates in the **non-saturated region**, avoiding the storage time delay caused by minority carrier recombination in saturated BJTs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Propagation Delay from Asymmetric Transitions

### Problem Statement
A TTL NAND gate exhibits the following measured timing characteristics:
- Low-to-High delay: $t_{pLH} = 12$ ns
- High-to-Low delay: $t_{pHL} = 8$ ns
- Load capacitance: $C_L = 15$ pF
- Output resistance (charging): $R_{p} = 1.2$ k$\Omega$
- Output resistance (discharging): $R_{n} = 800$ $\Omega$

Compute the **average propagation delay** $t_{pd}$ and the **maximum operating frequency** $f_{max}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the definition of average propagation delay**

The average propagation delay is the arithmetic mean of the two directional delays:

$$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the measured values**

Substituting $t_{pLH} = 12$ ns and $t_{pHL} = 8$ ns:

$$t_{pd} = \frac{12 \text{ ns} + 8 \text{ ns}}{2}$$

**Step 3: Perform the arithmetic sum**

$$t_{pd} = \frac{20 \text{ ns}}{2}$$

**Step 4: Simplify to obtain the result**

$$t_{pd} = 10 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 5: Apply the formula for maximum operating frequency**

For a logic gate to function as a binary toggle (flip-flop input), the period must be at least $2 \cdot t_{pd}$:

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$$

**Step 6: Substitute $t_{pd} = 10$ ns**

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}}$$

**Step 7: Compute the denominator**

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{20 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s}}$$

**Step 8: Final result**

$$f_{max} = 50 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz} = 50 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This TTL gate can reliably toggle a flip-flop at a maximum clock rate of 50 MHz. Operating it faster would cause timing violations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Speed-Power Product (Figure of Merit) Calculation

### Problem Statement
A CMOS inverter has:
- Propagation delay: $t_{pd} = 5$ ns
- Static power dissipation: $P_{static} = 0.1$ mW
- Dynamic power dissipation (at 10 MHz): $P_{dynamic} = 1.9$ mW
- Total power: $P_D = P_{static} + P_{dynamic} = 2.0$ mW

Compute the **Speed-Power Product (SPP)**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the Speed-Power Product**

The SPP is a *figure of merit* that combines speed and power into a single metric. Lower is better.

$$SPP = t_{pd} \times P_D$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$SPP = (5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}) \times (2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W})$

**Step 3: Perform the multiplication**

$SPP = 10 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J} = 10 \text{ pJ}$

**Step 4: Convert to standard units**

$SPP = 10 \text{ picojoules (pJ)}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A lower SPP (e.g., 1 pJ in modern CMOS) indicates an excellent balance of speed and power efficiency. High SPP (e.g., 100 pJ) implies either a slow, low-power design or a fast, power-hungry one.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Effect of Load Capacitance on Speed

### Problem Statement
A digital gate drives a load capacitance $C_L = 50$ pF. The output driver resistance is $R_{out} = 200$ $\Omega$. Calculate the **propagation delay** assuming RC charging behavior.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Recall the RC charging equation**

The output voltage across a capacitor through a resistor is:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{final} \cdot (1 - e^{-t/RC})$$

**Step 2: Determine the 50% transition time**

Set $V_{out}(t) = 0.5 \cdot V_{final}$:

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 3: Solve for the exponential term**

$$e^{-t/RC} = 0.5$$

**Step 4: Take the natural logarithm of both sides**

$$-t/RC = \ln(0.5) = -0.693$$

**Step 5: Isolate $t$**

$$t = 0.693 \cdot R \cdot C$$

**Step 6: Substitute the values**

$t_{pd} = 0.693 \times (200 \, \Omega) \times (50 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F})$

**Step 7: Compute the result**

$t_{pd} = 0.693 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{s} = 6.93 \, \text{ns}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For digital gates, the empirical propagation delay formula is **$t_{pd} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{out} \cdot C_L$**. This explains why adding more load (fan-out) makes a digital system slower.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Speed vs. Other Parameters

| **Design Goal** | **Effect on Speed** | **Effect on Power** | **Effect on Cost** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase $V_{CC}$ / $V_{DD}$ | ↑ Faster | ↑↑ Higher (quadratic in dynamic) | No direct effect |
| Increase Transistor Size ($W/L$) | ↑ Faster (lower $R_{out}$) | ↑ Higher (larger capacitance) | ↑ Higher (more silicon) |
| Reduce Load Capacitance | ↑ Faster | ↓ Lower (less charging) | ↓ Lower (less buffer) |
| Use Non-Saturated Logic (ECL) | ↑↑ Much Faster | ↑↑ Higher | ↑ Higher |
| Use CMOS instead of TTL | ↓ Slower (older tech) | ↓↓ Much Lower | ↓ Lower |
| Reduce Fan-Out | ↑ Faster | ↓ Lower | ↑ Higher (more drivers) |
| Use Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) | ↑↑↑ Extremely Fast | ↑↑↑ Very High | ↑↑↑ Very Expensive |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Factors Influencing Switching Speed

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the architectural flow of parameters that collectively determine the switching speed of a digital logic gate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Transition 50 percent] --> B[Input Buffer Stage]
    B --> C[Internal Parasitic Cap Cbc Cbe]
    C --> D[Transistor Switching Network]
    D --> E[Output Driver Stage]
    F[Load Capacitance CL External] --> E
    G[Power Supply VCC VDD] --> D
    H[Output Resistance Rout] --> E
    E --> I[Output Transition 50 percent]
    I --> J[Propagation Delay tpd]
    C --> J
    F --> J
    H --> J
    G --> J
    J --> K[Maximum Frequency fmax]
    J --> L[Speed Power Product SPP]
```

## 4.2 Logic Family Speed Comparison Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SLOW[SLOW Speed family] --> NMOS[NMOS 50 ns]
    NMOS --> CMOS[Standard CMOS 50 ns]
    CMOS --> HC[HC CMOS 8 ns]
    HC --> TTL[Standard TTL 10 ns]
    TTL --> LS[LS TTL 9 ns]
    LS --> AS[AS TTL 1.5 ns]
    AS --> FAST[FAST TTL 2 ns]
    FAST --> ECL10K[ECL 10K 2 ns]
    ECL10K --> ECL100K[ECL 100K 0.75 ns]
    ECL100K --> GAAS[GaAs MESFET 0.1 ns]
    GAAS --> FASTEST[ULTIMATE SPEED BOUND]
```

## 4.3 Speed Measurement Setup (Test Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PULSE[Function Generator Square Wave Input] --> GATE[Device Under Test DUT Logic Gate]
    GATE --> PROBE[Output Probe 50 percent Trigger]
    PROBE --> OSC[Oscilloscope Display]
    OSC --> T1[tpLH Low to High measurement]
    OSC --> T2[tpHL High to Low measurement]
    T1 --> CALC[Compute tpd Average]
    T2 --> CALC
    CALC --> FMAX[Compute fmax 1 over 2tpd]
```

## 4.4 Internal Node Behavior During Switching

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input HIGH to LOW change] --> B[Base Collector Cap Cbc discharges]
    B --> C[Transistor Q1 turns OFF]
    C --> D[Q2 turns ON]
    D --> E[Output node pulled LOW]
    E --> F[Fall time tf measured 90 to 10 percent]
    F --> G[Total tpHL complete]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Mermaid diagrams represent the **functional flow of signals and timing relationships**. In a physical lab, these would be visualized using oscilloscope waveforms showing the input pulse, the 50% trigger points, and the delayed output pulse.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define propagation delay of a logic gate. How is it related to the speed of operation?

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Set B]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Propagation delay ($t_{pd}$) is defined as the time interval between the **50% transition point of the input pulse** and the corresponding **50% transition point of the output pulse** of a logic gate. It is a measure of how quickly a gate responds to input changes.

**Mathematical Expression (1 mark):**

$$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2}$$

**Relation to Speed (0.5 mark):** The speed of a logic gate is **inversely proportional** to its propagation delay:

$$\text{Speed} \propto \frac{1}{t_{pd}}$$

A smaller $t_{pd}$ means a faster gate. For example, an ECL gate with $t_{pd} = 0.5$ ns is much faster than a standard CMOS gate with $t_{pd} = 50$ ns.

---

### Question 2: What is the Speed-Power Product? Why is it called a "figure of merit"?

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The Speed-Power Product (SPP) is defined as the product of the **propagation delay** ($t_{pd}$) and the **power dissipation** ($P_D$) of a logic gate:

$$SPP = t_{pd} \times P_D$$

The unit is **joules (J)**, typically expressed in **picojoules (pJ)**.

**Why "Figure of Merit" (1.5 marks):** It is called a figure of merit because it provides a **single quantitative metric** that combines two opposing performance parameters:
- **Speed** (high speed = low $t_{pd}$ = desired)
- **Power** (low power = low $P_D$ = desired)

A *low SPP* indicates an efficient gate (fast AND low power). It allows engineers to **compare different logic families objectively**. For example, modern CMOS has SPP ≈ 1 pJ, while older TTL has SPP ≈ 100 pJ, making CMOS vastly superior for VLSI design.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks - Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, End Semester Examination]**

#### (a) Explain in detail the switching speed of digital logic gates. Discuss the factors affecting the speed of operation. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction to Switching Speed (1 mark):**
Switching speed is the *most important dynamic characteristic* of a digital logic gate. It determines how quickly the gate can change its output state in response to a change in input. Faster switching enables higher clock frequencies and better system performance.

**2. Definition of Propagation Delay (1.5 marks):**
Propagation delay ($t_{pd}$) is the time taken for the output to reach its 50% level after the input has crossed its 50% level. There are two components:
- **$t_{pLH}$** (Low-to-High delay): Delay when output transitions from 0 to 1.
- **$t_{pHL}$** (High-to-Low delay): Delay when output transitions from 1 to 0.

The average is given by:
$$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$$

**3. Rise and Fall Times (1.5 marks):**
- **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time for output to rise from **10% to 90%** of $V_{OH}$.
- **Fall Time ($t_f$):** Time for output to fall from **90% to 10%** of $V_{OH}$.
- These are related to the RC time constant: $t_r \approx 2.2 \cdot R_{out} \cdot C_L$.

**4. Factors Affecting Speed (2 marks):**
- **Internal Capacitances:** Base-collector ($C_{bc}$) and base-emitter ($C_{be}$) capacitances in BJT; gate capacitances in MOSFET.
- **Load Capacitance ($C_L$):** External capacitive loading slows down switching.
- **Output Resistance ($R_{out}$):** Lower $R_{out}$ → faster charging/discharging.
- **Storage Time:** In saturated logic (TTL), minority carrier storage in the base delays turn-off.
- **Transistor Transit Time:** Time for carriers to cross the base region.
- **Supply Voltage ($V_{CC}$):** Higher voltage → faster transitions but more power.

**5. Conclusion (1 mark):**
Speed is fundamentally limited by the need to charge and discharge capacitances. Non-saturated logic (ECL) avoids storage time and is the fastest. Modern CMOS uses sub-nanometer processes to minimize intrinsic capacitances, achieving sub-nanosecond delays.

---

#### (b) Compare the switching speed of TTL, CMOS, and ECL logic families. Which is the fastest and why? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. TTL Switching Speed (2 marks):**
- Standard TTL has $t_{pd} \approx 10$ ns.
- Schottky TTL (74Sxx): $t_{pd} \approx 3$ ns.
- Advanced Schottky (74ASxx): $t_{pd} \approx 1.5$ ns.
- **Limitation:** TTL operates in *saturation*, which causes **storage time delay** due to minority carrier accumulation in the base. This limits turn-off speed.

**2. CMOS Switching Speed (2 marks):**
- Standard CMOS (4000 series): $t_{pd} \approx 50$ ns.
- HC-CMOS (74HCxx): $t_{pd} \approx 8$ ns.
- Modern nanometer CMOS: $t_{pd} < 1$ ns.
- **Limitation:** CMOS speed is limited by **gate oxide capacitance** and **channel transit time**. However, CMOS has near-zero static power, making it ideal for VLSI.

**3. ECL Switching Speed (2 marks):**
- ECL 10K series: $t_{pd} \approx 2$ ns.
- ECL 100K series: $t_{pd} \approx 0.75$ ns.
- **Advantage:** ECL operates in the **non-saturated (active) region** of the BJT. The transistors never go into saturation, so there is **NO storage time delay**. The differential emitter-coupled topology provides very fast current switching.

**4. Comparison Table (0.5 mark):**

| Family | $t_{pd}$ | Speed Ranking |
|---|---|---|
| Standard CMOS | 50 ns | Slowest |
| Standard TTL | 10 ns | Medium |
| Schottky TTL | 3 ns | Fast |
| ECL 10K | 2 ns | Very Fast |
| ECL 100K | 0.75 ns | Fastest |

**5. Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
**ECL is the fastest** logic family because it avoids the storage time delay inherent to saturated bipolar logic. By keeping transistors in the active region, ECL achieves sub-nanosecond switching, making it the choice for ultra-high-speed applications like supercomputers and GHz communication systems.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, End Semester Examination]**

#### (a) Define the terms rise time, fall time, and propagation delay. Derive the relationship between propagation delay and the RC time constant of a logic gate. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definitions (3 marks):**
- **Rise Time ($t_r$):** The time interval during which the output voltage of a logic gate transitions from **10% to 90%** of its final high-level value ($V_{OH}$). It indicates how quickly the gate can charge its load capacitance to a high state.

- **Fall Time ($t_f$):** The time interval during which the output voltage transitions from **90% to 10%** of $V_{OH}$. It indicates how quickly the gate can discharge its load capacitance to a low state.

- **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$):** The time interval between the **50% transition point of the input** and the **50% transition point of the output**. It is computed as:
$$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$$

**2. Derivation of RC Relationship (4 marks):**

Consider a logic gate driving a load capacitance $C_L$ through an equivalent output resistance $R_{out}$. The output voltage during charging follows:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{final} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

where $\tau = R_{out} \cdot C_L$ is the RC time constant.

To find the 50% transition time, we set $V_{out}(t) = 0.5 \cdot V_{final}$:

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-t_{pd}/\tau}$$

$$e^{-t_{pd}/\tau} = 0.5$$

$$-\frac{t_{pd}}{\tau} = \ln(0.5) = -0.693$$

$$\boxed{t_{pd} = 0.693 \cdot R_{out} \cdot C_L = 0.693 \cdot \tau}$$

**Conclusion:** The propagation delay is **directly proportional** to the RC time constant. To increase speed (reduce $t_{pd}$), we must either reduce the output resistance or minimize the load capacitance.

---

#### (b) A digital system uses a CMOS gate with $t_{pd} = 4$ ns and power dissipation $P_D = 0.5$ mW. Compute the Speed-Power Product and the maximum clock frequency. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Given Data (0.5 marks)**
- Propagation delay: $t_{pd} = 4$ ns = $4 \times 10^{-9}$ s
- Power dissipation: $P_D = 0.5$ mW = $0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ W

**Step 2: Compute Speed-Power Product (3 marks)**

$$SPP = t_{pd} \times P_D$$

$$SPP = (4 \times 10^{-9}) \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$SPP = 2 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J} = 2 \text{ pJ}$$

**Step 3: Compute Maximum Clock Frequency (3 marks)**

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f_{max} = 125 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz} = 125 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 4: Engineering Interpretation (0.5 marks)**

The CMOS gate has a Speed-Power Product of **2 pJ**, which is excellent — indicating good speed and low power. The maximum toggle frequency is **125 MHz**, suitable for mid-range microcontrollers, digital signal processors, and consumer electronics.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (1 mark lost):** Students often write $f_{max} = 1/t_{pd}$ instead of $f_{max} = 1/(2t_{pd})$. Remember: one full clock cycle requires *both* a high-to-low AND a low-to-high transition.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (1 mark lost):** Forgetting to convert units. $t_{pd} = 4$ ns must be written as $4 \times 10^{-9}$ s before multiplying with power in watts. Showing units clearly earns full marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (1 mark lost):** Confusing $t_{pd}$ with $t_r$ or $t_f$. Remember: $t_{pd}$ is the **50% to 50%** time, while $t_r$ and $t_f$ are **10%–90%** times. They are different parameters.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (0.5 mark lost):** Writing the propagation delay formula as $t_{pd} = t_{pLH} - t_{pHL}$ instead of the average. The correct formula is the **arithmetic mean**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Speed** of a digital gate is quantified by **propagation delay** $t_{pd}$, measured at the 50% transition point.
- The **average propagation delay** formula is: $t_{pd} = (t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}) / 2$.
- **Rise time** ($t_r$) = 10% to 90% transition; **Fall time** ($t_f$) = 90% to 10% transition.
- The **maximum operating frequency** is $f_{max} = 1/(2 \cdot t_{pd})$.
- The **Speed-Power Product (SPP)** is the figure of merit: $SPP = t_{pd} \times P_D$. **Lower is better.**
- The **empirical RC delay** formula: $t_{pd} \approx 0.693 \cdot R_{out} \cdot C_L$.
- **ECL is the fastest logic family** because it operates in the *non-saturated region*, avoiding storage time delay.
- **TTL is faster than standard CMOS** in older technology nodes due to bipolar transistor transit times, but modern CMOS matches or exceeds TTL.
- **Major speed-limiting factors:** internal parasitic capacitances, load capacitance, output resistance, and storage time in saturated logic.
- For KTU exams, always **draw waveform diagrams** showing $t_{pLH}$, $t_{pHL}$, $t_r$, and $t_f$ with proper 10%, 50%, and 90% markers.
- **Unit conversion is critical:** $1 \text{ ns} = 10^{-9} \text{ s}$; $1 \text{ pJ} = 10^{-12} \text{ J}$; $1 \text{ MHz} = 10^6 \text{ Hz}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT304 - DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operating Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Temperature** is the range of ambient temperatures over which a digital integrated circuit (IC) is guaranteed by the manufacturer to function reliably while meeting all its specified electrical characteristics (such as propagation delay, noise margin, fan-out, and logic voltage levels).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBBMT304 - Digital Electronics, Module 4: Logic Families & IC Characteristics)**, operating temperature is one of the four primary **DC and Switching Characteristics** of digital logic families (alongside fan-in, fan-out, propagation delay, and noise margin). It is a *boundary condition* that constrains where a digital circuit can be physically deployed.

The operating temperature range is always expressed as a **two-bound interval**:

$$T_{op} \in \left[ T_{min}, \; T_{max} \right]$$

where $T_{min}$ and $T_{max}$ are the **lower** and **upper limits** of ambient temperature, respectively, measured in **degrees Celsius (°C)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Human Body Comfort Zone**
> Think of a digital IC as a person working in an office. Just as a human can only focus and type accurately between, say, 18°C and 32°C, an IC can only produce correct logic outputs within a specific temperature window. Outside this window, the "thinking" (switching of transistors) becomes sluggish, noisy, or outright wrong. The manufacturer defines this "comfort zone" in the datasheet as the **Operating Temperature Range**.

## 1.3 Standard Industry Temperature Grades

Manufacturers (Texas Instruments, Intel, ON Semiconductor, etc.) classify ICs into **three commercial grades** based on operating temperature:

| Grade Name | Operating Range | Typical Application |
|------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Commercial** | **0 °C to +70 °C** | Desktop PCs, consumer electronics, indoor lab equipment |
| **Industrial** | **−40 °C to +85 °C** | Factory automation, automotive cabin, outdoor enclosures |
| **Military (MIL-STD-883)** | **−55 °C to +125 °C** | Aerospace, defense, space, military field equipment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students are expected to know the three standard temperature grades and be able to **justify the choice of grade** for a given application (e.g., "Why is a military-grade IC used in a satellite?").

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Unit of Measurement:** Degrees Celsius (**°C**), sometimes Kelvin (**K**) in physics-based derivations where $\Delta T$ is involved.
- **Reference Junction Temperature ($T_J$):** The temperature at the actual semiconductor die inside the IC package. This is the *true* temperature that affects transistor behaviour.
- **Thermal Resistance ($\theta_{JA}$):** Measured in **°C/Watt (°C/W)**, it quantifies how effectively the package transfers heat from the die to the ambient air.

$$T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA}$$

where $T_A$ is ambient temperature, $P_D$ is power dissipated, and $\theta_{JA}$ is junction-to-ambient thermal resistance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does Operating Temperature Matter?

Every semiconductor parameter is **temperature-dependent**. As temperature changes, the following IC characteristics drift:

1. **Threshold Voltage ($V_{TH}$)** of MOSFETs **decreases** with temperature.
2. **Carrier Mobility ($\mu$)** **decreases** with temperature (lattice scattering increases).
3. **Reverse Saturation Current ($I_S$)** of PN junctions **increases exponentially** with temperature.
4. **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$)** generally **increases** with temperature (slower switching).
5. **Noise Margin** may **degrade** at temperature extremes.
6. **Power Dissipation** may **change** non-linearly with temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU "Why" Question:** *"Why does the manufacturer specify an upper limit on operating temperature?"* — Answer: Because beyond a certain temperature, the IC's electrical parameters (especially leakage current $I_S$) rise exponentially, causing **thermal runaway** and permanent device failure.

## 2.2 The Three-Stage Temperature Behaviour

| Stage | Temperature Zone | Dominant Physical Effect | IC Behaviour |
|-------|------------------|--------------------------|--------------|
| **Stage 1** | $T < T_{min}$ (Sub-zero) | Carrier freeze-out, reduced mobility | Slow switching, possible latch-up |
| **Stage 2** | $T_{min} \leq T \leq T_{max}$ (Normal) | Balanced drift | **Guaranteed correct operation** |
| **Stage 3** | $T > T_{max}$ (Overheat) | Exponential leakage rise, electromigration | Erratic output, permanent damage |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Unit | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---------------------|-------------|------|--------------------|
| 1 | $T_{op} \in [T_{min}, T_{max}]$ | Operating temperature range | °C | Datasheet specification |
| 2 | $T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA}$ | Junction temperature | °C | Steady-state thermal model |
| 3 | $I_S(T) = I_{S0} \cdot 2^{(T - T_0)/10}$ | Reverse leakage current doubles every **10 °C** | A | Empirical rule for silicon |
| 4 | $V_{TH}(T) = V_{TH}(T_0) - k \cdot (T - T_0)$ | Threshold voltage decreases linearly | V | $k \approx 2 \text{ mV/°C}$ (typical CMOS) |
| 5 | $t_{pd}(T) = t_{pd}(T_0) \cdot \left[1 + \alpha \cdot (T - T_0)\right]$ | Propagation delay rises with temperature | s | $\alpha$ is a family-specific coefficient |
| 6 | $P_D = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC}$ | Static power dissipation | W | CMOS family |
| 7 | $\Delta T = T_{max} - T_{min}$ | Temperature window width | °C | E.g., Military: 180 °C; Commercial: 70 °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** The reverse leakage current $I_S$ approximately **doubles for every 10 °C rise** in temperature. This is a **frequently asked 3-mark question**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Defense:** Satellites experience −55 °C to +125 °C; hence military-grade ICs are mandatory.
- **Automotive Under-the-Hood:** Engine bay temperatures exceed 85 °C, demanding **automotive-grade (AEC-Q100)** ICs (−40 °C to +125 °C or wider).
- **Cryogenic Computing:** Quantum computer control electronics operate near **4 K (liquid helium)** and require specially characterized CMOS.
- **Industrial IoT:** Outdoor sensors in Kerala's tropical climate must survive **monsoon humidity + 40 °C heat**, so **industrial-grade** components are preferred.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Junction Temperature vs. Ambient Temperature

We derive the relationship between junction temperature, ambient temperature, power dissipation, and thermal resistance — a **favourite KTU numerical**.

### Given:
- Ambient temperature: $T_A$ (°C)
- Power dissipated by IC: $P_D$ (W)
- Junction-to-Ambient thermal resistance: $\theta_{JA}$ (°C/W)

### Step 1 — Definition of Thermal Resistance
By definition, thermal resistance relates the temperature *rise* across a path to the *heat flow* through it:

$$
\theta_{JA} \;=\; \frac{\Delta T}{P_D} \;=\; \frac{T_J - T_A}{P_D}
$$

### Step 2 — Rearranging for Junction Temperature
Multiply both sides by $P_D$:

$$
P_D \cdot \theta_{JA} \;=\; T_J - T_A
$$

### Step 3 — Solving for $T_J$

$$
\boxed{T_J \;=\; T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA}}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Illustration
**Problem (KTU Practice Style):** A CMOS gate dissipates $P_D = 100 \text{ mW} = 0.1 \text{ W}$, has $\theta_{JA} = 100 \text{ °C/W}$, and operates in an ambient of $T_A = 50 \text{ °C}$. Find the junction temperature.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_J &= T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA} \\
&= 50 + (0.1)(100) \\
&= 50 + 10 \\
T_J &= 60 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is well within the commercial range ($0$ to $+70$ °C), so the IC operates safely.

## 3.2 Derivation: Leakage Current Doubling Rule

The reverse saturation current of a PN junction follows:

$$
I_S(T) \;=\; K \cdot e^{E_g / (kT)}
$$

where $E_g$ is the silicon bandgap (≈ 1.12 eV), $k$ is Boltzmann's constant, and $K$ is a process constant.

### Step 1 — Ratio at Two Temperatures

$$
\frac{I_S(T_2)}{I_S(T_1)} \;=\; \frac{e^{E_g / (kT_2)}}{e^{E_g / (kT_1)}} \;=\; e^{\frac{E_g}{k}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)}
$$

### Step 2 — For $\Delta T = 10 \text{ °C}$ near $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$
Substituting $E_g = 1.12$ eV, $k = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K:

$$
\frac{I_S(T_1 + 10)}{I_S(T_1)} \;\approx\; 2.0
$$

This empirically confirms that leakage **doubles per 10 °C rise**.

### Step 3 — After 50 °C Rise (5 doublings)
$$
I_S(50 \text{ °C rise}) \;=\; 2^5 \cdot I_S(T_0) \;=\; 32 \cdot I_S(T_0)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 50 °C rise multiplies leakage by ~32×. This is why **thermal management** (heatsinks, fans, derating) is critical in densely packed PCBs.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Temperature on Logic Family Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Trend with ↑ Temperature | Physical Reason | Impact on Design |
|-----------|--------|--------------------------|------------------|------------------|
| Reverse Leakage Current | $I_S$ | ↑↑↑ (exponential) | Increased carrier generation | Higher static power |
| Threshold Voltage (CMOS) | $V_{TH}$ | ↓ (linear) | Fermi level shift | Lower noise margin (high) |
| Carrier Mobility | $\mu$ | ↓ (power law) | Increased lattice vibration | Slower switching |
| Propagation Delay | $t_{pd}$ | ↑ (linear) | Lower mobility → less drive current | Reduced max clock frequency |
| Resistivity of Al interconnects | $\rho$ | ↑ (linear) | Metal phonon scattering | Increased $RC$ delay |
| Noise Margin (HIGH) | $NM_H$ | ↓ (slight) | $V_{OH}$ drift | Reduced noise immunity |
| Power Dissipation (CMOS static) | $P_D$ | ↑ | Leakage dominates | Thermal runaway risk |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Takeaway:** For CMOS, **static power increases with temperature**, which in turn *increases temperature further* — a positive feedback loop that can destroy the chip if not managed. This is the classic **thermal runaway** problem.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: Verifying Safe Operation

**Question:** A 74LS00 (TTL) IC has $T_{max} = 70$ °C, $\theta_{JA} = 80$ °C/W, and $P_D = 50$ mW. The device is to be deployed at $T_A = 60$ °C. Will it operate safely?

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_J &= T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA} \\
&= 60 + (0.05)(80) \\
&= 60 + 4 \\
T_J &= 64 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $T_J = 64 \text{ °C} < T_{max} = 70 \text{ °C}$, the IC operates **safely** with a **6 °C thermal margin**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — IC Operating Temperature Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Application Defined"] --> B["What is the deployment environment?"]
    B --> C1["Indoor Lab, Consumer Electronics"]
    B --> C2["Factory Floor, Automotive Cabin, Outdoor"]
    B --> C3["Aerospace, Defense, Space, Cryogenic"]

    C1 --> D1["Select COMMERCIAL Grade"]
    C2 --> D2["Select INDUSTRIAL Grade"]
    C3 --> D3["Select MILITARY or Space Grade"]

    D1 --> E1["T range: 0 to 70 degC"]
    D2 --> E2["T range: -40 to 85 degC"]
    D3 --> E3["T range: -55 to 125 degC"]

    E1 --> F["Check TJ using thermal equation"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F

    F --> G{"TJ less than Tj max?"}
    G -- Yes --> H["SAFE: Deploy IC"]
    G -- No --> I["UNSAFE: Add heatsink, reduce PD, or upgrade grade"]
    I --> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Thermal Model of an IC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Silicon Die (Junction)"] -- "Heat flow PD" --> B["Package Body (Case)"]
    B -- "Conduction" --> C["PCB Copper Pads"]
    C -- "Convection" --> D["Ambient Air"]
    A -. "theta JC" .-> B
    B -. "theta CA" .-> C
    C -. "theta SA" .-> D

    E["TJ = TA + PD times theta JA"] --> A
    F["Power Source VCC x ICC"] --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The thermal resistance chain acts like an electrical series circuit, where *temperature drop* is analogous to *voltage drop* and *heat flow* is analogous to *current*. The total $\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CA} + \theta_{SA}$.

## 4.3 Mermaid Graph — Effect of Temperature on Key IC Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T["Temperature T rises"] --> A1["IS: exponential increase"]
    T --> A2["VTH: linear decrease"]
    T --> A3["Mobility: decrease"]
    T --> A4["Resistivity of metal: increase"]

    A1 --> B1["Static power PD rises"]
    A1 --> B2["Thermal runaway risk"]
    A2 --> B3["Noise margin HIGH decreases"]
    A3 --> C1["Propagation delay tpd rises"]
    A4 --> C1

    B1 --> D["Junction temperature rises further"]
    D --> T

    C1 --> E["Maximum clock frequency falls"]
    B3 --> F["Bit error rate rises"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "operating temperature" of a digital IC. List the three standard temperature grades used in industry.**

**Model Answer:**
Operating temperature is the range of ambient temperatures over which a digital IC is guaranteed by the manufacturer to meet all its specified electrical parameters (such as $V_{OH}$, $V_{OL}$, $t_{pd}$, and noise margin).

The three standard temperature grades are:
1. **Commercial:** 0 °C to +70 °C
2. **Industrial:** −40 °C to +85 °C
3. **Military:** −55 °C to +125 °C

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why does the reverse leakage current of a PN junction double for every 10 °C rise in temperature? Mention its effect on CMOS power dissipation.**

**Model Answer:**
The reverse saturation current of a PN junction follows the equation $I_S = K \cdot e^{E_g / (kT)}$, which is exponential in temperature. Evaluating this expression for a 10 °C step near 300 K yields a ratio of approximately 2.

**Effect on CMOS:** Static power dissipation $P_D = V_{CC} \cdot I_{leakage}$ rises exponentially with temperature, increasing the die temperature, which in turn raises leakage further — a positive feedback loop known as **thermal runaway**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 Internal Choice A]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **operating temperature** in digital ICs. With a neat table, list the three industry-standard temperature grades and their corresponding ranges. Justify why a **military-grade IC** is mandatory for satellite applications. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A CMOS logic gate operates at $V_{CC} = 5$ V and draws a static current of $I_{CC} = 2$ mA at room temperature. The IC has a thermal resistance $\theta_{JA} = 100$ °C/W and must operate in an environment where $T_A = 60$ °C. Calculate the **junction temperature** and comment on whether the IC is operating safely if $T_{max} = 125$ °C. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

**Operating Temperature Definition:**
Operating temperature is the range of ambient temperatures over which a digital IC is guaranteed to operate within its specified electrical parameters.

**Three Temperature Grades:**

| Grade | Range |
|-------|-------|
| Commercial | 0 °C to +70 °C |
| Industrial | −40 °C to +85 °C |
| Military | −55 °C to +125 °C |

**Justification for Satellite Use:**
Satellites experience extreme temperature swings — direct solar exposure can heat components above +100 °C, while shadowed regions in space can drop to −100 °C. A commercial IC (0 to +70 °C) or industrial IC (−40 to +85 °C) would fail catastrophically. Only **military-grade ICs (−55 °C to +125 °C)** can survive this envelope, making them mandatory.

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Static Power Dissipation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_D &= V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC} \\
&= (5 \text{ V}) \cdot (2 \text{ mA}) \\
&= 10 \text{ mW} = 0.01 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Junction Temperature Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_J &= T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA} \\
&= 60 + (0.01)(100) \\
&= 60 + 1 \\
T_J &= 61 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Safety Verdict:**
Since $T_J = 61 \text{ °C} \ll T_{max} = 125 \text{ °C}$, the IC operates **safely** with a thermal margin of **64 °C**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 Internal Choice B]`

**(a)** Discuss the **effects of temperature variation** on the following parameters of a digital IC: (i) Threshold voltage $V_{TH}$, (ii) Propagation delay $t_{pd}$, (iii) Reverse leakage current $I_S$, and (iv) Noise margin. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain **thermal runaway** in CMOS ICs. How does it relate to operating temperature limits? A CMOS IC has $P_D = 0.5$ W at 25 °C, and the leakage current doubles every 10 °C. Estimate the power dissipation at 75 °C. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**(i) Threshold Voltage $V_{TH}$:** Decreases linearly with temperature at a rate of approximately **−2 mV/°C** for CMOS, due to Fermi level shift in the MOS channel.

**(ii) Propagation Delay $t_{pd}$:** Increases linearly with temperature because carrier mobility decreases due to enhanced lattice scattering, reducing the drive current available to charge/discharge load capacitances.

**(iii) Reverse Leakage Current $I_S$:** Increases **exponentially** with temperature, doubling every 10 °C, due to enhanced thermal carrier generation across the PN junction.

**(iv) Noise Margin:** Noise margin (especially $NM_H$) decreases at high temperatures because $V_{OH}$ drifts downward as $V_{TH}$ falls, reducing the guaranteed noise-immunity voltage.

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Thermal Runaway Explanation:**
Thermal runaway is a positive feedback phenomenon in CMOS ICs where temperature rise → leakage increase → power dissipation rise → temperature rise further. Beyond $T_{max}$, this loop becomes self-sustaining and destroys the device.

**Numerical Estimation:**

Power dissipation scales approximately with leakage current. With $I_S$ doubling per 10 °C:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= 75 - 25 = 50 \text{ °C} \\
\text{Number of doublings} &= 50 / 10 = 5 \\
\text{Multiplication factor} &= 2^5 = 32
\end{aligned}
$$

If we assume static power is dominated by leakage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_D(75 \text{ °C}) &\approx P_D(25 \text{ °C}) \times 32 \\
&= 0.5 \text{ W} \times 32 \\
P_D(75 \text{ °C}) &= 16 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

This dramatic rise illustrates why temperature derating is essential for reliable CMOS design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Writing $P_D = 0.01$ instead of converting to Watts. Always convert mW → W before multiplying with °C/W.
> 2. **Confusing $T_{max}$ (operating) with $T_{Jmax}$ (junction):** The datasheet often lists *both*. $T_{Jmax}$ is usually 150 °C or 175 °C; $T_{max}$ is the *ambient* limit.
> 3. **Mixing up Commercial vs. Military ranges:** Memorize them as **0/70, −40/85, −55/125** — common 3-mark trap.
> 4. **Ignoring thermal runaway:** A question on "why is upper limit specified" expects *thermal runaway* as the answer, not just "the IC stops working."
> 5. **Linear vs. Exponential confusion:** Students often say "leakage increases linearly with temperature" — it is **exponential**. The "doubles per 10 °C" rule is exponential behaviour.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Operating Temperature** is the manufacturer-guaranteed ambient temperature range for correct IC operation.
- **Three Standard Grades:** Commercial (0/+70), Industrial (−40/+85), Military (−55/+125) — all in °C.
- **Junction Temperature Equation:** $T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA}$ — **must be ≤ $T_{Jmax}$**.
- **Thermal Resistance Unit:** °C/W (analogue of electrical resistance, but for heat).
- **Leakage Current Rule:** Approximately **doubles every 10 °C** rise (exponential behaviour).
- **Threshold Voltage:** Decreases by ~2 mV/°C in CMOS.
- **Propagation Delay:** Increases with temperature (mobility degradation).
- **Thermal Runaway:** Positive feedback loop of temperature → leakage → power → temperature; sets the practical upper limit.
- **Application-Grading Logic:** Indoor → Commercial; Outdoor/Automotive → Industrial; Aerospace/Defense → Military.
- **Unit Conversion Reminder:** 1 mW = 10⁻³ W. Always convert before using in $T_J$ formula.
- **Why Specify a Lower Limit?** Below $T_{min}$, carrier freeze-out, mobility collapse, and possible latch-up in CMOS can occur.
- **Why Specify an Upper Limit?** Above $T_{max}$, leakage explodes, thermal runaway risk, electromigration in metal interconnects accelerates.
- **Thermal Management Techniques:** Heatsinks, forced-air cooling, PCB copper pours, derating, thermal vias, selecting a higher-grade IC.
- **KTU Favourite Numerical Pattern:** Given $P_D$, $\theta_{JA}$, $T_A$ → find $T_J$ and compare with $T_{max}$.
- **Datasheet Symbol Conventions:** $\theta_{JA}$ = junction-to-ambient; $\theta_{JC}$ = junction-to-case; $\theta_{CA}$ = case-to-ambient.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TTL: Basic Working Principle of TTL NAND Gate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT304 - DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** TTL: Basic Working Principle of TTL NAND Gate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic)**
> TTL is a class of digital logic circuits built using **bipolar junction transistors (BJTs)** and diffused resistors on a single monolithic integrated circuit. In TTL, the input stage and the switching/amplifying function are both performed by transistors (unlike DTL, which uses diodes at the input). The canonical TTL building block is the **2-input NAND gate**, which historically defined the entire family.

The **basic TTL NAND gate** consists of three functional sub-circuits:
1. **Input Stage** — A **multi-emitter transistor** $Q_1$ that performs the AND function.
2. **Phase-Splitter Stage** — A single BJT $Q_2$ that generates two anti-phase drive signals.
3. **Output Stage** — A **totem-pole (push-pull) arrangement** of transistors $Q_3$ and $Q_4$ that actively pulls the output either HIGH or LOW.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Specific Syllabus Highlight**
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 syllabus, students must be able to *sketch the circuit of a basic 2-input TTL NAND gate, identify each transistor stage, and analytically justify the output for all four input combinations (00, 01, 10, 11).*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Tap & Tank" Intuition

Imagine a **water tank** with **two inlet valves (A and B)** and **one outlet pipe (Y)**. The tank is the **multi-emitter transistor $Q_1$**:

- If **either valve A or B is CLOSED (LOW = 0 V)** → no water pressure reaches the *base* of the next stage → the outlet pipe Y is **OPEN, water flows out HIGH (Logic 1)**.
- If **both valves A and B are OPEN (HIGH = Vcc)** → water rushes *into* the base of the next stage → the outlet pipe Y is **BLOCKED, no flow = LOW (Logic 0)**.

This is exactly the **NAND truth table**:

$$
Y = \overline{A \cdot B}
$$

| A | B | Y (NAND) | Valve Analogy |
|---|---|----------|---------------|
| 0 | 0 | 1 | Both closed → tank empties → HIGH |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | One closed → tank empties → HIGH |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | One closed → tank empties → HIGH |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Both open → tank floods → LOW |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorisation trick:** In TTL, the *multi-emitter transistor itself* is the AND gate; the *transistor $Q_2$ + totem-pole* stage is essentially the inverting amplifier that converts AND into NAND.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Values (Standard 74xx Series)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Vcc = **5 V**) |
|-----------|--------|-------------------------------|
| Supply Voltage | $V_{CC}$ | **5 V** ± 5% |
| HIGH output min | $V_{OH(min)}$ | **2.4 V** |
| LOW output max | $V_{OL(max)}$ | **0.4 V** |
| HIGH input min | $V_{IH(min)}$ | **2.0 V** |
| LOW input max | $V_{IL(max)}$ | **0.8 V** |
| Power dissipation / gate | $P_D$ | **10 mW** |
| Propagation delay | $t_{pd}$ | **10 ns** |
| Fan-out | $N$ | **10** |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Topology of a Basic 2-Input TTL NAND Gate

The standard TTL NAND gate (e.g., **7400 IC**) contains the following components:

| Component | Role | Typical Value |
|-----------|------|---------------|
| $R_1$ | Base bias resistor for $Q_1$ | 4 kΩ |
| $D_1, D_2$ | Input protection (clamping) diodes | — |
| $Q_1$ | Multi-emitter NPN input transistor (performs AND) | — |
| $R_2$ | Collector resistor of $Q_2$ | 1.6 kΩ |
| $Q_2$ | Phase-splitter NPN transistor | — |
| $R_3$ | Base resistor for $Q_3$ | 1.6 kΩ |
| $R_4$ | Base resistor for $Q_4$ | 130 Ω |
| $R_5$ | Collector (pull-up) resistor for $Q_4$ | 3 kΩ |
| $D_3$ | Level-shift diode (raises turn-on threshold of $Q_3$) | — |
| $Q_3$ | Upper output (active pull-up) NPN, emitter-follower | — |
| $Q_4$ | Lower output (active pull-down) NPN, common-emitter | — |

> [!NOTE]
> The totem-pole output is a defining feature of TTL. The two transistors $Q_3$ and $Q_4$ are never ON simultaneously — they switch in *anti-phase* — which gives TTL its low static power dissipation and fast switching.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Analysis (Why and How)

**Case 1 — Either Input (or both) is LOW (Logic 0, ≈ 0.2 V):**

- The **Base–Emitter junction** of $Q_1$ corresponding to that LOW input becomes **forward-biased**.
- $Q_1$ is driven into **saturation**.
- The voltage at the base of $Q_2$ is clamped to:

$$
V_{B2} = V_{BE1(sat)} + V_{IL} \approx 0.7 + 0.2 = 0.9\ \text{V}
$$

- $0.9\ \text{V}$ is **insufficient** to forward-bias the *Base–Emitter* of $Q_2$ (which needs ≥ 0.7 V) **plus** the diode $D_3$ (0.7 V) **plus** the BE of $Q_3$ (0.7 V) — a total of **≈ 2.1 V**.
- Therefore $Q_2$ is **OFF**, $Q_4$ is **OFF** (no base current via $R_4$).
- $Q_3$ receives base current through $R_3$ → $Q_3$ is **ON (active region, emitter-follower)**.
- Output Y is pulled up to:

$$
V_{OH} = V_{CC} - V_{BE3} - V_{D3} - I_{B3} R_2 \approx 5 - 0.7 - 0.7 - 0.2 \approx 3.4\ \text{V}
$$

  → **Logic HIGH (1)**.

**Case 2 — Both Inputs HIGH (Logic 1, ≈ Vcc):**

- All **Base–Emitter** junctions of $Q_1$ are **reverse-biased**.
- $Q_1$ operates in **inverse active mode**: current flows from $V_{CC}$ through $R_1$ into the **Base–Collector** junction of $Q_1$ (which is now forward-biased) and into the base of $Q_2$.
- $Q_2$ is driven into **saturation**.
- $V_{CE2(sat)} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$ → the base of $Q_3$ is at ≈ 0.2 + 0.7 = 0.9 V → **insufficient to turn ON $Q_3$ + $D_3$ + $Q_3$ BE stack** → $Q_3$ is **OFF**.
- Meanwhile, the collector voltage of $Q_2$ is at $V_{CE2(sat)} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$, so $Q_4$ receives ample base drive through $R_4$ → $Q_4$ is **ON (saturation)**.
- Output Y is pulled down to:

$$
V_{OL} = V_{CE4(sat)} \approx 0.2\ \text{V} \quad (\text{Logic 0, i.e., ≤ } 0.4\ \text{V})
$$

This is the **NAND truth table** behaviour.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are examinable. Memorise the **boundary voltages** and the **two saturation-state voltage equations**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical / Boundary Value |
|---|----------|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | NAND Output (Boolean) | $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$ | — |
| 2 | HIGH output voltage | $V_{OH} \approx V_{CC} - V_{BE3} - V_{D3}$ | **≥ 2.4 V** |
| 3 | LOW output voltage | $V_{OL} = V_{CE4(sat)}$ | **≤ 0.4 V** |
| 4 | Voltage at $Q_2$ base (input LOW) | $V_{B2} = V_{BE1(sat)} + V_{IL}$ | ≈ **0.9 V** |
| 5 | Threshold for $Q_2$ to turn ON | $V_{B2(th)} = V_{BE2} + V_{D3} + V_{BE3}$ | ≈ **2.1 V** |
| 6 | HIGH-state Noise Margin | $NM_H = V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)}$ | $2.4 - 2.0 =$ **0.4 V** |
| 7 | LOW-state Noise Margin | $NM_L = V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)}$ | $0.8 - 0.4 =$ **0.4 V** |
| 8 | Static Power Dissipation (per gate) | $P_D = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC(avg)}$ | **10 mW** |
| 9 | Propagation Delay | $t_{pd} = \dfrac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2}$ | **≈ 10 ns** |
| 10 | Fan-out | $N = \min\!\left(\dfrac{I_{OH}}{I_{IH}},\ \dfrac{I_{OL}}{I_{IL}}\right)$ | **10** |
| 11 | Speed–Power Product | $SPP = t_{pd} \cdot P_D$ | **≈ 100 pJ** |
| 12 | Base current of $Q_2$ (Both inputs HIGH) | $I_{B2} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BC1} - V_{BE2}}{R_1}$ | ≈ **0.9 mA** |

> ⚠ **Markdown-table note:** All `|x|` absolute-value expressions (if any) would be rendered as $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid breaking the table.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- TTL formed the **backbone of computer and industrial control systems from the 1960s to 1990s** (e.g., PDP-11, IBM System/370, Minicomputers, aerospace avionics).
- The **74xx, 74LSxx (Low-power Schottky), 74ASxx, 74Fxx** series are TTL sub-families.
- **Open-collector TTL variants** (e.g., 7401, 7403) are still used for **wired-OR buses** and **driving high-current loads** (relays, LEDs).
- Modern **TTL-compatible logic** (74HCT series) bridges to **CMOS** by accepting CMOS-level inputs while preserving TTL pin-outs — vital for **mixed-signal PCB design**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Output HIGH Voltage ($V_{OH}$)

We consider the condition **A = 0, B = 0** (or any single LOW input). In this case $Q_2$ is **OFF**, $Q_3$ is **ON** in the active (emitter-follower) region, and $Q_4$ is **OFF**.

The base current of $Q_3$ is supplied through $R_2$ and $R_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{KVL around the loop: } V_{CC} &= I_{B3}\,R_2 + I_{B3}\,R_3 + V_{BE3} + V_{D3} + V_{OH} \\
\\
\text{Since } I_{B3}\,R_2 + I_{B3}\,R_3 &\ll V_{BE3} + V_{D3} \text{ for small } I_{B3}, \\
\\
\therefore\ V_{OH} &\approx V_{CC} - V_{BE3} - V_{D3} \\
&= 5\ \text{V} - 0.7\ \text{V} - 0.7\ \text{V} \\
&= 3.6\ \text{V}\ \text{(no-load)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Under load (when sourcing $I_{OH}$), the voltage drops further; the *guaranteed* minimum is **2.4 V** (74xx TTL spec).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Output LOW Voltage ($V_{OL}$)

When **both inputs A = 1 and B = 1**, $Q_2$ saturates. The collector of $Q_2$ drops to $V_{CE2(sat)} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$, which is also the base of $Q_4$. This forward-biases the BE of $Q_4$ through $R_4$ and saturates it:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{B4} &= V_{CE2(sat)} = 0.2\ \text{V} \\
\\
I_{B4} &= \frac{V_{B4} - V_{BE4}}{R_4} = \frac{0.2 - 0.7}{130}\ \text{(clamped by BE junction)} \\
\\
\text{Forcing function: } V_{OL} &= V_{CE4(sat)} \approx 0.2\ \text{V} \le 0.4\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **guaranteed** TTL specification is $V_{OL(max)} = 0.4\ \text{V}$ at $I_{OL} = 16\ \text{mA}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Threshold at $Q_2$ Base

For $Q_2$ to **turn ON** and steer the output LOW, the voltage at its base must exceed the *stacked* threshold of three forward-biased junctions ($Q_2$ BE + $D_3$ + $Q_3$ BE):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{B2(th)} &= V_{BE2} + V_{D3} + V_{BE3} \\
&= 0.7 + 0.7 + 0.7 \\
&= 2.1\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

- If $V_{B2} < 2.1\ \text{V}$ → $Q_2$ OFF → output HIGH.
- If $V_{B2} \ge 2.1\ \text{V}$ → $Q_2$ ON (saturated) → output LOW.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **2.1 V threshold** is precisely why the input voltage $V_{IH(min)} = 2.0\ \text{V}$ is the lower bound for a *reliable* logic-1.

---

### 3.4 Noise Margin Computation

$$
\begin{aligned}
NM_H &= V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)} \\
&= 2.4\ \text{V} - 2.0\ \text{V} \\
&= 0.4\ \text{V} \\
\\
NM_L &= V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)} \\
&= 0.8\ \text{V} - 0.4\ \text{V} \\
&= 0.4\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

Both noise margins are **0.4 V** — a hallmark of standard TTL.

---

### 3.5 Power Dissipation Per Gate

Assuming a 50% duty cycle (typical) and using the average supply current from TTL datasheets ($I_{CC(avg)} = 2\ \text{mA}$ for the 7400 NAND):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_D &= V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC(avg)} \\
&= 5\ \text{V} \times 2\ \text{mA} \\
&= 10\ \text{mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.6 Speed–Power Product (Figure of Merit)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPP &= t_{pd} \cdot P_D \\
&= 10\ \text{ns} \times 10\ \text{mW} \\
&= 100\ \text{pJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

A **lower** SPP indicates a *better* digital logic family (e.g., 74LS TTL ≈ 20 pJ, 74HC CMOS ≈ 5 pJ).

---

### 3.7 Complete Truth-Table Analysis Matrix

| $V_A$ | $V_B$ | $Q_1$ mode | $Q_2$ | $Q_3$ | $Q_4$ | $V_Y$ | Logic |
|-------|-------|-----------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| 0.2 V (L) | 0.2 V (L) | Saturation | OFF | ON | OFF | ≈ 3.4 V | **1** |
| 0.2 V (L) | 3.4 V (H) | Saturation (A) | OFF | ON | OFF | ≈ 3.4 V | **1** |
| 3.4 V (H) | 0.2 V (L) | Saturation (B) | OFF | ON | OFF | ≈ 3.4 V | **1** |
| 3.4 V (H) | 3.4 V (H) | Inverse Active | Saturation | OFF | Saturation | ≈ 0.2 V | **0** |

This matrix is the **expected answer pattern** in KTU ESE valuation for a "describe the working of TTL NAND gate" question.

### 4.3 Schematic-Level Description (Textual)

For the benefit of the KTU answer script, the **schematic** of the basic TTL NAND gate should be drawn as:

```
                              ├── Collector ── R2 (1.6 kΩ) ── Vcc
                              │                    │
                              │                    ├─ R3 (1.6 kΩ) ── Base of Q3
                              │                    │
                              │                    └─ D3 ── Base of Q3
                              │                                │
                              │                              Q3 (NPN, Emitter Follower)
                              │                                │
                              │                                ├─ Emitter ──┐
                              │                                │            │
                              │  Base of Q4 ← R4 (130 Ω)       │            ├──► Y (output)
                              │                                │            │
                              │  Q4 (NPN, common emitter)       │            │
                              │  Collector → R5 (3 kΩ) → Vcc   │            │
                              │  Emitter ── GND                 │            │
                              │                                │            │
                              └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 4.4 ASCII Equivalent — Equivalent-Circuit for Each Case

**Case 1 (Any input LOW):**

```
   Vcc ---R1--- Base Q1 ---BE (forward)--- GND via LOW input
                        \
                         \ (Q1 in saturation; Q2 base ≈ 0.9 V → Q2 OFF)
                          \
   Vcc ---R2--- Collector Q2 (floating HIGH)
                    |
                    +--- R3 --- D3 --- Base Q3 (ON, emitter-follower)
                                              |
                                              Emitter Q3 --- Y = HIGH
                    |
                    +--- R4 --- Base Q4 (no drive → Q4 OFF)
```

**Case 2 (Both inputs HIGH):**

```
   Vcc ---R1--- Base Q1 (no current path to emitters)
                    |
                    +--- Base-Collector of Q1 (forward biased) --- drives Q2 base (≈ 2.4 V)
                                                          ↓
                                                       Q2 (SAT)
                                                  /         \
                              Collector Q2 ≈ 0.2 V          Emitter Q2 ≈ GND
                                   |                            
                                   +--- R3 --- D3 --- Base Q3 (≈ 0.9 V < 2.1 V) → Q3 OFF
                                   |
                                   +--- R4 --- Base Q4 (≈ 0.2 V + VBE drive) → Q4 SAT
                                                                                      |
                                                                                  Y = LOW (≈ 0.2 V)
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** *With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a basic 2-input TTL NAND gate. Identify the function of the multi-emitter transistor $Q_1$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A basic 2-input TTL NAND gate consists of three stages: **(i)** input stage with **multi-emitter NPN transistor $Q_1$** and base resistor $R_1$, **(ii)** a **phase-splitter transistor $Q_2$**, and **(iii)** a **totem-pole output stage** comprising $Q_3$ (upper, emitter-follower) and $Q_4$ (lower, common-emitter), with level-shift diode $D_3$.

**Function of $Q_1$:** $Q_1$ performs the **AND operation** at the input. When *any* input is LOW, the corresponding B–E junction of $Q_1$ is forward-biased → $Q_1$ saturates → the base of $Q_2$ is held at ≈ 0.9 V (insufficient to turn $Q_2$ on) → output goes HIGH. Only when *both* inputs are HIGH does $Q_1$ operate in **inverse-active mode** allowing current to drive $Q_2$ into saturation, pulling the output LOW. Thus $Q_1$ implements the AND function, and the totem-pole stage adds the inversion, giving the overall NAND operation $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** *Why is the diode $D_3$ used between the collector of $Q_2$ and the base of $Q_3$ in a TTL NAND gate? What happens if it is removed?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The diode $D_3$ introduces an additional **0.7 V level shift** in the base-drive path to $Q_3$. Its purpose is to **raise the turn-on threshold of the upper totem-pole transistor $Q_3$** so that $Q_3$ remains OFF when $Q_2$ is in saturation (output LOW). The threshold becomes:

$$
V_{B2(th)} = V_{BE2} + V_{D3} + V_{BE3} = 0.7 + 0.7 + 0.7 = 2.1\ \text{V}
$$

**If $D_3$ is removed**, the threshold drops to $V_{BE2} + V_{BE3} = 1.4\ \text{V}$. With $Q_2$ in saturation, its collector voltage (≈ 0.2 V) would still be insufficient to turn on $Q_3$ (so the gate *might* still work, but with degraded noise margin), and the LOW output level would **rise by ~0.7 V** — violating TTL specifications ($V_{OL} > 0.4\ \text{V}$). Hence $D_3$ is essential for proper logic-level compatibility and noise immunity.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / ESE Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **basic 2-input TTL NAND gate** and explain the operation in detail for all four input combinations, with reference to the conduction states of transistors $Q_1$, $Q_2$, $Q_3$, and $Q_4$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a standard 74xx TTL NAND gate operating at $V_{CC} = 5\ \text{V}$, compute the **noise margins** $NM_H$ and $NM_L$, given $V_{OH(min)} = 2.4\ \text{V}$, $V_{OL(max)} = 0.4\ \text{V}$, $V_{IH(min)} = 2.0\ \text{V}$, and $V_{IL(max)} = 0.8\ \text{V}$. If the fan-out is 10 and average supply current per gate is 2 mA, calculate the **speed–power product**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

Circuit: Draw $Q_1$ as a multi-emitter NPN with two emitters, base tied to $V_{CC}$ via $R_1$, and collector going to the base of $Q_2$. $Q_2$'s collector connects via $R_2$ to $V_{CC}$ and (through $R_3$ and $D_3$) to $Q_3$'s base. $Q_2$'s collector also feeds $Q_4$'s base via $R_4$. $Q_3$ is an emitter-follower (collector to $V_{CC}$); $Q_4$ is a common-emitter (emitter to ground). Outputs of $Q_3$ and $Q_4$ are tied at node $Y$.

**Operation table:**

| $A$ | $B$ | $Q_1$ | $Q_2$ | $Q_3$ | $Q_4$ | $Y$ |
|-----|-----|-------|-------|-------|-------|-----|
| 0 | 0 | Sat | OFF | ON | OFF | 1 |
| 0 | 1 | Sat | OFF | ON | OFF | 1 |
| 1 | 0 | Sat | OFF | ON | OFF | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | Inv. Active | Sat | OFF | Sat | 0 |

(Detailed explanation as in **§2.2** above — 7 marks distributed: 2 for diagram, 3 for case analysis, 2 for truth-table summary.)

**(b) [7 marks]**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NM_H &= V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)} = 2.4 - 2.0 = 0.4\ \text{V} \\
\\
NM_L &= V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)} = 0.8 - 0.4 = 0.4\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Power dissipation:**

$$
P_D = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC(avg)} = 5\ \text{V} \times 2\ \text{mA} = 10\ \text{mW}
$$

**Speed–Power Product (assuming $t_{pd} = 10\ \text{ns}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPP &= t_{pd} \cdot P_D \\
&= 10 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s} \times 10 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W} \\
&= 100\ \text{pJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Fan-out check** (given $N = 10$, $I_{OH}$ and $I_{IH}$ are within TTL spec).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the role of the **multi-emitter transistor $Q_1$** in a TTL NAND gate. Why is it preferred over a diode AND gate (as in DTL)? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **totem-pole output** of a TTL gate with an **open-collector output**. What is the function of the diode $D_3$? List any two advantages of totem-pole TTL output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

The **multi-emitter transistor $Q_1$** combines the input AND function with the level-shifting that drives $Q_2$. It is a single NPN transistor with *two (or more)* emitter regions diffused into a common base region.

**Why preferred over a diode AND gate:**
