# 3. Step-by-Step Worked Example — Designing a "Digital PPG Heart-Rate & SpO₂ Monitor" Project

This end-to-end worked example walks you through the entire project lifecycle, exactly as the KTU 2024 panel expects you to document yours.

---

## 3.1 Project Title

**"Wearable IoT Pulse Oximeter with OLED Display and Cloud Dashboard"**

---

## 3.2 Stage 1 — Literature Review (3 sample references, expanded)

**Ref. [1]** — *J. Allen, "Photoplethysmography and its application in clinical physiological measurement," Physiol. Meas., vol. 28, no. 3, pp. R1–R39, 2007.*
**Key Finding:** PPG waveform has a DC component (blood volume) and an AC component (pulsatile flow); SpO₂ is computed from the **ratio of ratios** $R$ of red-to-IR absorbance at systolic vs. diastolic peaks.

**Ref. [2]** — *Maxim Integrated, "MAX30102 High-Sensitivity Pulse Oximeter and Heart-Rate Sensor for Wearable Health," Datasheet Rev. 1, 2018.*
**Key Finding:** The MAX30102 integrates red (660 nm) + IR (880 nm) LEDs, a photodiode, and a **18-bit ADC** with 100 SPS default; communicates via I²C (address **0x57**).

**Ref. [3]** — *WHO, "Cardiovascular diseases (CVDs) — Key facts," 2021.* *"17.9 million deaths per year; 80 % of premature CVDs are preventable with continuous monitoring."*
**Clinical Motivation:** A low-cost, wearable, continuously-cloud-connected pulse oximeter addresses the WHO's call for *preventive* cardiovascular care.

> [!NOTE]
> The **PICOT** for this project:
> - **P**: Adult out-patients (25–65 yr)
> - **I**: Wrist-worn PPG oximeter (ESP32 + MAX30102)
> - **C**: Commercial fingertip oximeter (Contec CMS50DL)
> - **O**: SpO₂ accuracy ± 2 %; HR accuracy ± 2 bpm
> - **T**: 12-week semester

---

## 3.3 Stage 2 — Component Selection (Decision-Matrix Outcome)

| Block | Chosen Part | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor | MAX30102 | Integrated red+IR, 18-bit, I²C |
| MCU | ESP32-WROOM-32 | Built-in Wi-Fi + BLE, 12-bit SAR ADC, 240 MHz |
| Display | 0.96″ SSD1306 OLED | 128×64 px, I²C, low-power (20 mA) |
| Battery | 3.7 V 1000 mAh Li-Po | 8-h continuous streaming target |
| Buck Regulator | MCP1700-3.3V | Low Iq = 1.6 µA, SOT-23 |
| Enclosure | 3D-printed PLA | Custom wrist-strap form factor |

**Total BOM Cost ≈ ₹1,950**, well within the typical ₹5,000 KTU cap.

---

## 3.4 Stage 3 — Analysis (Quantitative Design)

### 3.4.1 Anti-Aliasing Filter

PPG signal bandwidth ≈ 0.5 – 5 Hz. Set the low-pass cut-off to **$f_c = 15$ Hz** (over-spec to remove 50 Hz mains noise).

Choose $C = 100 \text{ nF}$:

$$
R = \frac{1}{2 \pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi (15)(100 \times 10^{-9})}
$$

Step-by-step arithmetic:

$$
R = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.14159 \times 15 \times 100 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{9424.78 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{9.42478 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
= 106{,}103 \; \Omega \approx 100 \text{ k}\Omega \;\; \text{(E12 nearest)}
$$

> This gives a slightly shifted cut-off of:
> $$
> f_c^{'} = \frac{1}{2 \pi (100 \text{ k}\Omega)(100 \text{ nF})} \approx 15.92 \text{ Hz}
> $$
> which still attenuates the 50 Hz mains noise by **−30 dB/decade** roll-off.

### 3.4.2 Sampling-Rate Justification

$$
f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ Hz}
$$

We oversample at **$f_s = 100$ Hz** (the MAX30102 default), giving an oversampling factor of **10×** — relaxing the analog filter to a simple first-order RC.

### 3.4.3 SpO₂ Algorithm (Ratio of Ratios)

The classical Beer-Lambert derivation gives:

$$
SpO_2 (\%) = 110 - 25 \cdot R
$$

where the **ratio of ratios** $R$ is:

$$
R = \frac{(AC_{red} / DC_{red})}{(AC_{IR} / DC_{IR})}
$$

The AC and DC components are extracted by **tracking the moving minimum (DC)** and the **moving maximum-minus-minimum (AC)** over a 4-second window. This is a standard KTU-2024 accepted algorithm.

### 3.4.4 Battery-Life Estimation

System current draw (ESP32 Wi-Fi streaming + MAX30102 + OLED):

$$
I_{avg} = I_{ESP32} + I_{sensor} + I_{OLED} = 80 + 1.2 + 20 = 101.2 \text{ mA}
$$

Battery life with **1000 mAh 3.7 V** cell and buck efficiency $\eta = 0.90$:

$$
T_{life} = \frac{1000 \text{ mAh} \times 3.7 \text{ V}}{101.2 \text{ mA} \times 3.3 \text{ V} / 0.90}
$$

$$
= \frac{3700 \text{ mWh}}{371 \text{ mW}} \approx 9.97 \text{ hours}
$$

> **Verdict:** ~10 hours, which is sufficient for a daytime wearable use case. To extend to 24 h, the firmware must **disable Wi-Fi** (saves ~70 mA) and rely on **BLE notifications** (~10 mA), pushing life to > 24 h.

---

## 3.5 Stage 4 — Design (Schematic & State Flow)

### 3.5.1 Pin-Map (ESP32 ↔ Peripherals)

| ESP32 GPIO | Peripheral | Function |
|---|---|---|
| GPIO 21 | SDA (I²C bus) | MAX30102 + OLED data |
| GPIO 22 | SCL (I²C bus) | MAX30102 + OLED clock |
| GPIO 25 | INT (optional) | MAX30102 FIFO threshold |
| GPIO 2 | LED | Heartbeat indicator |
| 3V3 | VCC | Sensor + OLED power |
| GND | GND | Common ground |

I²C bus pull-ups: $R_{pull} = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$ to 3V3 (required for 400 kHz Fast-Mode).

### 3.5.2 Firmware State Machine (Finite-State Design)

States: **INIT → SENSOR_CONFIG → MEASURE → DISPLAY → STREAM → LOW_POWER**

The state machine is implemented as an enum and a switch-case in the Arduino-style firmware:

```cpp
enum State { INIT, SENSOR_CONFIG, MEASURE, DISPLAY, STREAM, LOW_POWER };
State current = INIT;

void loop() {
  switch (current) {
    case INIT:           // I²C scan, OLED splash
    case SENSOR_CONFIG:  // MAX30102 register setup
    case MEASURE:        // Read FIFO buffer, compute HR & SpO₂
    case DISPLAY:        // Update OLED every 1 s
    case STREAM:         // Push to MQTT every 5 s
    case LOW_POWER:      // ESP32 light-sleep, BLE-only
  }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> *This is the only permissible code snippet in this KTU 2024 module write-up — it is a **state machine**, which is a core **sequential-logic / digital-electronics** concept, not a software-engineering digression.*

---

## 3.6 Stage 5 — Development (Build Sequence)

1. **Day 1–2:** Solder header pins on ESP32 DevKit.
2. **Day 3–4:** Wire MAX30102 breakout on breadboard, run I²C scanner → confirm address 0x57.
3. **Day 5–6:** Load the SparkFun MAX30102 library, print raw IR/Red FIFO values to Serial Monitor.
4. **Day 7–9:** Implement moving-min/max for AC/DC extraction.
5. **Day 10–12:** Compute HR via peak-detection (autocorrelation or Pan-Tompkins-lite).
6. **Day 13–14:** Compute SpO₂ via ratio-of-ratios.
7. **Day 15–17:** Add OLED driver (Adafruit_SSD1306), display HR and SpO₂ in large font.
8. **Day 18–21:** Add Wi-Fi/MQTT, push to a free **ThingSpeak** cloud dashboard.
9. **Day 22–25:** Design PCB in EasyEDA, order from JLCPCB.
10. **Day 26–28:** 3D-print enclosure, assemble wearable.
11. **Day 29–30:** Re-test whole system on bench, debug EMI issues.

---

## 3.7 Stage 6 — Testing (Validation)

### 3.7.1 Bench Test (Simulator)

A **Fluke Index 2** SpO₂ simulator was used to generate synthetic PPG signals at known SpO₂ values: 70 %, 80 %, 90 %, 95 %, 98 %, 100 %.

| Reference SpO₂ (%) | Prototype Reading (%) | Error (%) | Pass / Fail |
|---|---|---|---|
| 70 | 72 | +2 | ✅ |
| 80 | 81 | +1 | ✅ |
| 90 | 90 | 0 | ✅ |
| 95 | 95 | 0 | ✅ |
| 98 | 97 | −1 | ✅ |
| 100 | 98 | −2 | ✅ |

> **Root-Mean-Square Error (RMSE):**
> $$
> \text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}
> $$
> Substituting the six error values (2, 1, 0, 0, −1, −2):
> $$
> = \sqrt{\frac{1}{6}(4 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 4)} = \sqrt{1.67} \approx 1.29\%
> $$
> RMSE < 2 % — **meets clinical-grade acceptance**.

### 3.7.2 Volunteer Test (10 healthy adults, 30 s readings, KTU Lab)

| Subject | Prototype HR (bpm) | Contec CMS50DL HR (bpm) | Prototype SpO₂ (%) | Contec SpO₂ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S01 | 74 | 75 | 98 | 98 |
| S02 | 82 | 81 | 97 | 98 |
| S03 | 68 | 69 | 99 | 99 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| S10 | 78 | 78 | 98 | 98 |

Pearson correlation for HR:

$$
r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}} \approx 0.987
$$

> **Verdict:** $r = 0.987 \geq 0.95$ → "Excellent" per KTU rubric.

### 3.7.3 Statistical Test (Bland-Altman)

The mean bias was **−0.4 bpm** with limits of agreement **±2.1 bpm** — clinically negligible. Plotting is required in the final report.

---

## 3.8 Stage 7 — Implementation (Live Deployment)

The prototype was demonstrated live to the KTU panel:

- Worn on the left wrist of the student volunteer.
- Real-time HR & SpO₂ visible on the OLED.
- Live data appeared on a public **ThingSpeak dashboard** within 2 s of acquisition.
- Continuous run for 8 h without battery recharge (initial 100 % → 22 %).

A **5-minute demonstration video** was archived on the project GitHub repo.

---

| Objective (SMART) | Status | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| Achieve SpO₂ accuracy ± 2 % | ✅ Met | RMSE = 1.29 % on simulator |
| Achieve HR accuracy ± 2 bpm | ✅ Met | $r = 0.987$ on volunteers |
| Battery life ≥ 8 h | ✅ Met | Measured 8 h with 22 % residual |
| Cloud dashboard functional | ✅ Met | ThingSpeak public channel |
| Cost ≤ ₹5,000 | ✅ Met | ₹1,950 actual |
| Git repo with ≥ 25 commits | ✅ Met | 38 commits, 4 branches |

> **Self-Grade: 96 / 100** (minor deductions for non-defibrillation-proof sensor and small LCD).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps**
> 1. **No quantitative analysis** — Students who skip the "Analysis" stage lose 5–7 marks immediately. The examiner expects at least **two numerical equations** with substitution.
> 2. **No reference to safety standards** — A biomedical project that does not mention **IEC 60601-1** is marked down by ≥ 3 marks.
> 3. **Copy-paste code** — Submitting Arduino example sketches without modification is plagiarism; marks = 0.
> 4. **No version control** — Absence of a Git repo URL is an automatic 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Single team-member viva** — If only the team leader can answer questions, the others get ≤ 6/10 individual marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*"Define the PICOT framework as used in defining a biomedical engineering project. Illustrate with an example."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
PICOT is an acronym for **P**opulation, **I**ntervention, **C**omparator, **O**utcome, **T**imeframe. It is a structured template, borrowed from evidence-based medicine, used to crystallize a project problem statement.

- **P (Population):** Adult patients with suspected arrhythmia.
- **I (Intervention):** Wrist-worn ECG monitor with deep-learning classifier.
- **C (Comparator):** Standard 12-lead hospital ECG.
- **O (Outcome):** ≥ 95 % sensitivity for ventricular ectopic beats.
- **T (Timeframe):** 12-week semester.

**Marking:** 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for component explanation + 1 mark for example = **3/3**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
*"State the Nyquist sampling theorem. What is the minimum sampling rate required to digitize an EMG signal with maximum frequency component of 500 Hz without aliasing? Justify."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
According to the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem, to reconstruct a band-limited signal without aliasing, the sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest frequency present in the signal:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

For EMG, $f_{max} = 500$ Hz, hence:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \times 500 = 1000 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Justification (1 mark):** KTU recommends oversampling by a factor of 4×–5× to relax anti-aliasing filter design; hence a practical $f_s = 2 \text{ kHz}$ or $4 \text{ kHz}$ is preferred.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Design-Oriented Choice*

**(a)** *Design a wearable digital heart-rate monitor using PPG. List the functional blocks and select one component for each block using a weighted decision matrix.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For the above design, calculate the (i) anti-aliasing filter cut-off and resistor value for $C = 100$ nF, (ii) battery life for a 1000 mAh cell, and (iii) expected quantization SNR if a 12-bit ADC is used.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Functional Blocks (2 marks):**
1. PPG Sensor
2. Analog Front-End (trans-impedance amplifier + filter)
3. ADC
4. Digital Processor (MCU)
5. Display
6. Wireless Link
7. Battery + Power Management

**Weighted Decision Matrix (3 marks):**

| Block | Candidates | Criterion | Weight | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor | MAX30102, AFE4400, custom photodiode | SNR, cost, integration | 0.3 / 0.2 / 0.5 | MAX30102 (score 8.5) |
| MCU | ATmega328P, STM32F407, ESP32 | ADC bits, Wi-Fi, cost | 0.3 / 0.3 / 0.4 | ESP32 (score 8.0) |
| Display | LCD 16×2, OLED 0.96″, TFT 3.5″ | Power, cost, I²C | 0.4 / 0.3 / 0.3 | OLED (score 8.4) |

**Justification (2 marks):** MAX30102 integrates red + IR LEDs, photodiode, and 18-bit ADC in a 5.6 × 3.3 mm OLGA package, eliminating discrete photodiode circuits. ESP32 has built-in Wi-Fi/BLE, enabling IoT cloud streaming without external modules.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Anti-aliasing filter (2 marks):**

Choose $f_c = 15$ Hz (≥ 3× the 5 Hz PPG bandwidth, before 50 Hz mains):

$$
R = \frac{1}{2 \pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi (15)(100 \times 10^{-9})}
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
= \frac{1}{2 \times 3.14159 \times 15 \times 100 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{9.42478 \times 10^{-6}} = 106{,}103 \; \Omega
$$

Closest E12 value: **$R = 100$ kΩ** giving $f_c' = 15.92$ Hz.

**(ii) Battery life (2 marks):**

$I_{avg} = 101.2$ mA, $V_{bat} = 3.7$ V, $Q = 1000$ mAh, $\eta = 0.90$:

$$
T_{life} = \frac{1000 \times 3.7}{101.2 \times 3.3 / 0.90} = \frac{3700}{371.1} \approx 9.97 \text{ hours}
$$

**(iii) Quantization SNR (3 marks):**

$$
\text{SNR}_q = 6.02 N + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 73.0 \text{ dB}
$$

(Note: If the *effective* ENOB of ESP32's internal ADC is 10.5 bits, $\text{SNR}_q \approx 65$ dB — must be stated as an assumption.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Testing / Evaluation-Oriented Choice (Alternative)*

**(a)** *Explain the three tiers of testing (unit, integration, system) for a biomedical digital project, with one example for each.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For a developed PPG oximeter tested on a Fluke Index 2 simulator, the readings were 72, 81, 90, 95, 97, 98 % for reference values 70, 80, 90, 95, 98, 100 % respectively. Compute the (i) Root-Mean-Square Error, (ii) Pearson correlation, and (iii) comment on whether the device meets the KTU clinical-acceptance threshold of ± 2 %.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

1. **Unit Testing (2 marks):** Verifies a *single* functional block in isolation. *Example:* Apply a 1 Hz square wave to the analog front-end input and verify the output at the ADC pin matches the expected amplification ($G = 1 + 49.4\text{k}/R_g$ for AD620).

2. **Integration Testing (2 marks):** Verifies the interaction between two or more blocks. *Example:* Connect MAX30102 → ESP32 → OLED; verify that finger-on-sensor causes HR to display correctly on the OLED within 3 s.

3. **System / Acceptance Testing (3 marks):** End-to-end validation against a clinical-grade reference. *Example:* Run the prototype alongside a Contec CMS50DL on 10 volunteers; compute Bland-Altman bias and Pearson $r$. A correlation $r \geq 0.95$ is the KTU "Excellent" threshold.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Data:
- Reference: $y = [70, 80, 90, 95, 98, 100]$
- Prototype: $\hat{y} = [72, 81, 90, 95, 97, 98]$
- Errors: $e = [+2, +1, 0, 0, -1, -2]$

**(i) RMSE (3 marks):**

$$
\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum e_i^2} = \sqrt{\frac{4+1+0+0+1+4}{6}} = \sqrt{1.667} \approx 1.29\%
$$

**(ii) Pearson $r$ (3 marks):**

$\bar{y} = 88.83, \bar{\hat{y}} = 88.83$. Compute numerator and denominator:

| $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $\hat{y}_i - \bar{\hat{y}}$ | Product | $(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ | $(\hat{y}_i - \bar{\hat{y}})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| −18.83 | −16.83 | 316.91 | 354.57 | 283.25 |
| −8.83 | −7.83 | 69.14 | 77.97 | 61.31 |
| 1.17 | 1.17 | 1.37 | 1.37 | 1.37 |
| 6.17 | 6.17 | 38.07 | 38.07 | 38.07 |
| 9.17 | 8.17 | 74.92 | 84.09 | 66.75 |
| 11.17 | 9.17 | 102.43 | 124.77 | 84.09 |
| **Σ** | | **602.84** | **680.84** | **534.84** |

$$
r = \frac{602.84}{\sqrt{680.84 \times 534.84}} = \frac{602.84}{\sqrt{364{,}151}} = \frac{602.84}{603.45} \approx 0.999
$$

**(iii) Comment (1 mark):** RMSE = 1.29 % ≤ 2 %; $r = 0.999 \geq 0.95$. **Device passes the KTU clinical-acceptance threshold with "Excellent" grade.**

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and revise 1 hour before the viva.**

- [ ] **PICOT** framework crystallizes the problem statement (Population, Intervention, Comparator, Outcome, Timeframe).
- [ ] **PRISMA** workflow standardizes the literature review (≥ 15–20 high-quality references expected).
- [ ] **Weighted Decision Matrix** is the KTU-preferred method for component selection; weights must sum to 1.0.
- [ ] **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; oversample by 4×–5× for practical anti-aliasing.
- [ ] **Quantization SNR:** $6.02 N + 1.76$ dB; remember the constant 1.76 dB (not 1.75).
- [ ] **RC Low-Pass Cut-off:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$; standard E12 values: 1.0, 1.2, 1.5, 1.8, 2.2, 2.7, 3.3, 3.9, 4.7, 5.6, 6.8, 8.2.
- [ ] **Battery Life:** include buck-converter efficiency $\eta$ in the denominator — students often forget this and overestimate by ~15 %.
- [ ] **IEC 60601-1 Patient Safety:** leakage current $\leq 10 \mu A$ DC, $\leq 100 \mu A$ AC under normal conditions.
- [ ] **Three Tiers of Testing:** Unit → Integration → System/Acceptance.
- [ ] **Statistical Validation:** Bland-Altman bias + Pearson $r$ + RMSE — at least one of these must appear in the report.
- [ ] **Pearson $r$ Thresholds:** $r \geq 0.95$ (Excellent), $0.85 \leq r < 0.95$ (Very Good), $0.70 \leq r < 0.85$ (Pass).
- [ ] **SpO₂ Formula:** $SpO_2 = 110 - 25R$ where $R = \dfrac{AC_{red}/DC_{red}}{AC_{IR}/DC_{IR}}$ — must state the calibration assumption.
- [ ] **10-20-30 Rule** for presentations: 10 slides, 20 minutes, ≥ 30-point font.
- [ ] **Git repo with ≥ 25 commits** is the KTU audit threshold for the development stage.
- [ ] **Viva is individual** — every team member must explain every block; specialization caps marks at ~6/10.
- [ ] **Always cite a clinical statistic** (WHO, AIIMS, ICMR) in the problem-statement slide.
- [ ] **3D-print an enclosure** — bare PCBs lose 2–3 marks for "lack of productization".
- [ ] **Photograph the prototype** from ≥ 5 angles; include a 3–5 min demo video link.
- [ ] **I²C address** of MAX30102 is **0x57** — common viva question; remember.
- [ ] **Cut-off for ECG monitoring** is 40 Hz, **diagnostic 12-lead ECG** is 150 Hz — confusion here loses 1 mark easily.
- [ ] **Disclose limitations** explicitly: non-medical-grade, not for clinical use, validation on simulators only.
